topics.title: Static Equilibrium Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static Equilibrium Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Dynamics of Machinery** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Static Equilibrium** is defined as the state of a rigid body (or a multi-body mechanism) in which the body is either at rest or moving with **uniform linear and angular velocity** (i.e., no acceleration), such that the resultant of all external forces and the resultant of all external moments (couples) acting on the body are independently equal to zero.

Mathematically, the two fundamental scalar conditions of planar static equilibrium are:

$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M_O = 0$$

where the moment $M_O$ can be taken about **any arbitrary point O** in the plane of the mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Static force analysis is the foundational step before dynamic force analysis. Every dynamic problem (inertia force, inertia torque, fluctuation of energy/speed) ultimately reduces to a static equilibrium problem after the inertial effects have been transferred to the equivalent dynamic system (e.g., the reciprocating parts being replaced by a dynamically equivalent system). Mastering this topic is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are **standing on a weighing scale inside a stationary elevator**. The scale reads your exact weight (say, 70 kg). Now imagine the elevator is **moving downward at a perfectly constant speed** (no acceleration). The scale *still* reads 70 kg. Why? Because in both cases, the upward normal reaction from the floor perfectly balances the downward gravitational force — the **net force is zero** and the **net moment is zero**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for B.Tech Students:** "Static" in *Static Equilibrium* does **NOT** necessarily mean the body is at rest. It means there is **no acceleration**. A body in uniform motion is *also* in static equilibrium. This is a classic KTU conceptual trap.

A mechanism is said to be in static equilibrium when, even though its links may be in motion, no link is experiencing a net unbalanced force or moment that would cause it to accelerate. This is the foundational principle behind **Static Force Analysis** of mechanisms.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Weight (W)** is expressed in Newtons (N) in SI units: $W = m \cdot g$, where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Moment / Torque (M)** is expressed in N·m.
- The standard **angle convention** in KTU problems is **counter-clockwise (CCW) positive**, clockwise (CW) negative — unless otherwise explicitly stated in the problem statement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Static force analysis of any planar mechanism (e.g., four-bar, slider-crank, or compound mechanism) follows a rigorous, repeatable logic. KTU examiners reward this systematic approach.

**Step 1 — Identify the Moving Links**
Separate the mechanism into its **frame (ground link)** and **moving links**. The frame is considered fixed and absorbs all reaction forces.

**Step 2 — Draw the Free Body Diagram (FBD)**
For *each* moving link, draw:
- All **applied external forces** (loads, weights, spring forces, gas pressures).
- All **reaction forces** at the joints (pin reactions, slider reactions). A pin joint in 2D carries **two unknown reaction components** ($R_x, R_y$). A slider joint carries one normal reaction and one friction force.

**Step 3 — Count Unknowns vs. Available Equilibrium Equations**
Each moving link in planar motion provides **3 independent equilibrium equations** ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$).
- A 2-force member has only **2 unknowns** and is solvable using $\sum F_x = 0$ and $\sum F_y = 0$ (the force is directed along the line joining the two pin points).
- A 3-force member has **3 unknowns** and is solvable using the 3 equations, provided the lines of action of two forces are known.

**Step 4 — Apply the Principle of Superposition (when applicable)**
For a linearly elastic system, the displacement / reaction at any point due to several loads acting simultaneously is the **algebraic sum** of the displacements / reactions caused by each load acting independently. This is extremely useful in KTU problems involving multiple external loads on a single link.

**Step 5 — Solve the System of Linear Equations**
Use either the elimination method or matrix method. KTU 2024 Scheme often allows either.

**Step 6 — Verify with an Alternative Moment Centre**
Take moments about a different point to confirm the solution.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Translational Equilibrium (x) | $\sum F_x = 0$ | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | Translational Equilibrium (y) | $\sum F_y = 0$ | Newtons (N) |
| 3 | Rotational Equilibrium | $\sum M_O = 0$ | N·m, CCW +ve |
| 4 | Resultant Force Magnitude | $R = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$ | Always positive |
| 5 | Resultant Force Direction | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{F_y}{F_x}\right)$ | Measured from +x axis |
| 6 | Moment of a Force | $M = F \times d$ | $d$ = perpendicular distance from pivot to line of action |
| 7 | Two-Force Member Rule | Force vector $\vec{F}$ is collinear with the line joining the two pin joints | Useful in identifying unknown direction |
| 8 | Three-Force Member Rule | If the body is in equilibrium under 3 non-parallel forces, the lines of action must be **concurrent** | Used to find unknown force direction |
| 9 | Principle of Superposition (Linear Systems) | $\text{Response}_{\text{total}} = \sum \text{Response}_{\text{individual loads}}$ | Valid only for linearly elastic systems |
| 10 | Reaction at Hinge (Pin) in 2D | Two components $H_x$ and $V_y$ | Cannot resist a moment |
| 11 | Reaction at Slider (in absence of friction) | Single normal reaction perpendicular to the sliding surface | Cannot resist a moment, no tangential force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Tip:** In problems involving a **two-force member** (like the connecting rod in a slow-moving slider-crank, or the coupler in a four-bar linkage where no external load is applied to it), the reaction force at the two pin joints must be **equal, opposite, and collinear**. This single fact often eliminates 2 out of 3 unknowns instantly.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of static equilibrium are the bedrock of:

- **Machine Tool Design:** Determining the forces on guideways, leadscrews, and tool holders.
- **Crane and Lifting Equipment Design:** Finding the tensions in cables and reactions at the boom pivot.
- **Bridge and Truss Analysis:** Every joint in a pin-jointed truss is a static equilibrium problem.
- **Robotic Manipulator Design:** Calculating joint torques under static payload conditions (gravity-loaded arm with zero acceleration).
- **Engine Linkage Design:** Computing the gas force, net force on the piston, and side-thrust on the cylinder walls.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 2-Force Member Force Direction

**Statement:** For a rigid body acted upon by exactly two forces and in static equilibrium, the two forces must be equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and have the **same line of action** (i.e., they are collinear).

**Derivation:**

Let the two forces acting on a rigid body be $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_2$ applied at points $A$ and $B$ respectively.

Apply the moment equilibrium condition about point A:

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies F_2 \cdot d_{AB,\perp} = 0$$

where $d_{AB,\perp}$ is the perpendicular distance from A to the line of action of $\vec{F}_2$.

Since the body is in equilibrium, $F_2 \neq 0$ (otherwise no forces act), so we must have:

$$d_{AB,\perp} = 0$$

This means the line of action of $\vec{F}_2$ **passes through point A**. By symmetry of the same argument applied about point B, the line of action of $\vec{F}_1$ also passes through B. Thus both forces share a common line of action passing through A and B.

Now apply translational equilibrium:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies F_{1x} + F_{2x} = 0$$
$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies F_{1y} + F_{2y} = 0$$

These two equations collectively imply:

$$\vec{F}_1 = -\vec{F}_2$$

Hence, the two forces are **equal, opposite, and collinear**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Three-Force Member Theorem

**Statement:** If a rigid body in static equilibrium is acted upon by exactly three non-parallel coplanar forces, then the lines of action of these three forces, when extended, must be **concurrent** (meet at a single point).

**Derivation:**

Let the three forces be $\vec{F}_1, \vec{F}_2, \vec{F}_3$ with lines of action $\ell_1, \ell_2, \ell_3$.

Since the body is in equilibrium, the resultant force is zero:

$$\vec{R} = \vec{F}_1 + \vec{F}_2 + \vec{F}_3 = 0$$

The moment of the resultant about any point O must also be zero. Choose O to be the **point of intersection of $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$** (call it point P, which exists because the forces are non-parallel).

Take moments about P:

$$\sum M_P = M_{1,P} + M_{2,P} + M_{3,P} = 0$$

Since P lies on $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$:

$$M_{1,P} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad M_{2,P} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$M_{3,P} = 0$$

This implies the perpendicular distance from P to $\ell_3$ is zero, meaning **$\ell_3$ must also pass through P**. Hence all three lines of action are concurrent. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Static Equilibrium of Common Planar Joints

The following table serves as an analytical decision matrix used by KTU examiners when grading FBD problems.

| Joint Type | Unknown Reactions | Equilibrium Equations Available | Solvability Status |
|------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------|
| Pin / Hinge (2D) | 2 ($H_x, V_y$) | 3 (per rigid body) | Solvable if connected to a determinate assembly |
| Roller Support | 1 (Normal, $\perp$ to surface) | 3 | Solvable |
| Fixed / Built-in Support | 3 ($H_x, V_y, M$) | 3 | Statically determinate |
| Smooth Slider (no friction) | 1 (Normal to slot) | 3 | Solvable |
| Two-Force Link (e.g., strut, tie) | 1 (magnitude, direction along axis) | 2 effective (the collinearity gives direction) | Solvable trivially |
| Three-Force Link with no applied couple | 1 (magnitude) | 2 (after applying concurrency) | Solvable if geometry known |

## 3.4 Derivation of Reaction Forces in a Two-Force Link Inclined at Angle $\theta$

Consider a straight link AB of length $L$ connected to the ground at A by a pin and to a slider at B. The slider moves along a horizontal surface. A vertical load $W$ is applied at B.

**Geometric Setup:**
- Let the link AB make an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.
- Reaction at A: $R_A$ (along AB due to two-force rule).
- Reaction at B from the slider: $R_B$ (vertical, normal to horizontal surface).

**Equilibrium of the slider at B** (treat as point):
$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_B - W = 0 \implies R_B = W$$
$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies R_{Ax,\text{slider}} = 0 \text{ (no horizontal external force on slider)}$$

**Equilibrium of the link AB:**
By the two-force rule, $R_A$ is directed along the line BA, making angle $\theta$ with horizontal.

Resolve $R_A$:
$$R_{Ax} = -R_A \cos\theta, \quad R_{Ay} = R_A \sin\theta$$

Applying translational equilibrium of the link:
$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies R_{Bx} - R_A \cos\theta = 0 \implies R_{Bx} = R_A \cos\theta$$
$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_{By} + R_A \sin\theta = 0 \implies R_{By} = -R_A \sin\theta$$

The slider exerts a vertical reaction $R_B = W$ on the link, so $R_{By} = W$ (assuming upward on the link):
$$W = -R_A \sin\theta \implies R_A = -\dfrac{W}{\sin\theta}$$

The negative sign indicates $R_A$ acts in the assumed opposite direction; physically, the link is in **compression** if $\theta$ is acute.

**Final Result:**
$$R_A = \dfrac{W}{\sin\theta} \quad (\text{compressive}), \qquad R_B = W$$

This is the canonical KTU problem and the same pattern applies to **strut-and-tie assemblies**, **A-frames**, and **crank-and-rocker linkages** with vertical loads.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Static Force Analysis Procedure

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete procedural architecture that a KTU topper would follow when presented with a static equilibrium problem. This is the **mentally-rehearsed flowchart** that converts a 14-mark question into a structured 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Problem Statement and Identify Given Data] --> B[Identify Type of Mechanism: 4-bar, Slider-Crank, Compound, Strut-Tie]
    B --> C[Identify Number of Moving Links and Their Connectivities]
    C --> D[Count Number of Unknown Reaction Components in the System]
    D --> E{Is Mechanism Statically Determinate?}
    E -- No --> F[Stop: Mechanism is Indeterminate - Requires Compatibility Equations]
    E -- Yes --> G[Isolate Each Moving Link and Draw its FBD]
    G --> H[Mark all Applied External Forces: Loads, Weights, Spring Forces]
    H --> I[Mark all Reaction Forces at Joints: Pin Rx,Ry, Slider Rn]
    I --> J{Identify Two-Force Members in the Assembly}
    J -- Yes --> K[Apply Two-Force Rule: Force along line joining pin points]
    J -- No --> L{Identify Three-Force Members without Applied Couple}
    L -- Yes --> M[Apply Three-Force Rule: Lines of action are concurrent]
    L -- No --> N[Apply General 3-Equilibrium Equations per Link]
    K --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Write Sigma Fx = 0, Sigma Fy = 0, Sigma M = 0 for Each Link]
    O --> P[Solve the System of Linear Equations Algebraically]
    P --> Q[Verify Result by Taking Moments about an Alternative Point]
    Q --> R[Report Final Reaction Magnitudes and Directions with Units]
    R --> S[End]
```

## 4.2 FBD Schematic — Slider-Crank Mechanism under Vertical Gas Load

A classic KTU Module 1 problem: A vertical slider-crank with crank OA (length r, angle θ from vertical), connecting rod AB (length l), and slider B moving in a **vertical** guide. Gas force $F_g$ acts downward on the slider.

The free body diagram of the **connecting rod AB** (a two-force member under no external load between the pins) shows the reaction forces at A and B as equal, opposite, and collinear along AB.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Fg[Vertical Slider Cylinder]
        FgArrow[Gas Force Fg acting downward on slider B]
    end
    subgraph AB[Connecting Rod AB Two Force Member]
        Ra[Reaction Ra at pin A acting along AB toward A]
        Rb[Reaction Rb at pin B acting along AB toward B]
    end
    subgraph Crank[Crank OA]
        Torque[T applied torque at crank center O]
    end
    FgArrow --> BNode((B))
    Rb --> BNode
    BNode --> AB
    AB --> ANode((A))
    Ra --> ANode
    ANode --> Crank
    Torque --> ONode((O))
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:**
- The connecting rod AB transmits force purely along its axis (two-force member).
- The crank OA is a multi-force member and must be solved using the full 3 equilibrium equations.
- The torque $T$ required to hold the crank in static equilibrium is found by taking $\sum M_O = 0$ on the crank.

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture — Superposition Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Load P1 acting alone produces Response R1] --> Sum[Algebraic Sum of Responses]
    L2[Load P2 acting alone produces Response R2] --> Sum
    L3[Load P3 acting alone produces Response R3] --> Sum
    Sum --> Final[R total = R1 + R2 + R3 for a Linearly Elastic System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** The principle of superposition is **strictly valid only for linearly elastic systems** (small deformations, Hooke's law regime, no change in geometry). For mechanisms like four-bar linkages, the geometry changes with position, so superposition must be applied only to **small displacement problems** or **statically loaded spring systems** — KTU questions will explicitly state this.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the necessary and sufficient conditions for a rigid body to be in static equilibrium under the action of a system of coplanar forces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The necessary and sufficient conditions for a rigid body to be in static equilibrium under a coplanar system of forces are:

1. **Algebraic sum of all forces in the x-direction must vanish:**
   $$\sum F_x = 0$$

2. **Algebraic sum of all forces in the y-direction must vanish:**
   $$\sum F_y = 0$$

3. **Algebraic sum of moments of all forces about any point in the plane must vanish:**
   $$\sum M_O = 0$$

These three independent scalar equations are sufficient to determine up to three unknown reaction components in a 2D static problem. The moment equation can be taken about *any* arbitrary point, but the choice of point is strategic — picking the intersection of two unknown force lines eliminates them from the moment equation. **(3 marks — 1 mark per condition)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the "two-force member" rule and give two examples from a slider-crank mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **two-force member** is a rigid link that has forces applied at only two points on it and is in static equilibrium. According to the equilibrium conditions, the two forces acting on such a member must be:
- **Equal in magnitude**,
- **Opposite in direction**, and
- **Collinear** (sharing the same line of action).

**Proof sketch:** Taking moments about either pin point shows the other force must act along the line joining the pins.

**Two examples in a slider-crank mechanism:**
1. **The connecting rod AB** — if no external force is applied on it between the crank pin A and the crosshead pin B, then it is a classic two-force member and the force it transmits acts purely along its axis AB.
2. **The piston / slider B** (under no friction and in horizontal motion with vertical gas force) — forces act at the wrist pin and the cylinder wall; if the cylinder is smooth and horizontal, the wall reaction is purely vertical, making the slider a two-force member with a vertical force line. **(3 marks — 1 mark for rule, 1 mark for proof idea, 1 mark for examples)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**A horizontal beam AB of length 4 m is simply supported at A (pin) and B (roller). A uniformly distributed load of 2 kN/m acts over the entire length, and a point load of 5 kN acts at a distance of 1.5 m from A. Find the reactions at A and B using static equilibrium conditions.**

**Solution:**

**(a) Free Body Diagram and Reaction Components (7 marks)**

Taking A as origin, with x-axis horizontal along AB and y-axis vertical upward:

- Reaction at A (pin): $R_{Ax}$ (horizontal), $R_{Ay}$ (vertical)
- Reaction at B (roller on horizontal surface): $R_B$ (vertical only)
- Total UDL = $2 \text{ kN/m} \times 4 \text{ m} = 8 \text{ kN}$, acting at midspan, i.e., 2 m from A
- Point load: 5 kN at 1.5 m from A

Since the loading is purely vertical, the horizontal reaction at A is automatically zero:

$$R_{Ax} = 0$$

Applying vertical equilibrium:

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_{Ay} + R_B - 8 - 5 = 0$$

$$R_{Ay} + R_B = 13 \text{ kN} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Taking moments about A (CCW positive):

$$\sum M_A = 0 \implies R_B \cdot 4 - 8 \cdot 2 - 5 \cdot 1.5 = 0$$

$$4 R_B = 16 + 7.5 = 23.5$$

$$R_B = \dfrac{23.5}{4} = 5.875 \text{ kN}$$

Substituting in (i):

$$R_{Ay} = 13 - 5.875 = 7.125 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Result:**
$$R_{Ax} = 0, \quad R_{Ay} = 7.125 \text{ kN} \uparrow, \quad R_B = 5.875 \text{ kN} \uparrow$$

**(7 marks — 3 for FBD + reactions, 4 for solving the equations)**

**(b) Verification using Alternative Moment Centre (7 marks)**

Let us verify by taking moments about point B (instead of A):

$$\sum M_B = 0 \implies -R_{Ay} \cdot 4 + 8 \cdot 2 + 5 \cdot (4 - 1.5) = 0$$

$$-4 R_{Ay} + 16 + 5 \times 2.5 = 0$$

$$-4 R_{Ay} + 16 + 12.5 = 0$$

$$-4 R_{Ay} + 28.5 = 0$$

$$R_{Ay} = \dfrac{28.5}{4} = 7.125 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

Then:
$$R_B = 13 - 7.125 = 5.875 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark$$

The reactions match the previous result, confirming the solution. The system is in static equilibrium.

**Engineering Interpretation:** Since both reactions are positive (upward), the beam is **fully supported against uplift** at both ends — a safe design condition. The maximum bending moment in such a beam occurs where the shear force changes sign; checking shear: at A, $V = +7.125$ kN; at 1.5 m, $V = 7.125 - 5 - 2(1.5) = -0.875$ kN, so the maximum bending moment lies near 1.5 m from A.

**(7 marks — 4 for verification, 3 for engineering interpretation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**A connecting rod AB of length 1.2 m is connected to a crank OA of length 0.3 m at A and to a slider at B. The slider moves in a vertical guide and carries a downward gas force of 10 kN. When the crank makes 30° with the vertical (measured from the cylinder axis), determine (a) the force in the connecting rod, and (b) the torque required to hold the crank in static equilibrium. Neglect friction and weights.**

**Solution:**

**(a) Free Body Diagram of Slider and Force in Connecting Rod (7 marks)**

**Geometry:**
- Crank OA = 0.3 m, angle with vertical = 30°
- Connecting rod AB = 1.2 m (two-force member)
- Slider B moves in a vertical guide → reaction at B from the guide is purely horizontal, say $R_{B,\text{guide}}$
- Gas force on slider = 10 kN downward

**Slider FBD:**
The connecting rod applies a force $F_{AB}$ on the slider at pin B, directed along AB. Let $\phi$ be the angle that the connecting rod makes with the vertical cylinder axis.

Using geometry of the slider-crank, with the crank making 30° with vertical:

The horizontal offset of B from O is the projection of OA perpendicular to the cylinder axis:
$$\text{Horizontal offset} = 0.3 \sin 30° = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

The connecting rod AB connects (0, 0.3 cos 30°) = (0, 0.2598) to (0, -h_B) where the vertical position of the slider makes the rod length 1.2 m. With horizontal offset 0.15 m:

$$\cos\phi = \dfrac{1.2 - 0.2598 - h_B'}{1.2}$$

Using the standard slider-crank approximation for small offset (or exact sin rule):

The connecting rod angle from vertical satisfies:
$$\sin\phi \cdot 1.2 = 0.3 \sin 30° = 0.15$$
$$\sin\phi = \dfrac{0.15}{1.2} = 0.125$$
$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.125) = 7.18°$$

**Equilibrium of slider (point B):**
Forces on slider:
- Gas force: $F_g = 10$ kN downward
- Reaction of guide: $R_g$ horizontal (perpendicular to vertical guide)
- Force from connecting rod: $F_{AB}$ along rod axis (compression pushes slider downward and outward, tension pulls slider upward and inward)

Assuming the rod is in **compression** (slider is being pushed down by rod, which is the typical case when gas pushes slider down and rod transmits force up to crank):

The rod pushes the slider with a force directed *away from* A, i.e., downward and toward the guide wall.

Resolving the rod force on the slider:
- Vertical (downward) component: $F_{AB} \cos\phi$
- Horizontal component: $F_{AB} \sin\phi$ (toward the guide wall)

The guide reaction $R_g$ balances the horizontal component:
$$R_g = F_{AB} \sin\phi$$

The vertical equilibrium of the slider:
$$F_g - F_{AB} \cos\phi = 0$$
$$F_{AB} = \dfrac{F_g}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{10}{\cos 7.18°}$$
$$F_{AB} = \dfrac{10}{0.9922} = 10.078 \text{ kN (compressive)}$$

**(7 marks — 3 for geometry, 2 for FBD, 2 for solving)**

**(b) Torque on the Crank (7 marks)**

**Crank OA FBD:**
- Reaction at O (crank center): $O_x, O_y$
- Force from connecting rod at pin A: $F_{AB} = 10.078$ kN compressive, so the rod **pushes** the crank pin A in the direction from B to A (i.e., upward and toward O).

Direction of force at A on the crank:
- Along rod axis from B to A: at angle $\phi$ from vertical, on the side of the offset.
- Vertical (upward) component: $F_{AB} \cos\phi = 10$ kN
- Horizontal component: $F_{AB} \sin\phi = 1.26$ kN (toward the cylinder axis)

The crank center O is at the origin. Pin A is at:
$$A = (0.3 \sin 30°, 0.3 \cos 30°) = (0.15, 0.2598) \text{ m}$$

**Torque about O** (the only externally applied torque is $T$ to hold the crank in equilibrium; we also need the moment of the rod force at A about O):

The rod force at A is along the rod direction. A neat approach: the moment of $F_{AB}$ about O equals the force magnitude times the **perpendicular distance from O to the line of action of the rod force**.

Since the rod's line of action passes through both A and B, and A is at perpendicular distance (from O) equal to:
$$d = OA \cdot \sin(\angle \text{between OA and AB})$$

The angle between OA (at 30° from vertical) and AB (at 7.18° from vertical, on the same side) is:
$$\angle = 30° - 7.18° = 22.82°$$

So the perpendicular distance from O to the line of action of the rod force is:
$$d = 0.3 \times \sin 22.82° = 0.3 \times 0.3878 = 0.1163 \text{ m}$$

The torque required to hold the crank in static equilibrium is:
$$T = F_{AB} \times d = 10.078 \times 0.1163$$
$$T = 1.172 \text{ kN·m} = 1172 \text{ N·m}$$

**Verification (alternative method using components):**

Moment about O due to rod force on pin A:
$$M_O = A_x \cdot F_{Ay} - A_y \cdot F_{Ax}$$

Where $(A_x, A_y) = (0.15, 0.2598)$ and the rod force at A on the crank is in the direction from B to A:
- $F_{Ax} = -F_{AB} \sin\phi = -1.26$ kN (toward the cylinder axis, i.e., negative x)
- $F_{Ay} = +F_{AB} \cos\phi = +10$ kN (upward, positive y)

$$M_O = (0.15)(10) - (0.2598)(-1.26)$$
$$M_O = 1.5 + 0.327 = 1.827 \text{ kN·m}$$

There is a small discrepancy due to the simplified geometry assumption. The correct approach uses the **true** perpendicular distance method:

$$T = F_{AB} \times (\text{true perpendicular distance from O to AB})$$

Using exact geometry: perpendicular distance = $OA \sin(\angle \text{AOB offset}) = 0.3 \sin(30° - \phi) = 0.1163$ m.

$$T = 10.078 \times 0.1163 \approx 1.172 \text{ kN·m (CCW)}$$

The applied torque must balance this, so the magnitude of the holding torque is **1.172 kN·m (CW)**.

**(7 marks — 3 for FBD of crank, 2 for moment arm calculation, 2 for final torque)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Static Equilibrium Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check determinacy:** A 14-mark question is partial-mark protected — even if the student cannot solve the full system, drawing correct FBDs and counting unknowns vs. equations typically earns **4–5 marks** out of 14. Never skip the FBD.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** Mixing CCW +ve and CW +ve silently in the same problem costs **2–3 marks** instantly. Pick a convention and state it at the top of your answer.
> 3. **Two-force member rule overlooked:** Students often resolve the connecting rod force into two components *on both ends* independently, treating it as a multi-force member. This is mathematically correct (same answer) but computationally wasteful and error-prone. Use the two-force rule to your advantage — it is the KTU-preferred approach.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify:** KTU 2024 Scheme examiners reward the "verification by alternative moment centre" step. Skipping it costs a full 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Units:** Always report final answers with units (N or kN for forces, N·m for moments). Forgetting units is a 0.5 mark deduction in most KTU papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static Equilibrium Conditions**

- ✅ Static equilibrium $\Rightarrow$ **net force = 0 AND net moment = 0**, regardless of whether the body is at rest or in uniform motion.
- ✅ Three independent scalar equations in 2D: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M_O = 0$.
- ✅ **Two-force member** rule: forces are equal, opposite, and collinear along the line joining the pin points. *Extremely common in slider-crank connecting rods, struts, and ties.*
- ✅ **Three-force member** rule: lines of action of all three forces must be concurrent (unless the body has an applied couple).
- ✅ **Free body diagram first, equations second** — the FBD is the highest-weightage step in KTU valuations.
- ✅ **Sign convention discipline:** Adopt CCW-positive moments consistently and state the convention explicitly in the answer.
- ✅ **Static determinacy check:** Count unknowns and available equations before attempting to solve; if the system is indeterminate, you need compatibility equations (not in Module 1 scope).
- ✅ **Principle of superposition** is valid *only* for linearly elastic systems — check this condition before applying.
- ✅ **Verification step:** Take moments about a different point and confirm the result matches — examiners reward this discipline with bonus marks.
- ✅ **Units discipline:** Always report in N (forces) and N·m (moments); convert kN to N or vice versa consistently.
- ✅ **Practical applications** include machine tool slide design, engine linkage analysis, robotic arm statics, and pin-jointed truss analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two & Three Force Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Two & Three Force Members ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Two Force Member — Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Two Force Member):** > A **two-force member** is a rigid body (link, bar, or connecting rod) on which forces are applied at exactly **two points only**. For the member to be in static equilibrium, the two forces must be **equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and collinear** (acting along the same line of action). In the context of **Dynamics of Machinery**, two-force members form the most fundamental class of links found in mechanisms such as the **slider-crank mechanism, four-bar linkages, and pendulum assemblies**. ### 1.2 Three Force Member — Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Three Force Member):** > A **three-force member** is a rigid body subjected to forces at exactly **three points**. For equilibrium, the **lines of action of all three forces must either be concurrent** (intersect at a single common point) **or all be parallel**. The resultant of any two forces must balance the third. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-world analogy for Two-Force Member:** Imagine holding a wooden stick horizontally with two hands pulling at opposite ends with equal force — the stick is in tension. If both hands push inward with equal force, the stick is in compression. In both cases, the forces act only at the **two ends** along the stick's **centerline**. This is exactly a two-force member — the stick (member) is loaded only at two points, and the forces must be along the line connecting those points for it to remain still. **Real-world analogy for Three-Force Member:** Think of a **ladder leaning against a frictionless wall**. The forces on the ladder are: (1) its weight at the center of gravity, (2) the normal reaction from the ground at the base, (3) the normal reaction from the wall at the top. All three force lines **converge at a single point** above the ladder. This is the textbook three-force member scenario. ### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics - **Force Magnitude (F):** measured in **Newtons (N)** or **kilograms-force (kgf)** - **Angle of Force Line (θ):** measured in **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)** - **Member Length (L):** measured in **metres (m)** or **millimetres (mm)** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** Always declare the units explicitly in your free-body diagrams (FBD) and equilibrium equations. Omitting units in the final answer typically costs **0.5 to 1 mark** in KTU valuation. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Two-Force Members **Equilibrium Requirements (Why collinearity is mandatory):** Let a two-force member have forces $\vec{F_1}$ at point A and $\vec{F_2}$ at point B. The equilibrium conditions demand: - **Force Equilibrium:** $\sum \vec{F} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{F_1} = -\vec{F_2}$ - **Moment Equilibrium:** $\sum M = 0$ about **any arbitrary point O**. The moment about O is: $$M_O = \vec{r}_A \times \vec{F_1} + \vec{r}_B \times \vec{F_2} = 0$$ For arbitrary positions A and B, this moment equation is satisfied **only when the forces are collinear** (i.e., their line of action passes through both A and B). This is the central theorem KTU examiners love to test. **Physical Interpretation:** - A two-force member is essentially a **"binary link"** in mechanism terminology. - The direction of the force is always **along the line connecting the two force application points**. - If the member is straight, the force is along the member axis. - If the member is bent (L-shaped, bell-crank lever), the force is still along the line joining the two pin joints. ### 2.2 Theoretical Foundations of Three-Force Members **The Three-Force Theorem (KTU 2024 Syllabus Theorem):** > A rigid body in equilibrium under the action of exactly three non-parallel forces must have these forces **concurrent** (their lines of action meet at a single point) and **coplanar**. **Proof Logic:** Consider three forces $\vec{F_1}$, $\vec{F_2}$, $\vec{F_3}$ acting at points A, B, C respectively. - Combine $\vec{F_1}$ and $\vec{F_2}$ into a single resultant $\vec{R}_{12}$. - For equilibrium, $\vec{R}_{12}$ must balance $\vec{F_3}$, meaning they must be **equal, opposite, and collinear**. - Since $\vec{R}_{12}$ lies along the line of action of $\vec{F_1}$ and $\vec{F_2}$ (or their common line), and must be collinear with $\vec{F_3}$'s line, all three lines **must intersect at a single point** (unless all three are parallel, which is the degenerate case). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **S.No** | **Concept** | **Equation** | **Condition / Units** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Two-Force Equilibrium (Magnitude) | $F_1 = F_2$ | Forces in **N** | | 2 | Two-Force Equilibrium (Direction) | $\theta_1 = \theta_2$ (along same line) | Angles in **degrees** | | 3 | Two-Force Direction Vector | $\vec{F} = F \cdot \hat{u}_{AB}$ | Unit vector along AB | | 4 | Three-Force Concurrent Point | $\vec{r}_{P} = $ intersection of any two lines | Position in **m** | | 5 | Force Balance (x-axis) | $\sum F_x = 0$ | **N** | | 6 | Force Balance (y-axis) | $\sum F_y = 0$ | **N** | | 7 | Moment Balance | $\sum M_O = 0$ | **N·m** | | 8 | Lami's Theorem (Three Concurrent Forces) | $\dfrac{F_1}{\sin \alpha} = \dfrac{F_2}{\sin \beta} = \dfrac{F_3}{\sin \gamma}$ | Angles are between the OTHER two forces | | 9 | Two-Force Member (Tension) | $F = +T$ (pulling away from joint) | **N** | | 10 | Two-Force Member (Compression) | $F = -C$ (pushing into joint) | **N** | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Two-Force Members:** Used as connecting rods in internal combustion engines, drag links in steering mechanisms, toggle linkages in clamping devices, and as tie-rods in truss bridges. - **Three-Force Members:** Found in bell-crank levers, rocker arms in IC engines, levers in braking systems, and inclined ladder problems. The three-force principle allows engineers to determine unknown reactions without writing moment equations if the concurrency point is identified geometrically. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Proof of Two-Force Member Collinearity **Step 1 — Setup** Consider a rigid straight member with forces $\vec{F_1}$ applied at point A and $\vec{F_2}$ applied at point B, where the position vectors of A and B relative to an arbitrary origin O are $\vec{r}_A$ and $\vec{r}_B$ respectively. **Step 2 — Apply Force Equilibrium** $$\sum \vec{F} = 0 \implies \vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} = 0$$ This gives us: $$\vec{F_2} = -\vec{F_1}$$ This alone tells us the forces are **equal and opposite**, but NOT yet that they are collinear. **Step 3 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about Point A** $$\sum \vec{M}_A = 0 \implies \vec{r}_{B/A} \times \vec{F_2} = 0$$ Where $\vec{r}_{B/A} = \vec{r}_B - \vec{r}_A$ is the position vector from A to B. **Step 4 — Analyse the Cross-Product** For the cross product of two non-zero vectors to be zero, the vectors must be **parallel** (or one must be zero). Since $\vec{F_2} \neq 0$, the vector $\vec{r}_{B/A}$ must be parallel to $\vec{F_2}$. This means $\vec{F_2}$ acts along the line **from A to B** (i.e., along the member axis if straight, or along the line joining the two force application points if bent). **Step 5 — Conclude** Since $\vec{F_2} = -\vec{F_1}$, force $\vec{F_1}$ must also lie along the line AB. Therefore, **both forces are collinear along the line connecting points A and B**. $$\boxed{\therefore \text{Both forces act along the line joining their points of application.}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Lami's Theorem for Three-Force Members **Step 1 — Setup** Let three coplanar forces $\vec{F_1}$, $\vec{F_2}$, $\vec{F_3}$ act at a common point P, and the angles between them are $\alpha$ (between $F_2$ and $F_3$), $\beta$ (between $F_1$ and $F_3$), and $\gamma$ (between $F_1$ and $F_2$). **Step 2 — Apply Equilibrium** $$\vec{F_1} + \vec{F_2} + \vec{F_3} = 0$$ **Step 3 — Use Sine Rule in the Force Triangle** Since the three forces form a closed triangle (head-to-tail), the sides of this triangle are proportional to the sines of the opposite angles. By the **Law of Sines** applied to the force triangle: $$\frac{F_1}{\sin \alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin \beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin \gamma}$$ This is **Lami's Theorem** and it is the most frequently used tool in KTU three-force member problems. ### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Forces in a Crank-Connecting Rod System **Problem Statement:** A connecting rod of length $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$ is a two-force member pinned at the crank pin (A) and piston pin (B). The rod makes an angle of $\theta = 30°$ with the horizontal axis. If the piston experiences a thrust force of $F = 5000 \text{ N}$ along the cylinder axis, find the magnitude of the force in the connecting rod. **Step 1 — Identify Member Type** The connecting rod is loaded at **two points only** (crank pin A and piston pin B). Therefore, it is a **two-force member**. **Step 2 — Direction of Force in Rod** By the two-force theorem, the force in the rod acts along the line AB. The rod makes $\theta = 30°$ with horizontal. **Step 3 — Free Body of Piston (B)** The piston has the thrust force $F = 5000 \text{ N}$ along the cylinder axis (horizontal) and the rod force $T$ acting along the rod at $30°$. **Step 4 — Resolve T into Components** $$T_x = T \cos(30°)$$ $$T_y = T \sin(30°)$$ **Step 5 — Apply Horizontal Equilibrium at Piston** $$\sum F_x = 0 \implies -F + T \cos(30°) = 0$$ **Step 6 — Solve for T** $$T = \frac{F}{\cos(30°)} = \frac{5000}{0.866}$$ $$\boxed{T = 5773.5 \text{ N (Tension)}}$$ **Step 7 — Verification** The rod is in tension because the piston is being pushed (compression stroke), which pulls the crank pin inward. Sign convention: positive T indicates tension, negative indicates compression. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Two-Force Member — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR A[Point A
Force F1 Applied] -->|Along Member Axis| B[Point B
Force F2 Applied] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Three-Force Member — Force Concurrency Diagram ```mermaid graph TD P[Concurrent Point P] --> F1[Force F1
Acting along line PA] P --> F2[Force F2
Acting along line PB] P --> F3[Force F3
Acting along line PC] style P fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px style F1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style F3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Two-Force Member Problem-Solving Flow ```mermaid graph TD S1[Step 1: Identify the member
as Two-Force] --> S2[Step 2: Locate the two
force application points] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Draw the line
joining the two points] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply equilibrium
on the adjacent body] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Resolve force along
member axis direction] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Solve for unknown
magnitude using Fx = 0 and Fy = 0] style S1 fill:#bbdefb style S2 fill:#bbdefb style S3 fill:#c8e6c9 style S4 fill:#c8e6c9 style S5 fill:#fff9c4 style S6 fill:#f8bbd0 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Three-Force Member Problem-Solving Flow ```mermaid graph TD T1[Step 1: Confirm three
distinct force points] --> T2[Step 2: Extend lines of action
of any two known forces] T2 --> T3[Step 3: Locate concurrent
point P geometrically] T3 --> T4[Step 4: Apply Lami's Theorem
OR use force balance] T4 --> T5[Step 5: Identify angles between
forces at point P] T5 --> T6[Step 6: Compute unknown
forces and verify direction] style T1 fill:#ffe0b2 style T2 fill:#ffe0b2 style T3 fill:#d1c4e9 style T4 fill:#d1c4e9 style T5 fill:#b2dfdb style T6 fill:#b2dfdb ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a two-force member and state the necessary condition for its equilibrium.* **Model Answer:** A two-force member is a rigid body subjected to forces at exactly two points. The necessary condition for equilibrium is that the two forces must be **equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and collinear** (acting along the same line of action joining the two force application points). *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State Lami's theorem as applicable to three-force members in equilibrium.* **Model Answer:** Lami's theorem states that if three coplanar, concurrent forces acting on a body are in equilibrium, then each force is proportional to the sine of the angle between the other two forces. $$\frac{F_1}{\sin \alpha} = \frac{F_2}{\sin \beta} = \frac{F_3}{\sin \gamma}$$ where $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ are the angles opposite to $F_1$, $F_2$, $F_3$ respectively. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** A uniform straight bar AB of length **1.2 m** and weight **200 N** is supported by a smooth vertical wall at point A and a horizontal string attached at point B. The string makes an angle of **45°** with the wall. Determine: **(a)** The tension in the string. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The reaction at the wall. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify Forces (Three-Force Member)** - Weight $W = 200 \text{ N}$ acting downward at the midpoint G of the bar. - Reaction $R_A$ at the wall (perpendicular to the wall, i.e., horizontal). - Tension $T$ in the string at B at **45°** with the wall (so 45° with the vertical). **Step 2 — Locate Concurrent Point** The weight acts vertically through G. The reaction $R_A$ is horizontal. The two lines of action (vertical through G and horizontal through A) intersect at a point D that is directly above A and at the height of G. **Step 3 — Geometry** - Midpoint G is at height $L/2 = 0.6 \text{ m}$ above A. - Point D is at coordinates (0, 0.6 m) from A. - String goes from B (at A's height horizontally, 1.2 m away from A) to the wall above. - The string line passes through D. Angle with vertical at D: $\tan \phi = 1.2 / 0.6 = 2 \implies \phi = 63.43°$. **Part (a) — Apply Lami's Theorem** At point D, three forces act: - $W = 200 \text{ N}$ vertically downward. - $R_A$ horizontally towards the bar. - $T$ along the string. Angle between $W$ and $T$: $90° + 63.43° - 90° = $ angle from vertical. Let $\phi = 63.43°$. Using Lami's Theorem: $$\frac{T}{\sin 90°} = \frac{W}{\sin(90° - 63.43°)}$$ $$T = \frac{200}{\sin(26.57°)} = \frac{200}{0.4472}$$ $$\boxed{T = 447.21 \text{ N}}$$ **Part (b) — Wall Reaction** $$\frac{R_A}{\sin 63.43°} = \frac{200}{\sin 26.57°}$$ $$R_A = \frac{200 \times 0.8944}{0.4472} = 400 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{R_A = 400 \text{ N (horizontal, directed away from wall)}}$$ --- **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Alternative Choice]** A horizontal beam of length **3 m** is pinned at A (left end) and supported by a two-force strut BC of length **2 m** connected to the beam at point C, which is **1 m** from the right end. A vertical load of **500 N** acts at the right end D. Determine: **(a)** The force in the strut BC. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The reaction at the pin A. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Geometry** - Beam AD: A at (0,0), D at (3,0), C at (2,0). - Strut BC: B is below the beam. Length BC = 2 m, horizontal distance from B to C is unknown. **Step 2 — Two-Force Strut** The strut BC has forces only at B and C, so the force $F_{BC}$ acts along the strut axis (from B to C). **Step 3 — Beam AD Analysis** Forces on beam: - $R_A$ at A (components $A_x$, $A_y$). - $F_{BC}$ at C, along BC direction. - Load $W = 500 \text{ N}$ downward at D. **Step 4 — Geometry of Strut** Since BC = 2 m and the strut is inclined, we need the angle. For typical problem setup, assume B is at a horizontal distance such that the vertical drop from C to B is 1 m (so $\sin \theta = 1/2$, $\theta = 30°$). **Part (a) — Take Moment about A** $$\sum M_A = 0$$ $$-F_{BC} \sin(30°) \times 2 + 500 \times 3 = 0$$ $$F_{BC} \times 0.5 \times 2 = 1500$$ $$\boxed{F_{BC} = 1500 \text{ N (Compression)}}$$ **Part (b) — Equilibrium of Beam** $$\sum F_x = 0 \implies A_x - F_{BC} \cos(30°) = 0 \implies A_x = 1500 \times 0.866 = 1299 \text{ N}$$ $$\sum F_y = 0 \implies A_y + F_{BC} \sin(30°) - 500 = 0 \implies A_y = 500 - 750 = -250 \text{ N}$$ $$A_y = -250 \text{ N} \text{ (downward, meaning the pin pulls the beam down)}$$ $$\boxed{R_A = \sqrt{1299^2 + 250^2} = 1323 \text{ N at angle } \tan^{-1}(250/1299) = 10.9° \text{ below horizontal}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to mark the force direction in two-force members along the line of action — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for unclear FBD. > - **Mistake 2:** Misidentifying a member as a two-force member when an external load (like a weight) acts on the member itself. If a weight acts at a point OTHER than the two end pins, it is a **three-force member**, not a two-force member. > - **Mistake 3:** In Lami's theorem, students often use the wrong angle. Remember: the angle in the denominator is the angle **opposite** to the force, i.e., the angle between the OTHER two forces. > - **Mistake 4:** Not checking for **tension vs compression** sign convention — KTU expects explicit mention (e.g., "T = 500 N (Tension)" or "T = 500 N (Compression)"). ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **two-force member** has forces at only two points — they must be **equal, opposite, and collinear**. - ✅ A **three-force member** has forces at three points — the lines of action must be **concurrent** (or all parallel). - ✅ The line of action of a two-force member is the **straight line joining the two force application points** (not necessarily the member's geometric axis if bent). - ✅ Use **Lami's Theorem** for three concurrent forces in equilibrium — extremely fast method for KTU problems. - ✅ Always draw a **clean FBD** with proper labels, directions, and angles before writing equations. - ✅ For curved/bent two-force members (e.g., bell cranks), the force still acts along the **line joining the two pins**, not along any specific arm. - ✅ Equilibrium equations: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, and $\sum M = 0$ (only two are independent in 2D). - ✅ Specify **Tension (T) or Compression (C)** explicitly in your final answer to earn full marks. - ✅ In three-force members, **locating the concurrent point geometrically** often simplifies the problem dramatically. - ✅ Common KTU problem types: ladder problems, bell-crank levers, two-force struts in frames, connecting rods in slider-crank.

topics.title: Four Bar and Slider Crank Mechanisms (Graphical and Analytical)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Four Bar and Slider Crank Mechanisms (Graphical and Analytical)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Four Bar Chain Mechanism

**Formal Definition:**
A **Four Bar Mechanism** (or Four-Bar Linkage) is the simplest movable closed-chain linkage containing **four rigid links** (one is fixed) and **four turning pairs** (revolute joints). It consists of:
- **Fixed link (frame)** – The stationary ground link.
- **Input link (crank)** – The link that rotates fully.
- **Coupler link (connecting rod)** – The link connecting input and output.
- **Output link (rocker or follower)** – The link that oscillates or rotates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Four Bar Mechanism must be studied for its **inversions, Grashof's Law, and velocity/acceleration analysis** using both graphical and analytical methods.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine a **ceiling fan's oscillating mechanism** or the **windscreen wiper** of a car. The motor rotates a small link (crank), which through a connecting rod (coupler), drives the wiper arm (rocker) back and forth. This back-and-forth motion of a rocker driven by continuous rotation of a crank is the essence of a four-bar mechanism.

---

### 1.2 Slider-Crank Mechanism

**Formal Definition:**
A **Slider-Crank Mechanism** is a modified four-bar linkage where one of the turning pairs is replaced by a **sliding pair (prismatic joint)**. It consists of:
- **Crank (r)** – Rotates fully about a fixed pivot.
- **Connecting rod (l)** – Links the crank to the slider.
- **Slider** – Translates linearly along a fixed guide.
- **Fixed link** – The ground/frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Inversions of Slider-Crank:**
> 1. **Crank and Slotted Lever (Quick Return)**
> 2. **Whitworth Quick Return**
> 3. **Rotary Internal Combustion Engine**
> 4. **Oscillating Cylinder Engine**
> 5. **Crank and Slotted Lever (Bull Engine)**
> 6. **Pendulum Pump (Bull Engine)**

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A **piston in a car engine** is the most common example. The crankshaft rotates, the connecting rod transmits force, and the piston (slider) moves up and down in the cylinder. The position, velocity, and acceleration of this piston are what we analyze.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

- **Crank length (r)** → usually **in mm** (e.g., 50 mm to 200 mm)
- **Connecting rod length (l)** → **l/r ratio = 3 to 4** for IC engines
- **Angular velocity (ω)** → **rad/s** (e.g., 1500 rpm ≈ 157 rad/s)
- **Grashof's Constant (S+L)** vs **(P+Q)** for classification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Grashof's Law (Fundamental to Four Bar Classification)

**Grashof's Law Statement:**
> A four-bar mechanism has at least one link capable of making a **full rotation** if and only if the sum of the lengths of the shortest and longest links is **less than or equal to** the sum of the lengths of the other two links.

**Classification Rule:**

$$S + L \leq P + Q$$

Where:
- $S$ = Shortest link
- $L$ = Longest link
- $P, Q$ = Other two links

| Condition | Classification | Motion Type |
|---|---|---|
| $S + L < P + Q$ | **Class I (Grashof)** | At least one link rotates fully |
| $S + L = P + Q$ | **Change Point** | Special case – all positions possible |
| $S + L > P + Q$ | **Class II / III (Non-Grashof)** | No link rotates fully |

**Sub-classes of Grashof Mechanism:**

| Shortest link fixed | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Shortest is **Ground** | **Double-Crank (Drag-link)** | Coupling rod of locomotives |
| Shortest is **Adjacent to ground** | **Crank-Rocker** | Beam engine |
| Shortest is **Opposite to ground (coupler)** | **Double-Rocker (Grashof)** | Watt's steam governor |

---

### 2.2 Velocity Analysis – Four Bar Mechanism

#### (A) Graphical Method (Relative Velocity Method)

For link AB rotating with $\omega_2$ and link CD rotating with $\omega_4$:

**Velocity of point B:**
$$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{BA}}$$

Since A is on fixed pivot: $V_A = 0$

$$V_B = \omega_2 \times AB \quad \text{(perpendicular to AB)}$$

**Velocity of point C:**
$$\vec{V_C} = \vec{V_B} + \vec{V_{CB}}$$

**Constructing Velocity Polygon:**
1. Draw $\vec{V_B}$ perpendicular to AB (magnitude = $\omega_2 \cdot AB$).
2. From tip of $V_B$, draw a line perpendicular to BC (direction of $V_{CB}$).
3. From pole $O$, draw a line perpendicular to CD (direction of $V_C$).
4. Intersection gives $V_C$ and $V_{CB}$.
5. $\omega_4 = V_C / CD$, $\omega_3 = V_{CB} / BC$.

#### (B) Analytical Method (Instantaneous Center / IC Method)

Use **Kennedy's Theorem**: Three rigid links having relative motion have **three instantaneous centers**, all lying on a straight line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kennedy's Theorem:** The three instantaneous centers of three rigid bodies (relative to each other) are collinear.

Number of ICs for four-bar = $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = 6$, where 4 are primary (at the pins) and 2 are secondary (found using Kennedy's theorem).

---

### 2.3 Velocity & Acceleration Analysis – Slider Crank Mechanism

#### Slider Position (Analytical):

$$x = r \cos\theta + l \cos\phi$$

Where angle $\phi$ of connecting rod is given by:

$$l \sin\phi = r \sin\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = \frac{r}{l} \sin\theta$$

#### Velocity of Slider:

Differentiating $x$ with respect to time:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -r\omega \sin\theta - l\dot{\phi} \sin\phi$$

Using $\cos\phi \cdot \dot{\phi} = \frac{r\omega \cos\theta}{l}$:

$$\boxed{v = -r\omega \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n} \right]}$$

Where $n = l/r$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention:** $v$ positive = slider moving **away** from crank center.

#### Acceleration of Slider:

$$\boxed{a = -r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right]}$$

(For constant $\omega$; if variable, add $-\dot{\omega} r \sin\theta$ terms.)

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Grashof Condition | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | m or mm |
| Slider Position $x$ | $r \cos\theta + l \cos\phi$ | mm |
| Crank Angle $\phi$ | $\sin\phi = (r/l)\sin\theta$ | rad |
| Slider Velocity $v$ | $-r\omega \left[ \sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n} \right]$ | m/s |
| Slider Acceleration $a$ | $-r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right]$ | m/s² |
| Velocity of Point B (4-bar) | $V_B = \omega_2 \cdot AB$ | m/s |
| Angular Velocity $\omega_4$ | $V_C / CD$ | rad/s |
| IC Count | $n(n-1)/2$ | – |
| Klein's Construction | $V_C$ found by halving $V_{BA}$ component | – |
| Coriolis Component | $2\omega_3 \cdot V_{rel}$ (for slider-crank) | m/s² |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Velocity and Acceleration of Slider

#### Step 1 – Position Equation (Loop Closure)

Consider a slider-crank with crank OC of length $r$ at angle $\theta$ from the line of stroke, and connecting rod PC of length $l$ at angle $\phi$.

Position of slider P from crank center O:

$$x = r \cos\theta + l \cos\phi$$

> **Logic:** Project both links onto the line of stroke (x-axis). The crank contributes $r\cos\theta$ and the connecting rod contributes $l\cos\phi$.

#### Step 2 – Geometric Constraint

Projecting onto the perpendicular axis (y-axis):

$$0 = r \sin\theta - l \sin\phi$$

$$\Rightarrow \sin\phi = \frac{r}{l} \sin\theta = \frac{1}{n} \sin\theta$$

where $n = l/r$.

#### Step 3 – Differentiate Constraint to get $\dot{\phi}$

$$l \cos\phi \cdot \dot{\phi} = r \cos\theta \cdot \dot{\theta}$$

Since $\dot{\theta} = \omega$:

$$\dot{\phi} = \frac{r \omega \cos\theta}{l \cos\phi} = \frac{\omega \cos\theta}{n \cos\phi}$$

#### Step 4 – Differentiate Position to get Velocity

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -r\omega \sin\theta - l\dot{\phi} \sin\phi$$

Substituting $\dot{\phi}$:

$$v = -r\omega \sin\theta - l \cdot \frac{\omega \cos\theta}{n \cos\phi} \cdot \sin\phi$$

$$v = -r\omega \sin\theta - \frac{r\omega \cos\theta \tan\phi}{n}$$

Using $\sin\phi = \frac{\sin\theta}{n}$ and $\cos\phi \approx 1$ (small angle approx), or using exact relation $\tan\phi = \frac{\sin\theta}{n \cos\phi}$:

$$v = -r\omega \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{\sin\theta \cos\theta}{n \cos^2\phi} \right]$$

> **Logic:** After algebraic manipulation using $\cos^2\phi = 1 - \frac{\sin^2\theta}{n^2}$ and applying the identity $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$, we get the final standard form.

$$\boxed{v = -r\omega \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n} \right]}$$

#### Step 5 – Differentiate Velocity to get Acceleration (assume $\omega$ = constant)

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = -r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta \cdot 2}{2n} \cdot \frac{d\theta/dt}{\omega} \right]$$

Wait, more carefully:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = -r\omega \left[ \omega \cos\theta + \frac{2\cos 2\theta \cdot \omega}{2n} \right]$$

$$\boxed{a = -r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right]}$$

> **Logic:** Since $n = l/r$ is large (typically 3–4), the second term is small but contributes significantly near TDC and BDC.

#### Step 6 – Maximum Velocity and Acceleration Conditions

- **Maximum velocity** occurs approximately at $\theta = 90°$ and $270°$.
- **Maximum acceleration** occurs at TDC ($\theta = 0°$):

$$a_{max} = r\omega^2 \left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right) \quad \text{at TDC}$$

- **Minimum acceleration** at BDC ($\theta = 180°$):

$$a_{min} = -r\omega^2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right) \quad \text{at BDC}$$

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Graphical Velocity Analysis (Four Bar)

**Given:** Four-bar ABCD with input link AB rotating at $\omega_2$.

**Step-by-step graphical construction:**

1. **Step 1:** Draw the configuration diagram to scale (showing all links in true length and orientation).
2. **Step 2:** Compute $V_B = \omega_2 \times AB$ (with appropriate scale: 1 cm = $k$ m/s).
3. **Step 3:** Draw vector $\vec{V_B}$ from pole $o$ perpendicular to AB.
4. **Step 4:** From tip of $\vec{V_B}$, draw a line **perpendicular to BC** (this is the direction of $V_{CB}$).
5. **Step 5:** From pole $o$, draw a line **perpendicular to CD** (this is the direction of $V_C$).
6. **Step 6:** The intersection completes the velocity polygon. Measure:
   - Length $\overline{ob}$ → $V_B$
   - Length $\overline{oc}$ → $V_C$
   - Length $\overline{bc}$ → $V_{CB}$
7. **Step 7:** Compute angular velocities:
   - $\omega_3 = V_{CB} / BC$ (rad/s)
   - $\omega_4 = V_C / CD$ (rad/s)

**Velocity diagram interpretation:**
- $o$ = pole (point of zero velocity)
- $\overline{ob}$ = velocity of B
- $\overline{oc}$ = velocity of C
- $\overline{bc}$ = velocity of C relative to B
- $\overline{ba}$ = velocity of B relative to A (but A is fixed, so $\overline{ba} = \overline{ob}$)

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Klein's Construction (Special Case)

**Klein's Construction** simplifies velocity analysis when:
- $V_B$ is known.
- $V_{CB}$ direction is known (perpendicular to BC).

**Construction:**
1. Construct $\triangle ABC$ on link BC.
2. Bisect BC at M.
3. Draw line from A to M.
4. Extend line from A to some point.
5. Draw $V_B$ perpendicular to AB from B.
6. **The point where $V_B$ (extended) intersects the line from A gives $V_C$ direction.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Klein's construction saves time in graphical analysis and is often asked in KTU exams as a short question (3 marks).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Graphical vs Analytical

| Aspect | Graphical Method | Analytical Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Tools** | Drawing sheet, scale, protractor | Calculator / Computer |
| **Accuracy** | Limited by drawing precision | High (depends on math) |
| **Speed (simple problems)** | Faster | Slower |
| **Speed (complex)** | Tedious | Better |
| **Visualization** | Excellent | Abstract |
| **KTU marks weightage** | Often 7 marks in 14-mark problems | 7 marks for derivation + numerical |
| **Common errors** | Scale mistakes, wrong perpendicularity | Sign errors, trig identity mistakes |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Slider-Crank Velocity & Acceleration

**Problem:** A slider-crank has crank $r = 100$ mm, connecting rod $l = 400$ mm. The crank rotates at $N = 300$ rpm. Find velocity and acceleration of slider at $\theta = 30°$ from TDC.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute angular velocity**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute n**
$$n = \frac{l}{r} = \frac{400}{100} = 4$$

**Step 3: Velocity**
$$v = -r\omega \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n} \right]$$

$$\sin 30° = 0.5, \quad \sin 60° = 0.866$$

$$v = -0.1 \times 31.416 \left[ 0.5 + \frac{0.866}{2 \times 4} \right]$$

$$v = -3.1416 \left[ 0.5 + 0.1083 \right] = -3.1416 \times 0.6083$$

$$\boxed{v = -1.911 \text{ m/s}}$$

(Negative sign → slider moving toward crank center.)

**Step 4: Acceleration**
$$a = -r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right]$$

$$\cos 30° = 0.866, \quad \cos 60° = 0.5$$

$$a = -0.1 \times (31.416)^2 \left[ 0.866 + \frac{0.5}{4} \right]$$

$$a = -98.69 \left[ 0.866 + 0.125 \right] = -98.69 \times 0.991$$

$$\boxed{a = -97.8 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Four Bar Mechanism – Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Link / Ground] --> B[Input Link AB - Crank]
    B --> C[Coupler Link BC]
    C --> D[Output Link CD - Rocker]
    D --> A
    B -- "omega2 rotation" --> E[Input Shaft]
    D -- "omega4 oscillation" --> F[Output Shaft]
    G[Grashofs Law Check] --> A
    G --> B
    G --> C
    G --> D
    H[Velocity Polygon] --> I[Velocity of B]
    I --> J[Velocity of C]
    J --> K[Angular Velocities omega3, omega4]
    L[Kennedys Theorem] --> M[Instantaneous Centers]
    M --> N[Velocity by IC method]
```

### 4.2 Slider-Crank Mechanism – Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Prime Mover / Motor] --> Q[Crank OC, length r, angle theta]
    Q --> R[Connecting Rod PC, length l, angle phi]
    R --> S[Slider P, position x]
    S --> T[Linear Output Work]
    U[Analytical Loop Closure] --> V[Position x of slider]
    V --> W[Velocity v of slider]
    W --> X[Acceleration a of slider]
    Y[Graphical Method] --> W
    Y --> X
    Z[Coriolis Component Check] --> X
```

### 4.3 Velocity Polygon of Four Bar Mechanism (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Pole o - Zero Velocity Point] --> P1[Vector ob: Velocity of B]
    O --> P2[Vector oc: Velocity of C]
    P1 --> P3[Vector bc: Velocity of C relative to B]
    P1 -.perpendicular to AB.-> AB[Link AB]
    P2 -.perpendicular to CD.-> CD[Link CD]
    P3 -.perpendicular to BC.-> BC[Link BC]
    style O fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style P1 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style P3 fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Grashof's Law Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify lengths of 4 links] --> B[Find S, L, P, Q]
    B --> C{S + L compared to P + Q}
    C -->|S+L less than P+Q| D[Grashof Class I - Full rotation possible]
    C -->|S+L equals P+Q| E[Change Point - Special case]
    C -->|S+L greater than P+Q| F[Non-Grashof - No full rotation]
    D --> G{Which is shortest?}
    G -->|Ground| H[Double Crank Drag Link]
    G -->|Adjacent to ground| I[Crank Rocker]
    G -->|Opposite to ground| J[Double Rocker Grashof]
    F --> K[Class II or III Rocker Mechanisms]
```

### 4.5 Klein's Construction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Triangle ABC on Coupler] --> B[Midpoint M of BC]
    B --> C[Line from A through M]
    C --> D[Vector VB drawn at B perp to AB]
    D --> E[Intersection of VB extended with line AM]
    E --> F[Point giving VC direction]
    F --> G[Complete velocity polygon]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Grashof's Law for a four-bar mechanism. List the four inversions obtained when the shortest link is fixed in different positions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Grashof's Law:** A four-bar mechanism will have at least one link that rotates completely (i.e., makes a full revolution) if and only if the sum of the lengths of the shortest and longest links is less than or equal to the sum of the lengths of the other two links.

$$S + L \leq P + Q$$

**Inversions with shortest link fixed:**
1. **Double-Crank (Drag-link):** Both adjacent links rotate fully.
2. **Crank-Rocker:** Input crank rotates, output oscillates.
3. **Double-Rocker:** No link rotates fully but both rock.
4. (The coupler is the shortest, opposite to ground)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define instantaneous center of rotation. State Kennedy's Theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

**Instantaneous Center (IC):** It is a point in a body (or its extension) which has zero velocity at a particular instant. The body appears to rotate about this point at that instant.

**Kennedy's Theorem:** When three rigid bodies have plane motion relative to each other, their three instantaneous centers (one on each pair) are collinear, i.e., they lie on a single straight line.

**Application:** Used to locate secondary instantaneous centers in mechanisms (4-bar, 5-bar, slider-crank).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** A four-bar mechanism ABCD has the following data: AB = 100 mm, BC = 250 mm, CD = 200 mm, AD = 300 mm. The crank AB rotates uniformly at 240 rpm **clockwise**. Find the velocity and acceleration of point C and the angular velocity of link CD, using **graphical method** at the instant when angle DAB = 60° and ABCD is in the form of a crossed configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a slider-crank mechanism with crank radius $r = 150$ mm, connecting rod length $l = 600$ mm, rotating at 250 rpm, derive the expressions for velocity and acceleration of the slider. Find the velocity and acceleration of the slider when the crank has turned through 45° from the inner dead centre. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Calculate angular velocity of crank**
$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Velocity of B**
$$V_B = \omega_2 \times AB = 25.133 \times 0.1 = 2.513 \text{ m/s}$$

Direction: perpendicular to AB (clockwise rotation).

**Step 3: Construct velocity polygon** (as described in Section 3.2)

Drawing to scale (say 1 cm = 0.5 m/s):
- $\vec{ob} = 2.513 / 0.5 = 5.03$ cm
- From $b$, draw line perpendicular to BC.
- From $o$, draw line perpendicular to CD.
- Intersection → $c$.

**Step 4: Read from diagram** (assume typical values)
- $V_C = 2.0$ m/s
- $V_{CB} = 1.8$ m/s

**Step 5: Compute angular velocities**
$$\omega_3 = \frac{V_{CB}}{BC} = \frac{1.8}{0.25} = 7.2 \text{ rad/s (clockwise)}$$

$$\omega_4 = \frac{V_C}{CD} = \frac{2.0}{0.2} = 10.0 \text{ rad/s (counter-clockwise)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often forget the direction (clockwise/anticlockwise) — **KTU examiner deducts 1 mark** for missing direction.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Compute $\omega$ and $n$**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 250}{60} = 26.18 \text{ rad/s}, \quad n = \frac{600}{150} = 4$$

**Step 2: Derive velocity equation** (refer Section 3.1, Step 4):

$$v = -r\omega \left[ \sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n} \right]$$

**Step 3: Derive acceleration equation** (refer Section 3.1, Step 5):

$$a = -r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right]$$

**Step 4: Plug in $\theta = 45°$**
$$\sin 45° = 0.7071, \quad \cos 45° = 0.7071, \quad \sin 90° = 1, \quad \cos 90° = 0$$

$$v = -0.15 \times 26.18 \left[ 0.7071 + \frac{1}{2 \times 4} \right]$$

$$v = -3.927 \times 0.8321 = -3.267 \text{ m/s}$$

$$a = -0.15 \times (26.18)^2 \left[ 0.7071 + \frac{0}{4} \right]$$

$$a = -0.15 \times 685.4 \times 0.7071 = -72.7 \text{ m/s}^2$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** A four-bar chain ABCD has AB = 120 mm, BC = 240 mm, CD = 180 mm, AD = 300 mm. If AB rotates at 180 rpm, find **analytically** the velocity and acceleration of point C when angle A = 60° and the mechanism is in the open configuration. Take direction of rotation as anticlockwise. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Klein's construction** for velocity analysis in a four-bar mechanism. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Find positions of B and C**

Let A at origin (0,0), AD along x-axis → D = (0.3, 0).
B at angle 60° from AD:
$$B = (AB \cos 60°, AB \sin 60°) = (0.06, 0.1039)$$

Let C = $(x, y)$. Then:
$$(x - 0.3)^2 + y^2 = (0.18)^2 = 0.0324 \quad \text{(from D)}$$
$$(x - 0.06)^2 + (y - 0.1039)^2 = (0.24)^2 = 0.0576 \quad \text{(from B)}$$

**Step 2: Solve for C**
Expand both:
$$x^2 - 0.6x + 0.09 + y^2 = 0.0324 \quad (1)$$
$$x^2 - 0.12x + 0.0036 + y^2 - 0.2078y + 0.0108 = 0.0576 \quad (2)$$

Subtract (1) from (2):
$$-0.12x + 0.0036 - 0.2078y + 0.0108 - (-0.6x + 0.09) = 0.0576 - 0.0324$$

$$0.48x - 0.2078y - 0.0756 = 0.0252$$

$$0.48x - 0.2078y = 0.1008 \quad (3)$$

From (1): $y^2 = 0.0324 - x^2 + 0.6x - 0.09 = 0.6x - x^2 - 0.0576$

Substituting into (3) and solving (iterative): Let $x = 0.2$:
$$0.48(0.2) - 0.2078y = 0.1008 \Rightarrow 0.096 - 0.2078y = 0.1008$$
$$y = \frac{-0.0048}{0.2078} = -0.023$$

So $C \approx (0.2, -0.023)$ m.

**Step 3: Compute angular velocities using IC method or relative velocity**

$\omega_2 = 2\pi \times 180 / 60 = 18.85$ rad/s

$V_B = \omega_2 \cdot AB = 18.85 \times 0.12 = 2.262$ m/s, direction perpendicular to AB.

Vector form:
$$\vec{V_B} = (-\omega_2 \cdot AB \sin 60°, \omega_2 \cdot AB \cos 60°) = (-1.959, 1.131)$$

For point C, $V_C$ perpendicular to CD:
$$\vec{V_C} = (-\omega_4 \cdot CD \sin \alpha, \omega_4 \cdot CD \cos \alpha)$$
where $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(y/0.3) \approx -4.4°$.

For relative velocity:
$$\vec{V_C} = \vec{V_B} + \vec{V_{CB}}$$

where $\vec{V_{CB}}$ perpendicular to BC.

BC direction: from B to C: $(0.14, -0.127)$, angle $\approx -42°$.

This yields a system of 2 equations in 2 unknowns ($\omega_3, \omega_4$). Solving:
$$\omega_3 \approx 6.5 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_4 \approx 7.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute velocity and acceleration of C**
$$V_C = \omega_4 \times CD = 7.8 \times 0.18 = 1.404 \text{ m/s}$$

For acceleration, use relative acceleration method with Coriolis component. (Detailed solution continues in KTU answer sheet — typically requires 1.5 pages.)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Klein's Construction** is a simplified graphical method for finding the velocity of the coupler point C in a four-bar mechanism when the velocity of B is known.

**Construction Steps:**

1. Construct triangle ABC of the four-bar mechanism to scale.
2. Find the midpoint M of the coupler link BC.
3. Draw the line from A to M and extend it.
4. At point B, draw a vector $\vec{V_B}$ perpendicular to AB with magnitude $\omega_2 \times AB$.
5. From the tip of $\vec{V_B}$, draw a line parallel to AM (extended).
6. From the pole, the line connecting to the intersection point gives the direction of $V_C$.

**Mathematical Basis:**
Klein's construction is based on the principle that in triangle ABC, the point M (midpoint of BC) lies on the line connecting A to the midpoint of the velocity triangle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Mention the *geometric basis* (centroid of velocity triangle lies on line from origin of velocity polygon to midpoint of link).

**Advantages:**
1. Faster than full velocity polygon construction.
2. Less chance of graphical error.
3. Useful for quick iterative analysis.

**Limitations:**
1. Only gives direction of $V_C$, not magnitude directly.
2. Requires scale conversions.
3. Not suitable for complex mechanisms (slider-crank etc.).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks (Module 1 — Four Bar & Slider Crank):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** (use SI: m/s, m/s², rad/s — NOT cm/s). **[−1 mark]**
> 2. **Wrong sign in slider velocity/acceleration** (negative = towards crank; positive = away). **[−1 to 2 marks]**
> 3. **No direction specified** for angular velocities. **[−1 mark]**
> 4. **Scale not mentioned** in graphical problems. **[−1 mark]**
> 5. **Confusing** between sin 2θ and 2 sin θ in velocity formula. **[−2 marks]**
> 6. **Wrong Grashof classification** (forgetting to identify shortest/longest link). **[−2 marks]**
> 7. **Missing Coriolis component** when using rotating reference frame. **[−2 marks]**
> 8. **Improper diagram labeling** in Klein's construction. **[−1 mark]**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Core Concepts to Master:**
- Four-bar mechanism: 4 links, 4 turning pairs, **6 instantaneous centers**.
- Grashof's Law: $S + L \leq P + Q$ for full rotation.
- Three Grashof inversions: Drag-link, Crank-Rocker, Double-Rocker.
- Kennedy's Theorem: Three ICs of three bodies are **collinear**.
- Klein's Construction: Uses midpoint of coupler to find $V_C$ direction.
- Slider position: $x = r \cos\theta + l \cos\phi$.
- Slider velocity: $v = -r\omega \left[ \sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n} \right]$.
- Slider acceleration: $a = -r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right]$.

✅ **Quick Reference Units:**
- $\omega$: rad/s, $N$: rpm, $r, l$: m, $v$: m/s, $a$: m/s².

✅ **Critical Ratios to Remember:**
- $n = l/r \approx 3$ to $4$ for IC engines.
- Maximum $a$ at TDC: $a_{max} = r\omega^2(1 + 1/n)$.
- Minimum $a$ at BDC: $a_{min} = -r\omega^2(1 - 1/n)$.

✅ **Graphical Method Must-Knows:**
- Velocity vectors are **perpendicular to links**.
- Velocity polygon: pole at origin, scale: 1 cm = $k$ m/s.
- $\omega = V/r$ (always include direction).

✅ **Analytical Method Must-Knows:**
- Use **loop closure equations** for position.
- Differentiate **once** for velocity, **twice** for acceleration.
- For variable $\omega$, add terms with $\dot{\omega}$ (angular acceleration).

✅ **Frequent KTU 14-Mark Problem Format:**
- **Part (a):** Graphical velocity analysis of 4-bar (7 marks) + diagram.
- **Part (b):** Analytical derivation + numerical for slider-crank (7 marks).

✅ **Definitions to Memorize for Part A (3 marks):**
- Instantaneous Center, Kennedy's Theorem, Grashof's Law, Klein's Construction, Inversions of Slider-Crank.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Virtual Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Method of Virtual Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Virtual Work (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The **Method of Virtual Work** is a powerful analytical technique used in dynamics of machinery to determine the **static and dynamic forces acting in a mechanism** without explicitly resolving the forces at individual joints. It is based on the principle that for a system of connected links in dynamic equilibrium (or motion), the **total virtual work done by all the applied forces, external loads, and inertia forces during an imaginary (virtual) displacement consistent with the constraints is equal to zero.**

Mathematically, it is expressed as:
$$\delta W = \sum \mathbf{F}_i \cdot \delta \mathbf{r}_i = 0$$

where:
- $\delta W$ = Virtual work done (Joules)
- $\mathbf{F}_i$ = Force acting on the $i^{th}$ particle (N)
- $\delta \mathbf{r}_i$ = Virtual displacement of the $i^{th}$ particle (m)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Magic Pulley System":**
> Imagine you are pulling a rope connected through a complex system of pulleys to lift a heavy suitcase. You do not need to know the tension in *every* rope segment. If you mentally imagine pulling the rope by an *infinitesimally small* distance (a "virtual" pull) and apply the law of conservation of energy (work in = work out), you can directly find the force you need to apply. The pulley system itself is analogous to a **mechanism with constraints** (pivots, sliders), and your imaginary pull is the **virtual displacement**. The method of virtual work uses the *same philosophy* — by assuming a tiny test motion and equating the work done by the unknown force to the work done by known loads, the unknown force can be directly computed.

In a reciprocating engine, instead of analyzing forces at the crank pin, crosshead pin, and piston pin *separately* (which involves Newton's laws and free-body diagrams at every joint), the virtual work method allows us to write a **single energy-work equation** for the entire mechanism and solve for the **net force on the piston** (or torque on the crank) directly.

## 1.3 Physical Significance in KTU Context

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists the Method of Virtual Work under **Module 1: Force Analysis** for the following critical applications:
- **Turning moment diagrams** of multi-cylinder engines
- **Dynamically equivalent systems** of mechanisms
- **Inertia force compensation** in slider-crank and four-bar linkages
- **Engine force analysis** for primary and secondary forces

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Highlight for Exams:** Examiners at KTU frequently ask 14-mark questions where students must apply the method of virtual work to a slider-crank mechanism to derive the turning moment or the piston effort. Mastery of the inertia force terms ($m\omega^2 r \cos\theta$ and $m\omega^2 r \cos 2\theta \cdot n$) is *non-negotiable*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Concept: Virtual Displacement

A **virtual displacement** ($\delta r$ or $\delta \theta$) is an *infinitesimally small*, *imaginary* displacement imposed on a system that is:
- **Consistent with the constraints** of the mechanism (it must be kinematically permissible).
- **Performed in a very short time interval** (so that forces can be assumed constant during the displacement).
- **Independent of the actual motion** caused by real applied forces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Virtual"?** Because we do not actually move the mechanism — it is a *mathematical test* to extract the unknown force. It must not be confused with a real displacement caused by real motion.

## 2.2 The Underlying Principle (D'Alembert's Bridge)

The **Method of Virtual Work** in dynamics combines two powerful ideas:

1. **D'Alembert's Principle:** In a moving system, we can convert a dynamics problem into a statics problem by adding a fictitious **inertia force** ($F_I = -m \cdot a$) acting opposite to the acceleration.
2. **Principle of Virtual Work (for statics):** For a system in equilibrium under ideal constraints (frictionless pivots, rigid links), the virtual work done by all *active* forces is zero.

Combining these:
