Sum F equals 0 Sum M equals 0] T3a --> SUM F4a --> SUM T4a --> SUM SUM --> OUT[Input Torque T_in at A] SUM --> REACT[Pin Reactions at A B C D] ``` ### 4.4 Equivalent Dynamic System — Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[ACTUAL Two-Mass Slider Piston plus Connecting Rod] --> B0[REPLACE rod with two equivalent masses at A and B plus concentrate moments] B0 --> C0[Single Equivalent Slider with mass m_eq plus m_p at point P] C0 --> D0[Single DOF Problem
Same a_P same inertia force as original system] A0 -.verify via.-> V1[Equal Kinetic Energy at every instant] A0 -.verify via.-> V2[Equal Driving Torque on crank] ``` --- ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, Q1(a)]** *Define the term "inertia force" as applied to a slider in a reciprocating mechanism. Why is it necessary to perform dynamic analysis even when the mechanism runs at constant speed?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** The inertia force of a slider (piston) is the product of its mass and the linear acceleration of its CG: $F_I = m_p \cdot a_p$. By D'Alembert's principle, this force is considered to act on the slider in a direction **opposite** to its acceleration. - **Why dynamic analysis (1.5 marks):** Even though the crank rotates at uniform angular velocity (ω = constant), the **slider does not** — it accelerates and decelerates every half revolution. This produces large inertia forces that vary in magnitude and direction every instant, leading to vibration, bearing wear, side thrust on the cylinder, and fluctuating crank effort. Static analysis ignores these effects. --- **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]** *State D'Alembert's principle. How is it used in the dynamic analysis of a four-bar mechanism?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement (1.5 marks):** D'Alembert's principle states that a system in dynamic equilibrium can be treated as being in **static equilibrium** if each moving mass is assumed to carry a fictitious force equal to its mass times acceleration, acting in the direction **opposite** to the acceleration (i.e., inertia force). - **Application to 4-bar (1.5 marks):** Each moving link (crank, coupler, rocker) is given a fictitious inertia force $m_i a_{G_i}$ at its CG and inertia torque $I_{G_i} \alpha_i$. The mechanism is then analyzed using the static equations $\sum F = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$ for each link. The result gives the pin reactions and the input crank torque, including all dynamic effects. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B Module-1 questions come in *internal choice*. Below are **TWO fully solved independent alternatives**. In the exam, you will be asked to answer **one of the two**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Slider-Crank Mechanism with Gas Load + Inertia* **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Model Paper, Q2(a)+(b), Module 1]** A horizontal slider-crank mechanism has the following data: - Crank radius **r = 150 mm**, connecting rod length **l = 600 mm** - Crank speed **N = 600 rpm (uniform)** - Mass of reciprocating parts (piston + pin) **m_p = 25 kg** - Mass of connecting rod **m_r = 30 kg**, CG at 0.4l from the big end, radius of gyration **k_G = 0.30 l** - Gas pressure above piston during expansion stroke varies as: $P_g = 0.6 + 0.3 \sin\theta$ N/mm² - Piston area **A = 0.005 m²** #### **Part (a)** — Find the **net piston effort** and **side thrust on the cylinder** at crank angle **θ = 30°** during the expansion stroke. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Crank angular velocity** $$ \omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi(600)}{60} \;=\; 62.832\;\text{rad/s} $$ **Step 2 — Piston linear acceleration at θ = 30°** Using the derived formula (with r/l = 150/600 = 0.25): $$ a_{P} \;=\; r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta \;+\; \dfrac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right) $$ $$ a_{P} \;=\; 0.150\,(62.832)^{2}\left(\cos 30^{\circ} \;+\; 0.25\cos 60^{\circ}\right) $$ $$ a_{P} \;=\; 0.150\,(3947.84)\left(0.8660 \;+\; 0.125\right) $$ $$ a_{P} \;=\; 592.176\,(0.9910) \;=\; 586.85\;\text{m/s}^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Inertia force of reciprocating parts** $$ F_{Ip} \;=\; m_p \cdot a_{P} \;=\; 25 \times 586.85 \;=\; 14{,}671.25\;\text{N} $$ This force acts on the piston in the direction **opposite** to the piston acceleration. At θ = 30° during expansion, the piston is moving toward TDC and decelerating — so the inertia force is *directed toward IDC* (i.e., in the same direction as the gas load, both pushing piston outward from cylinder head). > *Convention adopted:* Both positive — gas pressure pushes the piston; inertia also pushes it outward (deceleration). **Step 4 — Gas (external) load on piston** $$ P_g(\theta) \;=\; 0.6 + 0.3\sin 30^{\circ} \;=\; 0.6 + 0.3(0.5) \;=\; 0.75\;\text{N/mm}^{2} $$ $$ F_{gas} \;=\; P_g \cdot A \;=\; 0.75 \times 10^{6} \times 0.005 \;=\; 3750\;\text{N} $$ **Step 5 — Net Piston Effort (along the line of stroke)** For a horizontal mechanism, there is no gravity component along the stroke: $$ F_{effort} \;=\; F_{gas} \;+\; F_{Ip} \;=\; 3750 + 14{,}671.25 \;=\; 18{,}421.25\;\text{N} $$ Direction: pushing the piston toward the crank (this is what the crank-pin must resist during expansion). **Step 6 — Side thrust on the cylinder** Angle of connecting rod with stroke line: $$ \sin\phi \;=\; \dfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta \;=\; 0.25 \sin 30^{\circ} \;=\; 0.125 $$ $$ \phi \;=\; 7.18^{\circ} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \tan\phi \;=\; 0.1260 $$ $$ F_{N} \;=\; F_{effort} \tan\phi \;=\; 18{,}421.25 \times 0.1260 $$ $$ \boxed{\,F_{N} \;=\; 2321.07\;\text{N}\,} $$ **Step 7 — Net Piston Effort (final boxed answer)** $$ \boxed{\,F_{effort} \;=\; 18{,}421.25\;\text{N} \approx 18.42\;\text{kN}\,} $$ --- #### **Part (b)** — Determine the **crank-pin tangential force**, the **crank effort (torque)**, and the **turning moment** at θ = 30°. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Angle φ of connecting rod with stroke** From above, $\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - (0.125)^2} = 0.9922$. **Step 2 — Connecting-rod angular acceleration** $$ \alpha_{r} \;=\; -\dfrac{r}{l}\omega^{2}\sin\theta \;=\; -0.25\,(3947.84)(0.5) \;=\; -493.48\;\text{rad/s}^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Inertia torque of connecting rod** Moment of inertia of rod about CG: $$ I_{G} \;=\; m_{r} \cdot k_{G}^{2} \;=\; 30 \times (0.30 \times 0.6)^{2} \;=\; 30 \times (0.18)^{2} \;=\; 30 \times 0.0324 \;=\; 0.972\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ $$ T_{Ir} \;=\; I_{G} \cdot \vert \alpha_{r} \vert \;=\; 0.972 \times 493.48 \;=\; 479.66\;\text{N·m} $$ **Step 4 — Inertia force of rod CG** Linear acceleration of CG of rod (approximated for KTU level as $a_G \approx 0.4 \cdot a_P$ when CG is at 0.4l from big end and rod angle is small — for a more rigorous answer, $a_G$ should be found from the rigid-body acceleration analysis of the rod): $$ a_{G} \;\approx\; \left(\dfrac{c}{l}\right) \cdot a_{P} \;\text{ (first-order approx.)} $$ where c = distance from big end A to G = 0.4l = 0.24 m. $$ a_{G} \;\approx\; 0.4 \times 586.85 \;=\; 234.74\;\text{m/s}^{2} $$ $$ F_{Ir} \;=\; m_{r} \cdot a_{G} \;=\; 30 \times 234.74 \;=\; 7042.2\;\text{N} $$ **Step 5 — Resultant force on crank pin (tangential direction)** Forces on the crank pin must be resolved into radial (along OA) and tangential (perpendicular to OA) directions. The tangential component drives the crank. Using the standard KTU 2024 formula for a slider-crank: $$ F_{T} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{effort} \cdot \cos\phi}{\cos\theta} $$ $$ F_{T} \;=\; \dfrac{18{,}421.25 \times 0.9922}{\cos 30^{\circ}} \;=\; \dfrac{18{,}277.6}{0.8660} $$ $$ \boxed{\,F_{T} \;=\; 21{,}106.7\;\text{N} \approx 21.11\;\text{kN}\,} $$ (Plus a small correction from the rod's inertia torque, but the *crank effort* is the dominant answer at this level.) **Step 6 — Crank Effort (Torque on crank shaft)** $$ T_{crank} \;=\; F_{T} \cdot r \;=\; 21{,}106.7 \times 0.15 $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_{crank} \;=\; 3166.0\;\text{N·m} \approx 3.166\;\text{kN·m}\,} $$ **Step 7 — Turning Moment (same as crank torque)** In KTU Module-1 notation, the "turning moment" at the crank pin is identical to the crank effort: $$ \boxed{\,M_{\text{turning}} \;=\; 3166.0\;\text{N·m}\,} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Four-Bar Mechanism Dynamic Analysis* **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Model Paper, Q3 Module 1 (Alt Choice)]** A four-bar mechanism ABCD has the following dimensions and mass properties. The crank AB rotates **uniformly at 240 rpm** (ω₂ = constant). All links are in the plane of motion. | Link | Length (m) | Mass (kg) | CG location (% of length, from A side) | Radius of gyration (m) | |------|------------|-----------|----------------------------------------|------------------------| | 2 (Crank AB) | 0.20 | 8 | 50% | 0.07 | | 3 (Coupler BC) | 0.45 | 15 | 45% | 0.15 | | 4 (Rocker CD) | 0.35 | 12 | 50% | 0.10 | **Geometric position at the instant of interest (obtained by velocity/acceleration analysis):** - Crank angle θ₂ = 60° from fixed link AD - Angular velocity of link 3: ω₃ = +4.5 rad/s - Angular velocity of link 4: ω₄ = −2.1 rad/s - Angular acceleration of link 3: α₃ = +12.0 rad/s² - Angular acceleration of link 4: α₄ = −8.5 rad/s² - Acceleration of CG of link 3: a_{G3} = 18 m/s² (direction: known, resolved into components) - Acceleration of CG of link 4: a_{G4} = 9.5 m/s² #### **Part (a)** — Compute the **inertia force and inertia torque of the coupler (link 3)** and the **rocker (link 4)**. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Moments of inertia** $$ I_{G3} \;=\; m_3 \cdot k_{3}^{2} \;=\; 15 \times (0.15)^{2} \;=\; 15 \times 0.0225 \;=\; 0.3375\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ $$ I_{G4} \;=\; m_4 \cdot k_{4}^{2} \;=\; 12 \times (0.10)^{2} \;=\; 12 \times 0.01 \;=\; 0.12\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Inertia torques** For link 3 (coupler): $$ T_{I3} \;=\; I_{G3} \cdot \vert \alpha_{3} \vert \;=\; 0.3375 \times 12.0 \;=\; 4.05\;\text{N·m} $$ Applied in the **opposite direction** to α₃ (i.e., if α₃ is counter-clockwise, $T_{I3}$ acts clockwise on the link). For link 4 (rocker): $$ T_{I4} \;=\; I_{G4} \cdot \vert \alpha_{4} \vert \;=\; 0.12 \times 8.5 \;=\; 1.02\;\text{N·m} $$ Applied in the **opposite direction** to α₄. **Step 3 — Inertia forces at CG** For link 3: $$ F_{I3} \;=\; m_3 \cdot a_{G3} \;=\; 15 \times 18 \;=\; 270\;\text{N} $$ For link 4: $$ F_{I4} \;=\; m_4 \cdot a_{G4} \;=\; 12 \times 9.5 \;=\; 114\;\text{N} $$ **Summary Table:** | Link | Inertia Force (N) | Inertia Torque (N·m) | |------|--------------------|----------------------| | 3 (Coupler) | **270.0** | **4.05** | | 4 (Rocker) | **114.0** | **1.02** | (All act in directions opposite to their respective accelerations.) --- #### **Part (b)** — Determine the **input torque at the crank pin A** required to drive the mechanism against the inertia loads. (7 marks) **Solution:** **Strategy:** Use the **Principle of Virtual Work** (also called the *energy method*). The power input by the driving torque equals the rate of change of kinetic energy of the system. $$ T_{in} \cdot \omega_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\sum KE_{i}\right) $$ **Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of Each Link** For a rigid link in plane motion: $$ KE_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} m_i v_{G_i}^{2} \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2} I_{G_i} \omega_{i}^{2} $$ For link 2 (uniform crank): $$ KE_2 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}(8)(\omega_2 \cdot 0.10)^{2} \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2}(0.0392)(\omega_2)^{2} $$ (Here $k_2 = 0.07$ m, $I_{G2} = 8 \times 0.0049 = 0.0392$ kg·m²; CG at midspan = 0.10 m from A.) $$ \omega_2 \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi(240)}{60} \;=\; 25.133\;\text{rad/s} $$ $$ v_{G2} \;=\; 0.10 \times 25.133 \;=\; 2.513\;\text{m/s} $$ $$ KE_2 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}(8)(2.513)^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}(0.0392)(25.133)^{2} \;=\; 25.27 + 12.38 \;=\; 37.65\;\text{J} $$ For link 3: $$ KE_3 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}(15)(18)^{2} \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2}(0.3375)(4.5)^{2} \;=\; 2430 + 3.42 \;=\; 2433.42\;\text{J} $$ For link 4: $$ KE_4 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}(12)(9.5)^{2} \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2}(0.12)(2.1)^{2} \;=\; 541.5 + 0.265 \;=\; 541.77\;\text{J} $$ **Step 2 — Rate of Change of Total KE** $$ \dfrac{d}{dt}(KE_{tot}) \;=\; \dfrac{d}{dt}(37.65 + 2433.42 + 541.77) \;=\; \dfrac{d}{dt}(3012.84) $$ Since only the *rotational* part (containing ω) varies with time, and ω_2 = constant, we differentiate the ω-dependent terms: $$ \dfrac{d}{dt}(KE_2) \;=\; 0 \quad (\text{since } \omega_2 = \text{const, } v_{G2} = \text{const}) $$ $$ \dfrac{d}{dt}(KE_3) \;=\; m_3 \vec{v}_{G3} \cdot \vec{a}_{G3} \;+\; I_{G3} \omega_3 \alpha_3 $$ $$ \dfrac{d}{dt}(KE_3) \;=\; 15 \times 4.5 \times 18 \times \cos 0^{\circ} \;+\; 0.3375 \times 4.5 \times 12.0 $$ (Assuming $v_{G3} \cdot a_{G3}$ is the product of magnitudes when the velocity and acceleration are aligned for the instant of interest.) $$ \dfrac{d}{dt}(KE_3) \;=\; 1215 + 18.225 \;=\; 1233.225\;\text{W} $$ $$ \dfrac{d}{dt}(KE_4) \;=\; m_4 \vec{v}_{G4} \cdot \vec{a}_{G4} \;+\; I_{G4} \omega_4 \alpha_4 $$ $$ \dfrac{d}{dt}(KE_4) \;=\; 12 \times 2.1 \times 9.5 \;+\; 0.12 \times 2.1 \times 8.5 $$ $$ \dfrac{d}{dt}(KE_4) \;=\; 239.4 + 2.142 \;=\; 241.542\;\text{W} $$ **Step 3 — Total Power Required** $$ P_{in} \;=\; 0 + 1233.225 + 241.542 \;=\; 1474.77\;\text{W} $$ **Step 4 — Input Torque** $$ T_{in} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{in}}{\omega_2} \;=\; \dfrac{1474.77}{25.133} $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_{in} \;=\; 58.68\;\text{N·m}\,} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Dynamic Analysis — Module 1):** > 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often forget that the inertia force is **reversed** to acceleration, leading to wrong sign in the final piston effort. *Always* draw the direction of acceleration first, then mark the inertia force with an arrow in the **opposite** direction. > 2. **Forgetting the cos 2θ term (–2 marks):** In the piston acceleration formula, the secondary term $\frac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta$ is often omitted. This is acceptable only if the question explicitly says "neglect the obliquity of the connecting rod". > 3. **Inertia Torque of the Connecting Rod (–3 marks):** The rod's *inertia force at CG* and *inertia torque* are two separate effects. Many students compute only one. Both must be drawn on the free-body diagram. > 4. **4-Bar Mechanism: Wrong Angular Acceleration Sign (–4 marks):** In the virtual-work method, the sign of $\omega_i \alpha_i$ matters. If you get the sign of α wrong, the input torque sign will flip. > 5. **Mixing up the equivalent mass formula (–2 marks):** For two-mass reduction, write the full kinetic-energy-equivalence derivation rather than just quoting the formula. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print This List and Read Twice Before the Exam** - [ ] **D'Alembert's Principle** turns a *dynamic* problem into a *static-equilibrium* problem: include $-m\vec{a}$ and $-I\alpha$ on every moving part. - [ ] **Assumption in Module 1:** Crank rotates at **uniform ω** → crank itself has **zero angular acceleration (α = 0)**, but **slider and rod DO have acceleration**. - [ ] **Piston acceleration formula** has two parts: a **primary (cos θ)** term and a **secondary (cos 2θ)** term. Both are *positive* for $\theta \in (-90°, 90°)$, meaning the piston is accelerating toward IDC. - [ ] **Sign of inertia force = OPPOSITE to direction of acceleration.** Draw the acceleration arrow first, then the inertia force arrow opposite to it. - [ ] **Net Piston Effort = (Effective gas pressure × Area) ± Inertia force of reciprocating mass − Weight component (for vertical engines)**. Use the **right sign** for inertia. - [ ] **Side thrust on the cylinder = $F_P \tan \phi$** — this is what wears out piston rings. Minimise by minimising r/l. - [ ] **Connecting rod inertia** has TWO parts: a **force at its CG** and a **torque about its CG**. Treat them as separate effects. - [ ] **Equivalent Dynamic System** reduces a multi-link mechanism to a single equivalent slider with mass $m_{eq} = m_{rod} \cdot \dfrac{a_G}{a_B} + \dfrac{I_G \alpha}{a_B \cdot l}$ (approx.). Use this when the problem asks for "equivalent system". - [ ] **Crank Effort (Torque) = Tangential force at crank pin × crank radius**. Tangential force comes from the moment balance about the main bearing O. - [ ] **4-Bar Mechanism** dynamic analysis: use the **virtual-work / energy method** (cleanest at KTU level) or the **link-by-link equilibrium** method (more laborious but more general). - [ ] **Virtual Work formula** for input torque: $$ T_{in} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum \left( F_{Ii} v_{Gi} \cos\beta_i \;+\; T_{Ii} \omega_i \right)}{\omega_{crank}} $$ where $\beta_i$ is the angle between the velocity and acceleration of the CG of link $i$. - [ ] **Units check:** kg, m, s, N, N·m, rad, rad/s, rad/s². Do NOT mix N and kgf. - [ ] **Use g = 9.81 m/s²** in all gravitational and weight conversions. - [ ] **Free-body diagrams** are worth **at least 2-3 marks** in 14-mark questions. Always draw the FBD with all four forces on the rod: gas/piston force at B, crank-pin reaction at A, inertia force at G, inertia torque about G. - [ ] **Memorise the standard assumption:** "The crank rotates at uniform angular velocity" — this is the Module-1 gate. If this is not stated, the problem belongs to Module 2/3 (flywheel/torque fluctuation). ---

topics.title: Gyroscope: Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Bearings, Stability of Two Wheel and Four Wheel Vehicles, Ships and Aircrafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gyroscope: Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Bearings, Stability of Two Wheel and Four Wheel Vehicles, Ships and Aircrafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Gyroscope

A **gyroscope** is a symmetrical rotating body (a rotor or disc) mounted such that it is free to turn about any of the three mutually perpendicular axes passing through a common point. The three axes are:
1. **Spin axis** – the axis about which the disc/rotor spins with angular velocity $\omega$.
2. **Precession axis** – the axis about which the spin axis rotates, with angular velocity $\omega_p$.
3. **Torque axis (or Active axis)** – the axis perpendicular to both, along which the active gyroscopic couple $C$ acts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** A *gyroscopic couple* is the reactive couple that arises in a rotating body when it is compelled to change the direction of its axis of spin. Its magnitude is given by $C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p$, where $I$ is the mass moment of inertia of the rotor about the spin axis.

### 1.2 The Angular Momentum Concept

The angular momentum $\vec{H}$ of a rotating disc is given by:

$$\vec{H} = I\,\vec{\omega}$$

The direction of $\vec{H}$ (and $\vec{\omega}$) is found using the **Right-Hand Rule (RHR)**: curl the fingers of the right hand in the direction of rotation; the thumb points along $\vec{\omega}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Top:** Imagine a spinning top on a table. As long as it spins fast, it stays upright. If you gently push its tip sideways, instead of falling over, the top slowly *precesses* around the vertical. The same principle allows a bicycle wheel to "stay up" while moving and lets a ship's compass remain stable in rough seas. This resistance to change in the direction of the spin axis is what we exploit in the gyroscope.

### 1.3 Precession and Gyroscopic Rigidity

- **Gyroscopic Rigidity:** The property of a spinning rotor to maintain the direction of its spin axis in space.
- **Precession:** The slow circular motion executed by the spin axis when subjected to an external torque.

> [!TIP]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** 
> - **Active gyroscopic couple** — exerted *by* the rotor on the bearings.
> - **Reactive gyroscopic couple** — exerted *by* the bearings on the rotor, equal and opposite to the active couple.
> - The direction of the reactive couple is found by rotating the angular momentum vector $\vec{H}$ *toward* the precession vector $\vec{\omega_p}$ through 90°.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Exams

In KTU board examinations (2024 Scheme), this topic is a **high-weightage, high-scoring module** if practiced with vectors. Almost every year, the ESE has at least one 14-mark question from gyroscopic applications. The four major sub-topics tested are:

| Sub-Topic | Typical Marks |
|-----------|---------------|
| Gyroscopic couple on a bearing/shaft | 3–7 |
| Stability of a two-wheeled vehicle | 7–14 |
| Stability of a four-wheeled vehicle | 7–14 |
| Stability of a ship (steering/pitching/rolling) | 7–14 |
| Stability of an aircraft during turn | 3–7 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Core Equation — Derivation of Gyroscopic Couple

Consider a disc of mass $m$, radius of gyration $k$, spinning about its own axis with angular velocity $\omega$. The disc is mounted on a shaft that is itself turning about a perpendicular axis with angular velocity $\omega_p$ (precession).

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Angular momentum of the disc about the spin axis: $H = I\,\omega$, where $I = m k^2$.
2. The disc is forced to change the direction of $\vec{H}$ because the spin axis itself rotates.
3. The rate of change of angular momentum in precession equals the gyroscopic couple required:

$$\boxed{\,C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p \sin\theta\,}$$

4. Since the spin axis is *perpendicular* to the precession axis, $\theta = 90°$, and $\sin 90° = 1$. Therefore:

$$C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p = m k^2 \omega \omega_p$$

> [!NOTE]
> The gyroscopic couple is **zero** when $\omega = 0$ (no spin) or when $\omega_p = 0$ (no precession). It is *maximum* when $\omega$ and $\omega_p$ are both large and mutually perpendicular.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Application | Formula | Vector Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General gyroscopic couple | $C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p$ | Perpendicular to both $\vec{\omega}$ and $\vec{\omega_p}$ |
| 2 | Inertia form | $C = m\,k^2\,\omega\,\omega_p$ | — |
| 3 | Two-wheeler, turning | $C = I_W\,\omega_W\,\omega_p$ | Tilts vehicle outward or inward |
| 4 | Four-wheeler, all wheels | $C = I_W\,\omega_W\,\omega_p \times 2$ (rear-driven) | Tilts vehicle |
| 5 | Ship — steering | $C = I\,\omega_S\,\omega_p$ | Heeling couple |
| 6 | Ship — pitching | $C = I\,\omega_S\,\omega_p \cos\theta$ | Pitching couple |
| 7 | Ship — rolling | $C = I_E\,\omega_E\,\omega_p$ | Rolling couple |
| 8 | Aircraft — turning | $C = I_P\,\omega_P\,\omega_p$ | Tilts aircraft |

> **Symbols Used:**
> - $I_W$ = moment of inertia of one wheel, $I_P$ = propeller/engine, $I_E$ = engine, $I_S$ = ship.
> - $\omega$ = spin angular velocity, $\omega_p$ = precession (turning) angular velocity.
> - $C$ = active gyroscopic couple, $-C$ = reactive gyroscopic couple.

### 2.3 Vector Method for Direction (Mandatory for KTU)

The **active gyroscopic couple** vector is given by:

$$\vec{C}_A = \vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H} = \vec{\omega_p} \times (I\,\vec{\omega})$$

The **reactive gyroscopic couple** on the rotor:

$$\vec{C}_R = -\vec{C}_A = \vec{H} \times \vec{\omega_p} = I\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{\omega_p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always draw the two vectors $\vec{\omega}$ and $\vec{\omega_p}$, find their cross product, and label the active couple. Then the reactive couple is on the *opposite* side. This 1-step diagram carries 2 marks alone.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Inertial Navigation Systems (INS), Hubble Telescope pointing, gyrocompasses.
- **Automotive:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC), motorcycle safety via counter-steering, ride dynamics.
- **Marine:** Anti-rolling gyros on yachts and warships, ship stabilization fins.
- **Defense & Robotics:** Ring-laser gyros, MEMS gyroscopes in smartphones and drones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Bearings

A disc of mass $m$ and radius of gyration $k$ is mounted on a shaft rotating at $\omega$ rad/s. The whole shaft is constrained to turn (precess) about a perpendicular axis at $\omega_p$ rad/s.

The disc is supported by two bearings $A$ and $B$. The gyroscopic couple $C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p$ acts on the rotor; the **reactive couple** on the bearings is equal and opposite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use:** In turbine rotors of aircraft and ships, the bearing reactions must be designed to carry both the static weight and the dynamic gyroscopic reactive couple, otherwise the bearings fail.

### 3.2 Stability of a Two-Wheeled Vehicle (Motorcycle/Bicycle)

**Given:**
- Mass of vehicle with rider: $m$ kg
- Radius of each wheel: $R$ m
- Moment of inertia of each wheel about spin axis: $I_W$ kg·m²
- Speed of vehicle: $v$ m/s
- Radius of curvature of path: $r$ m
- Height of C.G. of vehicle from road: $h$ m
- Distance between wheels (wheelbase): $a$ m
- Angle of lean with vertical: $\phi$

**Step 1 — Spin angular velocity of wheel:**

The wheel rolls without slipping, so:

$$\omega_W = \frac{v}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Precession (turning) angular velocity:**

When the vehicle moves on a curved path of radius $r$:

$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Active gyroscopic couple:**

$$C = I_W \cdot \omega_W \cdot \omega_p = I_W \cdot \frac{v}{R} \cdot \frac{v}{r} = \frac{I_W \, v^2}{R\,r}$$

**Step 4 — Direction (Left Turn):**
- $\vec{\omega_W}$ points along the wheel axis to the left.
- $\vec{\omega_p}$ points vertically upward.
- $\vec{C}_A = \vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H}$ → points in the forward direction.
- Reactive couple $\vec{C}_R$ → points **inward** (toward the centre of the curve).

**Step 5 — Condition for stability (no overturning):**

The centrifugal force $F_c = \dfrac{m v^2}{r}$ acts outward at the C.G. The gyroscopic reactive couple $C$ acts inward (on the wheels) — i.e., it tries to tilt the rider **outward** (in the same sense as overturning).

The overturning moment about the outer wheel contact point:

$$M_{ov} = F_c \cdot h = \frac{m v^2 h}{r} + C$$

The restoring moment due to the weight of the vehicle, which is leaned at angle $\phi$ inward:

$$M_{res} = m g \cdot x$$

where $x$ is the horizontal distance between the C.G. and the contact point of the outer wheel.

**Condition for stability:**

$$\frac{m v^2 h}{r} + C \;\le\; m g \cdot x \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{m v^2 h}{r} + I_W \frac{v^2}{R r} \le m g \tan\phi \cdot h$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Exams:** Students *add* $C$ to the centrifugal moment thinking the gyroscopic couple stabilizes the vehicle. **In fact, the reactive couple acts in the *unfavourable* direction (outward tilt)** when the wheels rotate such that their spin axis is along the vehicle's lateral axis during a turn. Always re-derive the direction carefully using the cross product.

**Step 6 — Minimum radius for safe turn (or minimum speed):**

For a given lean angle $\phi$ and wheelbase $a$ (where $x = a\sin\phi$ approximately if the lean axis is at the wheel contact):

$$\frac{m v^2 h}{r} + \frac{I_W v^2}{R r} = m g a \sin\phi \quad \text{(for two-wheelers, with $x = a\sin\phi$)}$$

Solving for $r$:

$$r = \frac{v^2}{g \tan\phi} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{I_W}{m R h}\right)$$

Or equivalently for the lean angle:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{v^2}{g r} \left(1 + \frac{I_W}{m R h}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> The term $\left(1 + \dfrac{I_W}{m R h}\right)$ is the **gyroscopic correction factor**. It shows that the gyroscopic effect of the wheels *increases the required lean angle* for a given turn. This is why racing motorcycles lean much more than bicycles.

### 3.3 Stability of a Four-Wheeled Vehicle

A four-wheeler has *two* pairs of wheels. Each pair has the same spin axis. There are **two distinct cases** based on which axle is the driving axle.

#### Case 1: Rear-Wheel Driven (e.g., most cars, rear-engine cars)

All **four wheels** have the same spin axis (transverse) and same direction of spin. The total angular momentum of the wheels is:

$$H = 4 I_W \omega_W = 4 I_W \cdot \frac{v}{R}$$

**Gyroscopic couple:**

$$C = H \cdot \omega_p = 4 I_W \cdot \frac{v}{R} \cdot \frac{v}{r} = \frac{4 I_W v^2}{R r}$$

**Direction:** 
- For a left turn, $\vec{\omega_p}$ is upward, $\vec{\omega_W}$ is to the left.
- $\vec{C}_A = \vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H}$ points **forward**.
- $\vec{C}_R$ points **backward** (rearward).

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Result:** In a rear-driven car taking a left turn, the reactive gyroscopic couple tends to tilt the **front of the car upward** and the **rear downward** — the car appears to "squat" at the rear.

The overturning moment about the outer wheels:

$$M_{ov} = F_c \cdot h + C = \frac{m v^2 h}{r} + \frac{4 I_W v^2}{R r}$$

The restoring moment due to weight, with half-wheelbase $b/2$ where $b$ = wheel track:

$$M_{res} = m g \cdot \frac{b}{2}$$

**Stability condition:**

$$\frac{m v^2 h}{r} + \frac{4 I_W v^2}{R r} \le m g \cdot \frac{b}{2}$$

#### Case 2: Front-Wheel Driven (e.g., front-engine front-wheel-drive cars)

The front pair and rear pair have **opposite spin senses** (they must, to roll the car forward). Therefore, their angular momenta are in **opposite directions** and *cancel out partially*:

$$H_{net} = 2 I_W \omega_W - 2 I_W \omega_W \cdot (-1) = 0 \;\; \text{(if wheels are identical)}$$

Wait — careful: both *front* wheels spin one way and both *rear* wheels spin the *opposite* way. So:

$$H_{front} = 2 I_W \omega_W, \quad H_{rear} = -2 I_W \omega_W$$

If the wheel moments are equal in magnitude, $H_{net} = 0$, and the **gyroscopic effect is theoretically zero**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In a four-wheeler, do *not* blindly add the inertias of all four wheels. Look carefully at the spin directions. For front-driven cars, the front and rear wheel contributions cancel. This is a classic KTU trick question.

### 3.4 Stability of a Ship

A ship has three principal motions, all of which involve gyroscopic effects due to the propeller (engine) rotating at high speed.

#### 3.4.1 Steering of a Ship

When the ship's rudder is turned, the ship turns with angular velocity $\omega_p$. The propeller spins with angular velocity $\omega_S$.

**Given:**
- $m$ = mass of ship
- $k$ = radius of gyration of ship about transverse axis (for steering, pitch axis)
- $I = m k^2$ = moment of inertia of ship
- $\omega_S$ = angular velocity of ship/engine
- $\omega_p = \dfrac{v}{R}$ where $R$ = radius of turn, $v$ = ship speed

**Active gyroscopic couple:**

$$C = I \cdot \omega_S \cdot \omega_p = \frac{m k^2 \omega_S v}{R}$$

**Effect on ship:** 
The reactive couple tends to **heel** the ship. If the ship steers to the **port (left)**, the propeller's spin axis is along the ship's longitudinal axis, and precession is vertical. The cross product gives a couple about the ship's transverse axis that heels the ship.

**Direction rule:** When the ship steers to port:
- The reactive couple causes **heeling toward the starboard** (outward side).
- Conversely, steering to starboard causes heeling toward port.

#### 3.4.2 Pitching of a Ship

Pitching is the fore-and-aft oscillation caused by waves. The ship rolls about a transverse axis (pitch axis) at $\omega_p$ rad/s. The propeller spin axis is along the longitudinal axis. The angle between the spin axis and the precession (pitch) axis is **90°** (perpendicular).

$$C = I \cdot \omega_S \cdot \omega_p = m k^2 \omega_S \omega_p$$

**Effect:** The reactive couple causes the ship to **pitch nose-up or nose-down** depending on the wave direction.

If the pitching motion is at an angle $\theta$ to the vertical of the propeller axis (waves coming at angle), the perpendicular component of pitching is $\omega_p \cos\theta$, and:

$$C = m k^2 \omega_S \omega_p \cos\theta$$

#### 3.4.3 Rolling of a Ship

Rolling is side-to-side motion about the longitudinal axis. The propeller spin axis is *parallel* to the rolling axis. Therefore, the angle between $\vec{\omega_S}$ and $\vec{\omega_p}$ is **0° or 180°**, so:

$$C = m k^2 \omega_S \omega_p \sin 0° = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result — Rolling:** When the ship rolls, the propeller's spin axis is parallel to the roll axis, so the gyroscopic couple is **zero**. Rolling motion is *not* resisted by the gyroscopic effect of the propeller; it is countered by bilge keels, anti-roll tanks, or stabilizer fins.

> **Summary Table for Ship Stability:**

| Motion | Axis of Motion | Angle to Spin Axis | Gyroscopic Couple |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steering | Transverse (heeling) | 90° | $m k^2 \omega_S \omega_p$ |
| Pitching | Transverse (longitudinal rocking) | 90° | $m k^2 \omega_S \omega_p \cos\theta$ |
| Rolling | Longitudinal | 0° | $0$ |

### 3.5 Stability of an Aircraft

An aircraft's main gyroscopic element is the **propeller** (in propeller aircraft) or the **turbojet/fan rotor** (in jets). The spinning rotor's axis is the longitudinal axis of the aircraft.

**Three scenarios:**

#### 3.5.1 Aircraft taking a turn
- Aircraft yaws (turns) at $\omega_p$ rad/s about the vertical axis.
- The propeller spin axis is *longitudinal*, so the spin axis is horizontal, perpendicular to the vertical precession axis.
- Active couple: $C = I_P \omega_P \omega_p$, where $I_P$ is the moment of inertia of the propeller.

**Effect:** The reactive couple causes the aircraft to **roll**. For a left turn, the aircraft rolls outward (right wing dips), tending to *oppose* the turn.

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why the ailerons and the entire flight control system are designed — the gyroscopic effect is small for typical turn rates but is critical in aerobatic maneuvers.

#### 3.5.2 Aircraft pitching (climb/dive)
- Aircraft pitches about the transverse axis at $\omega_p$.
- Propeller axis is longitudinal; pitch axis is transverse. They are **perpendicular**.
- Active couple: $C = I_P \omega_P \omega_p$.

**Effect:** The reactive couple causes **yawing** of the aircraft nose.

#### 3.5.3 Aircraft rolling
- Aircraft rolls about the longitudinal axis.
- Propeller axis *coincides* with the roll axis.
- Angle between them = 0° or 180°, so $C = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result — Rolling Aircraft:** When the aircraft rolls, the gyroscopic effect of the propeller is **zero**. Rolling is purely an aerodynamic phenomenon, not a gyroscopic one.

### 3.6 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| System | Motion | Spin Axis vs Precession Axis | Couple |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disc on shaft (generic) | Turning | Perpendicular | $I\omega\omega_p$ |
| Two-wheeler | Turning | Perpendicular | $I_W \omega_W \omega_p$ |
| Four-wheeler (rear drive) | Turning | Same direction, all 4 wheels | $4 I_W \omega_W \omega_p$ |
| Four-wheeler (front drive) | Turning | Opposite directions, cancel | $0$ (net) |
| Ship | Steering | 90° | $mk^2\omega_S\omega_p$ |
| Ship | Pitching | 90° | $mk^2\omega_S\omega_p\cos\theta$ |
| Ship | Rolling | 0° | $0$ |
| Aircraft | Turning (yaw) | 90° | $I_P\omega_P\omega_p$ |
| Aircraft | Pitching | 90° | $I_P\omega_P\omega_p$ |
| Aircraft | Rolling | 0° | $0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Gyroscopic Couple in a Turning Vehicle

The following mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of forces and couples acting on a two-wheeled vehicle taking a left turn. This is the closest representation suitable for the text-based KTU answer key; a 3D physical sketch (axes, vectors) should accompany this in the answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Driver Input: Steering Left] --> nodeB[Vehicle Velocity v]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Wheel Spin omegaW equals v over R]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Angular Momentum H equals IW times omegaW]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Precession omegaP equals v over r]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Cross Product omegaP cross H]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Active Couple CA along Forward Axis]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Reactive Couple CR along Rearward Axis]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Heeling Moment outward tilt]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Stability Check}
    nodeJ -->|Satisfied| nodeK[Vehicle Remains Stable on Curve]
    nodeJ -->|Violated| nodeL[Vehicle Skids or Topples Outward]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Ship Motion Classification and Gyroscopic Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ship in Motion] --> nodeB{Motion Type}
    nodeB -->|Helm Deflection| nodeC[Steering omegaP vertical]
    nodeB -->|Wave Action Fore-Aft| nodeD[Pitching omegaP transverse]
    nodeB -->|Wave Action Side-Side| nodeE[Rolling omegaP longitudinal]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Angle Spin to Precession equals 90 deg]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Angle equals 90 deg with cos theta factor]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Angle equals 0 deg]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Gyroscopic Couple mk squared omegaS omegaP]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Gyroscopic Couple mk squared omegaS omegaP cos theta]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Couple equals 0]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Effect Heeling of Ship]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Effect Pitch Oscillation]
    nodeK --> nodeN[No Gyroscopic Effect on Rolling]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Aircraft Gyroscopic Effects

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Propeller Spinning at omegaP along Longitudinal Axis] --> nodeB{Aircraft Motion}
    nodeB -->|Turn Yaw| nodeC[Precession Vertical Axis]
    nodeB -->|Climb Dive Pitch| nodeD[Precession Transverse Axis]
    nodeB -->|Bank Roll| nodeE[Precession Longitudinal Axis]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Active Couple IP omegaP omegaP yaw]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Active Couple IP omegaP omegaP pitch]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Couple Equals 0 Roll]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Reactive Effect Aircraft Rolls Outward]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Reactive Effect Nose Yaws]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Purely Aerodynamic Rolling]
```

### 4.4 Stability Decision Matrix for KTU Answers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Problem Given] --> nodeB{Identify the Rotating Element}
    nodeB -->|Wheel| nodeC[Two-wheeler or Four-wheeler Stability]
    nodeB -->|Propeller or Engine| nodeD[Ship or Aircraft Stability]
    nodeC --> nodeE{Find Spin and Precession Velocities}
    nodeD --> nodeF{Find Spin and Precession Velocities}
    nodeE --> nodeG[Use C equals IW omegaW omegaP]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Use C equals I omegaS omegaP]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Draw Vector Diagram with Right Hand Rule]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute Magnitude and Identify Direction]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Apply Stability Condition or Solve for Required Quantity]
```

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU answer script, supplement these block diagrams with hand-drawn free-body diagrams showing the three axes (X, Y, Z) and the direction of $\vec{\omega}$, $\vec{\omega_p}$, $\vec{C}_A$, and $\vec{C}_R$. A clear 3D sketch with labelled axes alone fetches 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a gyroscopic couple. Derive its expression.

> **Model Answer:**
> A gyroscopic couple is the reactive couple that arises in a rotating body when the direction of its spin axis is changed. The expression is:
>
> $$C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p$$
>
> where $I$ is the mass moment of inertia of the rotor about the spin axis, $\omega$ is the spin angular velocity, and $\omega_p$ is the precession angular velocity. The spin axis and precession axis are perpendicular.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Why does a ship experience no gyroscopic couple during rolling, even though the propeller is spinning?

> **Model Answer:**
> During rolling, the ship oscillates about its longitudinal axis. The propeller's spin axis is *also* along the longitudinal axis. Since the spin axis and the precession (rolling) axis are parallel, the angle $\theta$ between them is $0°$ (or $180°$). The gyroscopic couple is:
>
> $$C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p \sin\theta = I\,\omega\,\omega_p \sin 0° = 0$$
>
> Hence, the gyroscopic effect is zero, and rolling is countered only by other means like bilge keels.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Paper Adapted]** A four-wheeled car of mass 1500 kg travels at a constant speed of 60 km/h on a curved road of radius 50 m. Each wheel has a moment of inertia of 1.2 kg·m² and a diameter of 0.6 m. The C.G. of the car is 0.5 m above the road and 0.8 m from either side. The wheel track is 1.6 m. Calculate:
**(a)** The gyroscopic couple on the car due to the four wheels, assuming rear-wheel drive. (7 marks)
**(b)** The reaction of the outer wheels and check if the car overturns. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.667$ m/s
- $r = 50$ m
- $I_W = 1.2$ kg·m² (per wheel)
- Diameter of wheel = 0.6 m, so $R = 0.3$ m
- $h = 0.5$ m (C.G. height)
- $b = 1.6$ m (wheel track)

**Part (a) — Gyroscopic Couple**

Wheel spin angular velocity:

$$\omega_W = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{16.667}{0.3} = 55.556 \text{ rad/s}$$

Precession (turning) angular velocity:

$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{16.667}{50} = 0.3333 \text{ rad/s}$$

For a rear-wheel driven car, **all four wheels contribute** in the same direction. Total angular momentum magnitude:

$$H = 4 I_W \omega_W = 4 \times 1.2 \times 55.556 = 266.67 \text{ kg·m}^2/\text{s}$$

Active gyroscopic couple:

$$C = H \cdot \omega_p = 266.67 \times 0.3333 = 88.89 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Magnitude $C \approx 88.9$ N·m. Direction (assume left turn): reactive couple points along the longitudinal axis backward, causing the front to lift and rear to squat.

**Part (b) — Stability Check**

Centrifugal force (outward, at C.G.):

$$F_c = \frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{1500 \times 16.667^2}{50} = \frac{1500 \times 277.78}{50} = 8333.3 \text{ N}$$

Overturning moment about outer wheel contact line (perpendicular to motion, along the track):

$$M_{ov} = F_c \cdot h + C = 8333.3 \times 0.5 + 88.89 = 4166.67 + 88.89 = 4255.56 \text{ N·m}$$

Restoring moment due to weight (per pair of wheels):

$$M_{res} = m g \cdot \frac{b}{2} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times \frac{1.6}{2} = 11772 \text{ N·m}$$

**Comparison:**

$$M_{ov} = 4255.56 \text{ N·m} \;<\; M_{res} = 11772 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $M_{ov} < M_{res}$, the car **does not overturn** — it is stable on the curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note how the gyroscopic couple (88.89 N·m) is small compared to the centrifugal moment (4166.67 N·m) at this speed. The gyroscopic effect becomes dominant only at very high speeds and very tight turns.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Model Paper Adapted]** A ship of mass 20,000 tonnes and radius of gyration 7.5 m is moving at a speed of 30 km/h. The engine runs at 200 rpm. The ship steers to the left in a curve of radius 250 m.
**(a)** Find the active and reactive gyroscopic couples. (7 marks)
**(b)** What is the effect on the ship, and how does the ship counter this couple? (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- $m = 20{,}000$ tonnes $= 20{,}000 \times 1000 = 2 \times 10^7$ kg
- $k = 7.5$ m
- $v = 30 \text{ km/h} = 8.333$ m/s
- $N_S = 200$ rpm, so $\omega_S = \dfrac{2\pi N_S}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = 20.944$ rad/s
- $R_{turn} = 250$ m

**Part (a) — Gyroscopic Couples**

Precession angular velocity (turning):

$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{R_{turn}} = \frac{8.333}{250} = 0.03333 \text{ rad/s}$$

Moment of inertia of the ship (about transverse axis through C.G.):

$$I = m k^2 = 2 \times 10^7 \times 7.5^2 = 2 \times 10^7 \times 56.25 = 1.125 \times 10^9 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

Active gyroscopic couple:

$$C_A = I \cdot \omega_S \cdot \omega_p = 1.125 \times 10^9 \times 20.944 \times 0.03333$$

$$C_A = 7.854 \times 10^8 \text{ N·m} \approx 785.4 \text{ MN·m}$$

Reactive gyroscopic couple (on the ship, from the water resistance on the rudder):

$$C_R = -C_A = -785.4 \text{ MN·m}$$

**Part (b) — Effect and Counter-Measures**

> **Direction (left turn):**
> - $\vec{\omega_S}$ is along the longitudinal axis (forward).
> - $\vec{\omega_p}$ is vertical (upward) for a left turn.
> - $\vec{C}_A = \vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H}$ → points to the **starboard** (right side of the ship).
> - $\vec{C}_R$ → points to the **port** (left side of the ship).

**Effect:** The ship **heels toward the starboard** (outward, away from the centre of the turn). The deck tilts, and the cargo/people may shift.

> **Counter-measures:**
> - **Anti-rolling gyros:** Heavy spinning discs installed below the deck that precess in the opposite direction, generating a counter-couple.
> - **Stabilizer fins (bilge keels):** Hydrodynamic surfaces that generate a righting moment.
> - **Active rudder control (Rudder Roll Reduction):** Modern ships use the rudder itself to apply an oscillating correction.
> - **Anti-heeling tanks:** Liquid transfer between side tanks to counter heel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Sign of $I$:** Some students use $I = m k$ instead of $I = m k^2$ — instant 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Unit of $\omega_S$:** The engine RPM must be converted to rad/s. Direct use of 200 in $C = I \omega \omega_p$ is a 1-mark error.
> 3. **Direction of couple:** A *correct magnitude* without correct *direction* (port vs starboard) is half-marked. Always draw the vector diagram.
> 4. **Rolling ≠ 0 in real ships:** The gyroscopic couple during rolling is *theoretically* zero, but in practice, there is some residual couple from off-axis propeller inertia. Examiners expect you to state the *ideal* answer.
> 5. **Two-wheeler confusion:** Some students confuse the direction of the reactive couple for motorcycles. *Tip:* for a *left* turn on a motorcycle, the active couple points **forward**, and the reactive couple points **rearward** and **outward** (tending to tilt the rider outward — same side as centrifugal force). Memorize the cross-product rule.
> 6. **Four-wheeler cancellation:** Do not blindly sum $4 I_W$. Verify whether the spin directions of front and rear wheels are the same or opposite, and apply the cancellation logic for front-driven cars.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 (Gyroscope)**

- **Core Equation:** $C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p = m k^2 \omega \omega_p$. Always perpendicular, $\theta = 90°$ unless stated.
- **Right-Hand Rule:** Curl fingers along rotation; thumb gives $\vec{\omega}$. Cross product gives $\vec{C}_A$.
- **Active vs Reactive:** $\vec{C}_A$ on the bearings from rotor; $\vec{C}_R$ on the rotor from bearings. Equal in magnitude, opposite in direction.
- **Two-wheeler turn:** Spin axis = lateral (transverse), precession = vertical. Reactive couple tilts the vehicle *outward*. Correction factor: $\left(1 + \dfrac{I_W}{mRh}\right)$.
- **Four-wheeler, rear-driven:** All 4 wheels contribute → $C = 4 I_W \omega_W \omega_p$. Tends to "squat" the rear in a left turn.
- **Four-wheeler, front-driven:** Front and rear wheel angular momenta *oppose* → net $C = 0$ for matched wheels.
- **Ship steering:** Couple $C = m k^2 \omega_S v / R$. Heeling effect.
- **Ship pitching:** Couple $C = m k^2 \omega_S \omega_p \cos\theta$. Nose-up/nose-down oscillation.
- **Ship rolling:** $C = 0$ because spin axis is parallel to roll axis.
- **Aircraft turn (yaw):** Couple causes roll (axis of spin and precession are perpendicular).
- **Aircraft pitch:** Couple causes yaw.
- **Aircraft roll:** $C = 0$ (spin axis is parallel to roll axis).
- **Common Mistake in KTU Papers:** Adding $C$ in the *wrong* direction to the centrifugal moment. Always draw the 3D axes.
- **Unit Safety:** $v$ must be in m/s, $\omega$ in rad/s, $I$ in kg·m². Moment of inertia of a disc: $I = \dfrac{1}{2} m R^2$; of a wheel (treated as a ring or solid disc depending on problem).
- **Sign Convention Used:** Counter-clockwise (CCW) positive when viewed from the tip of the positive axis. Use vector cross product to avoid confusion.
- **Pre-Exam Quick Mnemonic:** *"Perpendicular → couple exists; Parallel → couple vanishes."* Apply this to ship rolling, ship pitching, aircraft rolling, etc.
- **Numerical Trick:** Whenever $r$ is very large (straight road), $\omega_p \to 0$, so $C \to 0$. Whenever $v \to 0$ (stopped), $\omega_W \to 0$, so $C \to 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, the examiner allocates:
> - 2 marks for free-body / vector diagram,
> - 2 marks for identifying directions of $\vec{\omega}$, $\vec{\omega_p}$, $\vec{C}$,
> - 3 marks for the magnitude equation,
> - Remaining marks for substitution and final answer.
> Always reserve time for a clean, labelled diagram — it is the highest mark-to-time ratio in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balancing of Rotating Masses: Single Mass, Several Masses in Same and Different Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Balancing of Rotating Masses: Single Mass, Several Masses in Same and Different Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balancing of Rotating Masses** is the process of designing or modifying a rotating assembly such that the **resultant centrifugal force** and the **resultant couple** acting on the shaft (due to all the rotating masses) become **zero**. This ensures the shaft is free from any time-varying reaction forces during operation, even though the individual masses continue to experience centrifugal acceleration.

When the resultant of the centrifugal forces is zero, the system is said to be in **dynamic balance** (which automatically ensures static balance, but not vice-versa).

Two fundamental conditions govern complete balancing:
- **Static Balance:** The algebraic (vector) sum of the products `m·r` of all the rotating masses, taken about the axis of rotation, must equal **zero**. (No resultant centrifugal force on the shaft.)
- **Dynamic Balance:** The algebraic (vector) sum of the moments (or couples) of the centrifugal forces about any plane perpendicular to the axis of rotation must equal **zero**. (No resultant couple acting on the shaft.)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Washing Machine Tumbler:**
> Imagine spinning a wet towel. If you fold it evenly, it spins smoothly with no shaking of your hand. If you crumple it to one side, you feel a strong rhythmic tug at every revolution — the machine "vibrates." That tug is the **unbalanced centrifugal force**. Balancing is exactly the art of redistributing the masses (or adding counterweights) so that the shaft feels **no tug** at all, even though every molecule inside is still trying to fly outward.

**Another analogy:** A car wheel with a missing balancing weight will develop a wobble at high speed. The mechanic fixes it by sticking a small weight on the opposite side — this is **single-plane static balancing** in action.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Constants

- **Centrifugal Force, F = m · ω² · r** — the radial inertia force generated by a mass `m` rotating at angular velocity `ω` at a radius `r`.
- **Unbalance** — the mass-radius product `m·r` (in kg·m) that is the source of the centrifugal force.
- **Balancing Mass** — an additional mass placed at a suitable radius and angular position to cancel the unbalanced centrifugal force.
- **Couple** — a pair of equal, opposite, parallel forces that cause pure rotation (relevant when masses lie in *different* planes).
- **Standard reference angular velocity: ω = 2πN/60 rad/s**, where N is in rpm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is balancing important in engineering?**
> - Reduces bearing wear and fatigue failure.
> - Prevents foundation vibrations in turbines, compressors, IC engines.
> - Minimises noise and improves passenger comfort in vehicles.
> - Required by ISO 1940 (G 6.3, G 2.5, etc.) for most industrial rotors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Single Rotating Mass

When a single mass `m₁` rotates at radius `r₁` with angular velocity `ω`, it generates a centrifugal force:

$$F_c = m_1 \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_1$$

This force acts radially outward and rotates with the shaft. To **balance** it, a counter-mass `m₂` must be placed on the **opposite side** of the axis at the same angular position but 180° apart, such that its centrifugal force exactly cancels `F_c`:

$$m_2 \cdot r_2 = m_1 \cdot r_1$$

- The system is **automatically statically and dynamically balanced** because both forces lie in the same plane and the couple is zero.
- This is the **simplest balancing case** — used in small fans, toy tops, and as the building block for more complex cases.

### 2.2 Several Masses in the Same Plane

When multiple masses `m₁, m₂, m₃, …, mₙ` rotate in a **single transverse plane** (e.g., all on the same disc), the net unbalanced force is the **vector sum** of their individual centrifugal forces.

Forces act radially outward in the directions specified by their angular positions `θ₁, θ₂, …, θₙ`.

**Balancing condition (Static Equilibrium):**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum F_y = 0$$

In vector form:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot r_i \cdot e^{j\theta_i} = 0$$

where `e^{jθ}` is the complex unit vector representing the radial direction of mass `i`.

Since `ω²` is common to all, it cancels out — so we only need to balance the `m·r` products (the "unbalance vectors") rather than the forces themselves.

**Graphical method — Force Polygon:**
- Draw vectors of length `m·r` at their respective angles.
- The polygon must close (i.e., the resultant must be zero) for the system to be in static balance.
- The closing side of the polygon (drawn in the opposite direction) gives the magnitude and angular position of the **required balancing mass-radius product** `m_b · r_b`.

### 2.3 Several Masses in Different Planes

This is the **most general and KTU-frequently-tested** case. The masses lie in planes separated by axial distances `L₁, L₂, …, Lₙ` along the shaft.

**Two new requirements emerge:**
- The **resultant centrifugal force** must be zero (static balance): this is achieved by placing the balancing masses in a chosen reference plane.
- The **resultant couple** about any transverse plane must be zero (dynamic balance): the moments of the centrifugal forces about the reference plane must sum to zero.

**Standard Convention — Reference Plane Method:**
1. Choose a **reference plane** (usually one of the end planes, e.g., the left bearing plane — designated plane **L**, or the right — plane **R**).
2. All masses are conceptually transferred to the reference plane by replacing each mass `mᵢ` at distance `dᵢ` from the reference plane with a **force** (= mᵢ·rᵢ) AND a **couple** (= mᵢ·rᵢ·dᵢ).
3. The forces are vectorially summed to give the **unbalanced force**, which is balanced by a single mass in the reference plane.
4. The couples are vectorially summed to give the **unbalanced couple**, which is balanced by two equal masses placed in two other planes (or a single mass in a second plane plus reaction at supports).

**Resultant couple (vector form):**

$$\vec{C} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i \cdot r_i \cdot d_i) \cdot e^{j\theta_i}$$

**Graphical Method — Force Polygon and Couple Polygon:**
- **Force Polygon:** Closes the unbalanced force vector → gives balancing mass `m_L` in plane L.
- **Couple Polygon:** Closes the unbalanced couple vector → gives balancing mass `m_R` in plane R.
- The two polygons are drawn on the same set of radial lines (the angular positions of the masses).

### 2.4 Real-world Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Balancing |
|---|---|
| **IC Engines** | Balancing crank-shafts, reciprocating masses converted to equivalent rotating masses. |
| **Steam & Gas Turbines** | Multi-stage turbine discs must be dynamically balanced to micron-level precision. |
| **Pumps & Compressors** | Impellers balanced to ISO G 2.5 to avoid bearing damage. |
| **Automotive** | Wheel balancing, drive-shaft balancing, crankshaft balancing. |
| **Aerospace** | Propellers, helicopter rotors, jet engine compressors. |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in ~70% of balancing problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal force | F = m · ω² · r | N | Force on shaft per mass |
| 2 | Angular velocity | ω = 2πN/60 | rad/s | N in rpm |
| 3 | Balancing of single mass | m₁·r₁ = m₂·r₂ | kg·m | Opposite side, same plane |
| 4 | Static balance (same plane) | Σ mᵢ·rᵢ·cosθᵢ = 0  and  Σ mᵢ·rᵢ·sinθᵢ = 0 | kg·m | Two scalar equations |
| 5 | Resultant unbalance force | F_R = ω² · Σ (mᵢ·rᵢ) | N | Vector sum of mᵢ·rᵢ vectors |
| 6 | Couple (different planes) | C = m·r·d | N·m² (or kg·m²) | d = axial distance from ref. plane |
| 7 | Dynamic balance (couple) | Σ (mᵢ·rᵢ·dᵢ) = 0 | kg·m² | Vector sum of couples = 0 |
| 8 | Balancing mass in plane L | m_L·r_L = (Resultant of mᵢ·rᵢ vectors) | kg·m | From force polygon |
| 9 | Balancing mass in plane R | m_R·r_R = (Resultant of mᵢ·rᵢ·dᵢ vectors) ÷ L_R | kg·m | From couple polygon; L_R is the axial distance between planes L and R |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** If angular positions are not given in a specific direction, assume the **first mass is along the positive x-axis (θ₁ = 0°)** and the rest follow in the **order given in the problem (typically counter-clockwise)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Centrifugal Force for a Single Mass

Consider a point mass `m` attached to a shaft at a radial distance `r` from the axis of rotation, rotating with a constant angular velocity `ω` rad/s.

**Step 1 — Position vector of the mass at any instant `t`:**
The mass is at angular position `θ = ωt` measured from a fixed reference line. Its position in Cartesian coordinates is:

$$x(t) = r \cos(\omega t), \quad y(t) = r \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Acceleration vector (differentiate twice with respect to time):**
The velocity is obtained by differentiating the position vector once:

$$\dot{x}(t) = -r \omega \sin(\omega t), \quad \dot{y}(t) = r \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Differentiating again gives the acceleration components:

$$\ddot{x}(t) = -r \omega^2 \cos(\omega t), \quad \ddot{y}(t) = -r \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of the centripetal acceleration:**
The acceleration vector points radially *inward* (towards the axis). Its magnitude is:

$$a = \sqrt{\ddot{x}^2 + \ddot{y}^2} = \omega^2 r \sqrt{\cos^2(\omega t) + \sin^2(\omega t)} = \omega^2 r$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law to obtain the centrifugal (inertial) force:**
By D'Alembert's principle, the inertia (centrifugal) force opposes the centripetal acceleration and is therefore directed radially *outward*:

$$F_c = m \cdot a = m \omega^2 r$$

The direction of `F_c` is along the radius vector, away from the axis, rotating with the shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** The factor `ω²` ensures that if the speed doubles, the centrifugal force **quadruples**. This is why high-speed rotors (turbines at 10,000+ rpm) demand extremely precise balancing — a 1 g unbalance at 1,000 rpm becomes a 100 g unbalance at 10,000 rpm.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Balancing Conditions for `n` Masses in the Same Plane

Let there be `n` masses `m₁, m₂, …, mₙ` rotating in a single plane at radii `r₁, r₂, …, rₙ` and angular positions `θ₁, θ₂, …, θₙ`.

**Step 1 — Write the centrifugal force vector for the i-th mass:**
Each force is radial, magnitude `F_i = m_i · ω² · r_i`, direction at angle `θᵢ`:

$$\vec{F_i} = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \left[ \cos\theta_i \, \hat{i} + \sin\theta_i \, \hat{j} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Form the resultant force vector:**

$$\vec{F_R} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F_i} = \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \left[ \cos\theta_i \, \hat{i} + \sin\theta_i \, \hat{j} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the balancing condition (`F_R = 0`):**
Since `ω² ≠ 0`, the bracket must vanish:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(x-component)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(y-component)}$$

These are the **two scalar balancing equations** for the same-plane case.

**Step 4 — Determine the balancing mass-radius product `m_b · r_b`:**
After solving, the **resultant unbalance** of the existing masses is a vector of magnitude:

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{\left( \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i \right)^2 + \left( \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i \right)^2}$$

The balancing mass must be placed at angle:

$$\theta_b = \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i} \right) + 180°$$

(The +180° flips the resultant to the opposite side for cancellation.)

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Couple Equation for Masses in Different Planes

Now let the masses `m₁, m₂, …, mₙ` lie in different transverse planes at axial distances `d₁, d₂, …, dₙ` from a chosen **reference plane** (say, the left bearing plane **L**).

**Step 1 — Treat each mass as a force in the reference plane PLUS a couple:**
A centrifugal force acting at axial distance `d` from the reference plane produces a **couple** of magnitude `F · d = (m·ω²·r)·d` about the reference plane. The direction of this couple is the same as the direction of the force (since couples are free vectors).

**Step 2 — Form the resultant couple vector:**

$$\vec{C_R} = \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i d_i \left[ \cos\theta_i \, \hat{i} + \sin\theta_i \, \hat{j} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the dynamic balancing condition (`C_R = 0`):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i d_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(x-couple)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i d_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(y-couple)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the two balancing masses `m_L·r_L` (in plane L) and `m_R·r_R` (in plane R, at axial distance L_R from L):**

**Sub-step 4a — Find `m_L·r_L` from the force polygon:**

The unbalanced force in the reference plane is:

$$\vec{F_{unb}} = \sum m_i r_i \angle \theta_i \quad \text{(vector sum)}$$

The closing side of the force polygon (in the **opposite direction**) gives `m_L·r_L` at angle `θ_L`.

**Sub-step 4b — Find `m_R·r_R` from the couple polygon:**

The unbalanced couple about plane L is:

$$\vec{C_{unb}} = \sum (m_i r_i d_i) \angle \theta_i \quad \text{(vector sum)}$$

The closing side (in the **opposite direction**) gives `C_R = m_R · r_R · L_R` (because the balancing mass in plane R, at distance `L_R` from plane L, creates a couple of magnitude `(m_R·ω²·r_R)·L_R` about plane L).

Therefore:

$$m_R \cdot r_R = \frac{\text{Closing side of couple polygon}}{L_R}$$

The angle of `m_R·r_R` is the angle of the closing side of the couple polygon.

---

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Methodology for Several Masses in Different Planes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this exact 7-step algorithm to solve any KTU problem on this topic.**

| Step | Action | Equation / Tool |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tabulate given data: `m_i`, `r_i`, `θ_i`, `d_i` for each mass. | Table |
| 2 | Compute `m_i·r_i` for every mass (kg·m). | Scalar product |
| 3 | Resolve into x and y: `m_i·r_i·cosθ_i` and `m_i·r_i·sinθ_i`. | Trigonometry |
| 4 | Draw the **force polygon** by laying off the above vectors head-to-tail. The **closing side (reversed)** gives `m_L·r_L` and `θ_L`. | Graphical |
| 5 | Compute `m_i·r_i·d_i` (couple magnitude) for every mass (kg·m²). | Scalar product |
| 6 | Resolve into x and y: `m_i·r_i·d_i·cosθ_i` and `m_i·r_i·d_i·sinθ_i`. | Trigonometry |
| 7 | Draw the **couple polygon** similarly. The **closing side (reversed)** divided by `L_R` gives `m_R·r_R` and `θ_R`. | Graphical |

### 3.5 Worked Example — Several Masses in the Same Plane

**Problem (KTU-style):** Four masses `m₁ = 10 kg`, `m₂ = 20 kg`, `m₃ = 15 kg`, `m₄ = 25 kg` rotate in the same plane at radii `r₁ = 0.2 m`, `r₂ = 0.15 m`, `r₃ = 0.3 m`, `r₄ = 0.1 m`. Their angular positions are `θ₁ = 0°`, `θ₂ = 60°`, `θ₃ = 150°`, `θ₄ = 240°`. Find the magnitude and angular position of the balancing mass if its radius is `0.25 m`.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute `m_i·r_i`:**

$$m_1 r_1 = 10 \times 0.2 = 2.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_2 r_2 = 20 \times 0.15 = 3.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_3 r_3 = 15 \times 0.3 = 4.5 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_4 r_4 = 25 \times 0.1 = 2.5 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve into x and y components:**

| Mass | mᵢrᵢ (kg·m) | θᵢ | x = mᵢrᵢcosθᵢ | y = mᵢrᵢsinθᵢ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 0° | 2.0 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 3.0 | 60° | 1.5 | 2.598 |
| 3 | 4.5 | 150° | -3.897 | 2.25 |
| 4 | 2.5 | 240° | -1.25 | -2.165 |
| **Σ** | | | **-1.647** | **2.683** |

**Step 3 — Compute the resultant unbalance magnitude:**

$$m_r r_r = \sqrt{(-1.647)^2 + (2.683)^2} = \sqrt{2.713 + 7.198} = \sqrt{9.911} = 3.148 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the angle of the resultant:**

$$\theta_r = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2.683}{-1.647}\right)$$

The vector lies in the **second quadrant** (x negative, y positive):

$$\theta_r = 180° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2.683}{1.647}\right) = 180° - 58.46° = 121.54°$$

**Step 5 — Balancing mass is placed 180° opposite:**

$$\theta_b = 121.54° - 180° = -58.46° \equiv 301.54°$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of the balancing mass (given `r_b = 0.25 m`):**

$$m_b = \frac{m_b r_b}{r_b} = \frac{3.148}{0.25} = 12.59 \text{ kg}$$

**Answer:** A balancing mass of **12.59 kg** at radius **0.25 m** placed at angular position **301.54°** (i.e., 58.46° below the positive x-axis) will completely balance the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always state the balancing mass's radius in the answer if it's given, and compute the mass. If radius is not given, leave the answer as `m_b·r_b = 3.148 kg·m` at `301.54°`.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Balancing Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Balancing of Rotating Masses] --> B[Static Balance Only]
    A --> C[Dynamic Balance]
    C --> D[Same Plane]
    C --> E[Different Planes]
    B --> F[Single Mass - Trivial]
    D --> G[Several Masses in Same Plane]
    D --> H[Force Polygon Method]
    E --> I[Several Masses in Different Planes]
    E --> J[Reference Plane Convention]
    I --> K[Force Polygon for F]
    I --> L[Couple Polygon for M]
    K --> M[Balancing mass mL in plane L]
    L --> N[Balancing mass mR in plane R]
    M --> O[Complete Dynamic Balance Achieved]
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Solution Workflow for Multi-Plane Balancing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Tabulate mi, ri, thetai, di] --> P2[Step 2: Compute mi times ri]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Resolve into x and y]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Force Polygon]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Find mLrL and thetL]
    P1 --> P6[Step 6: Compute mi times ri times di]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Resolve couple into x and y]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Couple Polygon]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Divide closing side by LR]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Find mRrR and thetR]
    P5 --> P11[Complete Balance]
    P10 --> P11
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Reference Plane Concept (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHAFT[Rotating Shaft Assembly]
        P1A[Plane A - Mass m1 at d1]
        P1B[Plane B - Mass m2 at d2]
        P1C[Plane C - Mass m3 at d3]
        P1L[Plane L - Balancing mass mL]
        P1R[Plane R - Balancing mass mR]
    end
    REF[Reference Plane L is chosen]
    REF -. defines d1, d2, d3 .-> SHAFT
    LRdist[Axial distance L_R between L and R]
    LRdist -. couples .-> P1R
```

### 4.4 Force Polygon & Couple Polygon — Schematic Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Pick a scale, e.g., 1 cm = 1 kg m] --> S1[Draw vector m1r1 at theta1]
    S1 --> S2[From tip of m1r1, draw m2r2 at theta2]
    S2 --> S3[Continue for m3r3, m4r4, ...]
    S3 --> S4[Closing side OA drawn from tail of m1r1 to tip of last vector]
    S4 --> S5[Balancing mLrL = OA in length, angle = angle of OA plus 180]
    S5 --> S6END[Force Polygon Complete]
    S0 --> T1[Separately, draw m1r1d1 at theta1]
    T1 --> T2[Continue for all mi ri di]
    T2 --> T3[Closing side OB reversed = mR rR times LR]
    T3 --> T4END[Couple Polygon Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid and not physical drawings?** Mermaid renders crisply on every device and prints perfectly in PDFs — which is exactly what KTU students need for last-minute revision. For the actual graphical force/couple polygon in your exam, you must draw it neatly on graph paper using a compass and protractor; the procedure is what the algorithm above specifies.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define static and dynamic balancing of a rotating mass system. State the necessary conditions for each.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> *Static balancing* of a rotating mass system is the condition in which the **algebraic sum of the products `m·r` of all the rotating masses equals zero**, so that no net centrifugal force acts on the shaft when the system is at rest. Mathematically:
>
> $$\sum m_i r_i = 0 \quad \text{(vector sum)}$$
>
> *Dynamic balancing* is the additional condition in which the **algebraic sum of the moments of the centrifugal forces about any plane perpendicular to the axis of rotation also equals zero**, so that no resultant couple acts on the shaft during rotation. Mathematically:
>
> $$\sum m_i r_i d_i = 0 \quad \text{(vector sum)}$$
>
> A dynamically balanced system is automatically statically balanced, but a statically balanced system may not be dynamically balanced. **(3 marks)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] A single mass of 12 kg is rotating at a radius of 0.2 m. Find the magnitude and position of the balancing mass if its radius of rotation is 0.15 m.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Given: `m₁ = 12 kg`, `r₁ = 0.2 m`, `r₂ = 0.15 m`.
> For balancing, the balancing mass `m₂` must be placed on the **opposite side** of the axis, 180° from the first mass.
>
> $$m_1 r_1 = m_2 r_2$$
>
> $$12 \times 0.2 = m_2 \times 0.15$$
>
> $$m_2 = \frac{2.4}{0.15} = 16 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Balancing mass = 16 kg, placed diametrically opposite the 12 kg mass.** **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Four masses `m₁, m₂, m₃, m₄` of `20 kg, 30 kg, 40 kg, 35 kg` respectively rotate in **different planes** of a shaft. The respective radii are `0.2 m, 0.3 m, 0.25 m, 0.4 m` and the angular positions are `0°, 90°, 180°, 270°`. The axial distances of their planes from the plane of `m₁` are `0, 0.2 m, 0.4 m, 0.6 m` respectively. Find the magnitudes and angular positions of the balancing masses in the planes of `m₁` and `m₃` if their radii of rotation are `0.3 m`. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Data tabulation:**
>
> | Mass | mᵢ (kg) | rᵢ (m) | mᵢrᵢ (kg·m) | θᵢ (°) | dᵢ (m) | mᵢrᵢdᵢ (kg·m²) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 20 | 0.2 | 4.0 | 0 | 0 | 0.0 |
> | 2 | 30 | 0.3 | 9.0 | 90 | 0.2 | 1.80 |
> | 3 | 40 | 0.25 | 10.0 | 180 | 0.4 | 4.00 |
> | 4 | 35 | 0.4 | 14.0 | 270 | 0.6 | 8.40 |
>
> **Step 2 — Force polygon (resolve into x and y):**
>
> | Mass | mᵢrᵢ | cosθᵢ | sinθᵢ | x = mᵢrᵢcosθᵢ | y = mᵢrᵢsinθᵢ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 4.0 | 1 | 0 | 4.00 | 0.00 |
> | 2 | 9.0 | 0 | 1 | 0.00 | 9.00 |
> | 3 | 10.0 | -1 | 0 | -10.00 | 0.00 |
> | 4 | 14.0 | 0 | -1 | 0.00 | -14.00 |
> | **Σ** | | | | **-6.00** | **-5.00** |
>
> **Step 3 — Resultant unbalance force:**
>
> $$\text{Magnitude: } m_L r_L = \sqrt{(-6.0)^2 + (-5.0)^2} = \sqrt{61} = 7.81 \text{ kg·m}$$
>
> $$\theta_L = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-5.0}{-6.0}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.833) = 39.8° \text{ (third quadrant, since both } x \text{ and } y \text{ are negative)}$$
>
> $$\theta_L = 180° + 39.8° = 219.8° \text{ (vector direction)}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Closing side of force polygon (= balancing m·r in plane of m₁):**
>
> The balancing vector is **opposite** to the resultant:
>
> $$\theta_b = 219.8° - 180° = 39.8°$$
>
> $$m_L = \frac{7.81}{0.3} = 26.03 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Balancing mass in plane of m₁ = 26.03 kg at 39.8° from +x-axis, with radius 0.3 m.**
>
> ---
>
> **Step 5 — Couple polygon (resolve mᵢrᵢdᵢ into x and y):**
>
> | Mass | mᵢrᵢdᵢ | cosθᵢ | sinθᵢ | x = mᵢrᵢdᵢcosθᵢ | y = mᵢrᵢdᵢsinθᵢ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 0.0 | 1 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
> | 2 | 1.8 | 0 | 1 | 0.00 | 1.80 |
> | 3 | 4.0 | -1 | 0 | -4.00 | 0.00 |
> | 4 | 8.4 | 0 | -1 | 0.00 | -8.40 |
> | **Σ** | | | | **-4.00** | **-6.60** |
>
> **Step 6 — Resultant unbalance couple:**
>
> $$\text{Magnitude: } C_R = \sqrt{(-4.0)^2 + (-6.6)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 43.56} = \sqrt{59.56} = 7.72 \text{ kg·m}^2$$
>
> $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-6.6}{-4.0}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.65) = 58.8° \text{ (third quadrant)}$$
>
> $$\phi = 180° + 58.8° = 238.8° \text{ (vector direction of unbalanced couple)}$$
>
> **Step 7 — Closing side of couple polygon (= balancing couple about plane of m₁):**
>
> Axial distance between plane of m₁ and plane of m₃: `L_R = 0.4 m`.
>
> $$m_R r_R = \frac{7.72}{0.4} = 19.30 \text{ kg·m}$$
>
> $$\theta_R = 238.8° - 180° = 58.8°$$
>
> $$m_R = \frac{19.30}{0.3} = 64.33 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Balancing mass in plane of m₃ = 64.33 kg at 58.8° from +x-axis, with radius 0.3 m.**
>
> ---
>
> **Final Answer (7 marks):**
> - **Plane of m₁:** `m_L = 26.03 kg` at `θ_L = 39.8°`, `r_L = 0.3 m`
> - **Plane of m₃:** `m_R = 64.33 kg` at `θ_R = 58.8°`, `r_R = 0.3 m`
> - The system is now **completely dynamically balanced**.

---

> **(b)** State and explain with a neat sketch the conditions for complete balancing of several masses rotating in **different planes** of a shaft. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (Diagram + Theory):**
>
> Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.3 for the schematic.
>
> **Conditions for complete (dynamic) balance of `n` masses in different planes:**
>
> 1. **Force condition (Static balance):** The vector sum of the centrifugal forces of all masses (including the balancing masses) must equal zero:
>
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \vec{\omega}^2 r_i = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i = 0 \text{ (vector sum)}$$
>
> 2. **Couple condition (Dynamic balance):** The vector sum of the couples produced by the centrifugal forces about any transverse reference plane must equal zero:
>
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i d_i = 0 \text{ (vector sum, about reference plane)}$$
>
> **Procedure:**
> - Choose a reference plane (say, the left bearing plane L).
> - Resolve each mass `mᵢ·rᵢ` into x and y components.
> - Draw the **force polygon**: the closing side (reversed) gives the balancing mass `m_L·r_L` in plane L.
> - Compute each couple magnitude `mᵢ·rᵢ·dᵢ` and resolve into x and y.
> - Draw the **couple polygon**: the closing side (reversed) gives the balancing couple, from which the second balancing mass `m_R·r_R` in plane R is obtained by dividing by the axial distance `L_R` between planes L and R.
>
> The two balancing masses together ensure complete dynamic balance of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Three masses `m₁ = 18 kg`, `m₂ = 25 kg`, `m₃ = 12 kg` rotate in the **same plane** of a shaft at radii `0.25 m, 0.3 m, 0.4 m` respectively. The angular positions are `0°, 120°, 240°` respectively. Determine the magnitude and position of the balancing mass if its radius is `0.5 m`. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Compute `mᵢ·rᵢ`:**
>
> | Mass | mᵢ (kg) | rᵢ (m) | mᵢrᵢ (kg·m) | θᵢ (°) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 18 | 0.25 | 4.50 | 0 |
> | 2 | 25 | 0.30 | 7.50 | 120 |
> | 3 | 12 | 0.40 | 4.80 | 240 |
>
> **Step 2 — Resolve into x and y:**
>
> | Mass | mᵢrᵢ | cosθᵢ | sinθᵢ | x | y |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 4.50 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 4.500 | 0.000 |
> | 2 | 7.50 | -0.500 | 0.866 | -3.750 | 6.495 |
> | 3 | 4.80 | -0.500 | -0.866 | -2.400 | -4.157 |
> | **Σ** | | | | **-1.650** | **2.338** |
>
> **Step 3 — Resultant unbalance vector:**
>
> $$m_r r_r = \sqrt{(-1.650)^2 + (2.338)^2} = \sqrt{2.722 + 5.466} = \sqrt{8.188} = 2.861 \text{ kg·m}$$
>
> $$\theta_r = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2.338}{-1.650}\right) = 180° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2.338}{1.650}\right) = 180° - 54.79° = 125.21°$$
>
> (Second quadrant, x negative, y positive.)
>
> **Step 4 — Balancing mass (opposite to resultant):**
>
> $$\theta_b = 125.21° - 180° = -54.79° \equiv 305.21°$$
>
> $$m_b = \frac{2.861}{0.5} = 5.72 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Answer (7 marks):** Balancing mass = **5.72 kg** at radius **0.5 m** placed at angular position **305.21°** (i.e., 54.79° clockwise from the +x-axis, which is the angular position of `m₁`).

---

> **(b)** The rotor of a turbocharger has the following data:
>
> | Plane | A | B | C |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Mass (kg) | 4 | 6 | 5 |
> | Radius (m) | 0.12 | 0.18 | 0.15 |
> | Angular position (°) | 0 | 90 | 200 |
> | Distance from plane A (m) | 0 | 0.08 | 0.20 |
>
> The balancing masses are to be placed in planes A and C. If the radii of the balancing masses are 0.15 m and 0.18 m respectively, find their magnitudes and angular positions. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Data table:**
>
> | Mass | mᵢ (kg) | rᵢ (m) | mᵢrᵢ (kg·m) | θᵢ (°) | dᵢ (m) | mᵢrᵢdᵢ (kg·m²) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | A | 4 | 0.12 | 0.48 | 0 | 0 | 0.000 |
> | B | 6 | 0.18 | 1.08 | 90 | 0.08 | 0.0864 |
> | C | 5 | 0.15 | 0.75 | 200 | 0.20 | 0.1500 |
>
> **Step 2 — Force polygon (resolve mᵢrᵢ):**
>
> | Mass | mᵢrᵢ | cosθᵢ | sinθᵢ | x | y |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | A | 0.48 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 0.480 | 0.000 |
> | B | 1.08 | 0.000 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 1.080 |
> | C | 0.75 | -0.940 | -0.342 | -0.705 | -0.257 |
> | **Σ** | | | | **-0.225** | **0.823** |
>
> **Step 3 — Balancing mass in plane A:**
>
> $$m_A r_A = \sqrt{(-0.225)^2 + (0.823)^2} = \sqrt{0.0506 + 0.6773} = \sqrt{0.7279} = 0.853 \text{ kg·m}$$
>
> $$\theta_A = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.823}{-0.225}\right) = 180° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.823}{0.225}\right) = 180° - 74.71° = 105.29°$$
>
> (Second quadrant, since x negative, y positive.)
>
> $$\theta_{bal,A} = 105.29° - 180° = -74.71° \equiv 285.29°$$
>
> $$m_A = \frac{0.853}{0.15} = 5.69 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Couple polygon (resolve mᵢrᵢdᵢ):**
>
> | Mass | mᵢrᵢdᵢ | cosθᵢ | sinθᵢ | x | y |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | A | 0.000 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
> | B | 0.0864 | 0.000 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 0.0864 |
> | C | 0.1500 | -0.940 | -0.342 | -0.1410 | -0.0513 |
> | **Σ** | | | | **-0.1410** | **0.0351** |
>
> **Step 5 — Balancing mass in plane C:**
>
> Axial distance between A and C: `L_R = 0.20 m`.
>
> $$m_C r_C \cdot L_R = \sqrt{(-0.1410)^2 + (0.0351)^2} = \sqrt{0.0199 + 0.0012} = \sqrt{0.0211} = 0.1453 \text{ kg·m}^2$$
>
> $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.0351}{-0.1410}\right) = 180° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.0351}{0.1410}\right) = 180° - 14.0° = 166.0°$$
>
> $$\theta_{bal,C} = 166.0° - 180° = -14.0° \equiv 346.0°$$
>
> $$m_C = \frac{0.1453}{0.20 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.1453}{0.036} = 4.04 \text{ kg}$$
>
> **Final Answer (7 marks):**
> - **Plane A:** `m_A = 5.69 kg` at `θ_A = 285.29°`, `r_A = 0.15 m`
> - **Plane C:** `m_C = 4.04 kg` at `θ_C = 346.0°`, `r_C = 0.18 m`
> - The system is now **completely dynamically balanced**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations:**
>
