> 1. **Forgetting to add 180° to the balancing mass angle.** Students often report the angle of the *resultant* unbalance (e.g., 125.21°) as the answer. The balancing mass must be placed **opposite** to the resultant. Always subtract 180° (or add 180° if it gives a positive angle).
>
> 2. **Not converting degrees to radians in trigonometric functions.** Use calculator in **degree mode**. Trigonometric values like `cos 200°` are negative — be alert.
>
> 3. **Wrong sign on the couple's axial distance.** The distance `dᵢ` from the reference plane is **always positive** (it's a magnitude). Only the angular position carries the sign/direction.
>
> 4. **Dividing couple by `L_R` is forgotten.** The closing side of the couple polygon gives `m_R · r_R · L_R`, not `m_R · r_R`. Many students stop at this step and lose 2-3 marks.
>
> 5. **Same-plane case wrongly treated as different-plane case (or vice-versa).** Re-read the problem statement — if all axial distances are zero, the masses are in the same plane, and you only need the force polygon.
>
> 6. **Not stating the units.** KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units like `kg·m`, `kg·m²`, or `°`. Always include them.
>
> 7. **Assuming the angular position of `m₁` is something other than 0°.** By KTU convention, the first mass is along the +x-axis unless stated otherwise.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for last-minute preparation:**

- **Centrifugal force** on a rotating mass: `F = m·ω²·r`. It is radial, outward, and rotates with the shaft.
- **Single mass** is balanced by an equal `m·r` product placed 180° opposite in the same plane.
- **Static balance (same plane):** `Σ mᵢrᵢcosθᵢ = 0` and `Σ mᵢrᵢsinθᵢ = 0` (two scalar equations; or single vector equation).
- **Dynamic balance (different planes):** `Σ mᵢrᵢdᵢcosθᵢ = 0` and `Σ mᵢrᵢdᵢsinθᵢ = 0` (two additional scalar equations; or single vector equation).
- **Reference plane convention:** Choose a reference plane (L). All masses transfer to it as `mᵢrᵢ` (force) + `mᵢrᵢdᵢ` (couple). Balancing mass `m_L` in plane L cancels the **force**. Balancing mass `m_R` in plane R (at distance `L_R` from L) cancels the **couple**.
- **Force polygon** is drawn first; its closing side (reversed) gives the balancing `m_L·r_L` and `θ_L`.
- **Couple polygon** is drawn next; its closing side (reversed) divided by `L_R` gives `m_R·r_R` and `θ_R`.
- **ω² cancels out** from the balancing equations — you only balance `m·r` (and `m·r·d` for couples), not the forces themselves. So balancing is **speed-independent** for a given geometry.
- **Dynamic balance ⇒ Static balance**, but **Static balance ⇏ Dynamic balance**.
- **Quadrant check:** If `Σx < 0` and `Σy > 0`, the resultant vector is in the second quadrant — compute the angle as `180° − tan⁻¹(|y|/|x|)`.
- **Units to remember:** `m·r` in `kg·m`, `m·r·d` in `kg·m²`, angle in `degrees (°)`, angular velocity `ω` in `rad/s`.
- **Graphical method** uses force and couple polygons; **analytical method** uses component sums (as shown in the worked examples).
- **KTU 2024 typical marks split:** Same-plane problem = 3-7 marks; Different-planes problem = 7-14 marks; Theory = 3-7 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balancing of Reciprocating Masses, Partial Balancing of Single Cylinder and Multi-Cylinder Inline Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Balancing of Reciprocating Masses, Partial Balancing of Single Cylinder and Multi-Cylinder Inline Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Balancing of Reciprocating Masses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balancing of Reciprocating Masses** is defined as the process of eliminating or minimizing the dynamic inertia forces and moments generated by the reciprocating components (piston, connecting rod, etc.) of an engine or machine, by suitably distributing the masses, counterweights, or by adjusting the crank angular positions, so that the machine operates with minimum vibration and smooth running.

In a slider-crank mechanism, the reciprocating parts (piston, wrist pin, and a portion of the connecting rod) undergo **simple harmonic motion** with high acceleration. By Newton's second law, this acceleration produces **inertia forces** that vary in magnitude and direction throughout each engine cycle. If these forces are not balanced, they cause:

1. **Vibrations** in the engine and its supporting structure
2. **Forces on bearings** leading to wear and bearing failure
3. **Noise and operator discomfort**
4. **Reduced fatigue life** of components

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Partial" Balancing?**
> In a single-cylinder engine, it is **impossible** to completely balance all inertia forces throughout the entire cycle. Engineers can only achieve a compromise — typically balancing the **primary force** (which is larger and has lower frequency) at the cost of increasing the **secondary force**. This compromise is termed **Partial Balancing**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are shaking a heavy bucket of water up and down with your right hand. Your hand experiences a large reaction force — the bucket pushes back. Now if you hold a similar bucket in your left hand and shake it **in the opposite direction at the same time**, the forces cancel each other, and you barely feel any push.

That is exactly how **dynamic balancing** works in engines:
- Each piston creates a "shaking force" (inertia force) along the cylinder axis.
- By placing another piston or rotating mass on the **opposite side** and timing it correctly, we can cancel the shake.

> [!NOTE]
> A washing machine with clothes unevenly distributed vibrates heavily during the spin cycle because of **unbalanced reciprocating rotation**. Modern washing machines have counter-rotating dampers for the same reason — an everyday example of balancing.

## 1.3 Primary vs Secondary Inertia Forces

The **inertia force** of a reciprocating mass in a slider-crank mechanism is mathematically expressed as:

$$F_i = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right]$$

This expression contains two distinct harmonic components:

| Component | Mathematical Term | Frequency | Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Force** ($F_P$) | $m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | **1 × engine speed** | Crank rotation |
| **Secondary Force** ($F_S$) | $m \cdot r^2 \cdot \omega^2 / L \cdot \cos 2\theta$ | **2 × engine speed** | Connecting rod angularity |

Where:
- $m$ = mass of reciprocating parts (kg)
- $r$ = crank radius (m)
- $L$ = connecting rod length (m)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of crank (rad/s)
- $\theta$ = crank angle from TDC (degrees)

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU 2024)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Threshold of human perception for vibration:** ~0.01 g acceleration
> - **Standard N-ratio:** $N = L/r$ (typically **3.5 to 4** for I.C. engines)
> - **Primary to Secondary ratio:** For $N = 4$, secondary force ≈ **25% of primary force**
> - **Balancing quality grades:** ISO 1940 (G0.4 to G4000) — used for rotating masses

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Slider-Crank Mechanism — Inertia Force Analysis

Consider a slider-crank mechanism with:
- **O** = crank center (fixed pivot)
- **Crank** = $r$ (rotates at $\omega$)
- **Connecting rod** = $L$
- **Piston** = reciprocating mass $m$
- **$\theta$** = crank angle measured from **Inner Dead Center (IDC)** or **Top Dead Center (TDC)**

### Step-by-Step Logic of Piston Motion

1. **Crank Position**: The crank pin $A$ lies at angle $\theta$ from IDC.
2. **Piston Displacement** $x$ (from TDC): The horizontal distance from $A$ to the wrist pin $B$ is $L$.
3. **Geometric relation**: $x = r \cos\theta + L \cos\phi$, where $\sin\phi = (r/L) \sin\theta$.
4. **Approximation** for $L >> r$: $\cos\phi \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta$.
5. **Final displacement**: $x \approx r \cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{2L}\sin^2\theta = r\cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{4L}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$.
6. **Acceleration** = $\ddot{x} = -r\omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right]$.
7. **Inertia Force**: $F_i = -m\ddot{x} = m r \omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two frequencies?**
> The **primary** component ($\cos\theta$) oscillates once per revolution, while the **secondary** component ($\cos 2\theta$) oscillates twice per revolution. The secondary is smaller by factor $r/L$ but its frequency is higher, so it causes more irritation to the human ear.

## 2.2 Partial Balancing of a Single-Cylinder Engine

In a single-cylinder engine, the **rotating mass** ($m_r$ at radius $r$) and **reciprocating mass** ($m_{rec}$ at radius $r$) coexist. The rotating part creates a **centrifugal force** that is constant in magnitude and rotating with the crank.

### The Concept of Partial Balance Ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fraction $x$ of the primary force is balanced by adding a **counterweight** on the opposite side of the crank. The fraction $x$ is called the **balance ratio**. The remaining $(1-x)$ of the primary force appears as an unbalanced primary force.

If $m_c$ is the counterweight mass at radius $r_c$:

$$m_c \cdot r_c \cdot \omega^2 = x \cdot m_{rec} \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

$$\boxed{m_c r_c = x \cdot m_{rec} \cdot r}$$

### Unbalanced Forces After Partial Balance

| Force | Expression | Magnitude Relative to Original |
|---|---|---|
| **Unbalanced Primary** | $F_P' = (1 - x) \cdot m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | $(1 - x) \times$ original primary |
| **Unbalanced Secondary** | $F_S' = m \cdot r^2 \cdot \omega^2 / L$ | **Unchanged** (cannot be balanced by single rotating mass) |
| **Residual Vertical** | $F_V = F_S' \cos 2\theta$ | Varies twice per rev |
| **Hammer Blow** (Lurching) | $F_{HB} = m_c r_c \omega^2 - x m r \omega^2$ | Along line of stroke |
| **Swaying Couple** | $C_S = F_P' \cdot h$ | Variable once per rev |

> [!WARNING]
> **Hammer Blow** is the **direct force on the foundation/rails** caused by the rotating counterweight. Reducing the unbalanced primary force ($x$ closer to 1) **increases** the hammer blow on the foundation. This is a critical trade-off in locomotive balancing.

## 2.3 Multi-Cylinder Inline Engine Balancing

A multi-cylinder inline engine has 2, 3, 4, 6, or more cylinders in a line. The crank throws can be arranged at different angles to achieve cancellation of inertia forces and couples.

### 2.3.1 Two-Cylinder Inline Engine

- **Cylinders:** 2, with crank throws at **180° apart** (in-line configuration).
- **Phase difference** between piston motions: **180°** (one at TDC, other at BDC).
- **Primary forces cancel** (because $\cos\theta$ at $\theta$ and $\cos(180° + \theta) = -\cos\theta$).
- **Secondary forces cancel** (similar logic for $\cos 2\theta$).
- **Result:** Perfect primary and secondary force balance, but a **rocking couple** remains.

### 2.3.2 Three-Cylinder Inline Engine (Crank at 120°)

Three cranks at **120° apart**:
- Primary forces at angles $\theta$, $\theta + 120°$, $\theta + 240°$.
- Vector sum: $\cos\theta + \cos(\theta + 120°) + \cos(\theta + 240°) = 0$. ✓
- Primary couple: must be checked using perpendicular components.

### 2.3.3 Four-Cylinder Inline Engine (Two Cranks at 180°)

- Cranks 1 & 4 at angle $0°$ and $180°$ (outer pair).
- Cranks 2 & 3 at $180°$ and $0°$ (inner pair, cross-plane).
- Both primary and secondary forces **completely balance** in a 4-cylinder inline 4-stroke engine.

### 2.3.4 Six-Cylinder Inline Engine

Cranks arranged symmetrically (typically $0°, 60°, 60°, 0°, 60°, 60°$ or $0°, 120°, 240°, 180°, 300°, 60°$). Perfect primary, secondary, and couple balance.

## 2.4 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **canonical formulas** that KTU expects students to memorize and apply.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Piston displacement | $x = r\cos\theta + \dfrac{r^2}{2L}\sin^2\theta$ | From TDC |
| 2 | Piston acceleration | $a = r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right]$ | Directed toward crank center |
| 3 | Primary inertia force | $F_P = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | Frequency: 1/rev |
| 4 | Secondary inertia force | $F_S = \dfrac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L} \cos 2\theta$ | Frequency: 2/rev |
| 5 | Total inertia force | $F_i = F_P + F_S$ | Algebraic sum along line of stroke |
| 6 | Counterweight required (Partial balance) | $m_c r_c = x \cdot m_{rec} \cdot r$ | For balance ratio $x$ |
| 7 | Unbalanced primary (after partial) | $F_P' = (1-x) m r \omega^2$ | Swaying force |
| 8 | Hammer blow (Locomotive) | $F_{HB} = (m_c r_c - m_{rec} r) \omega^2$ | Vertical force on rails |
| 9 | Swaying couple | $C_S = F_P' \cdot h$ | $h$ = height of CG above rail |
| 10 | Multi-cylinder primary sum (vector) | $\sum F_P = m r \omega^2 \sum \cos\theta_i$ | At $\omega t = 0$ |
| 11 | Multi-cylinder secondary sum | $\sum F_S = \dfrac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L} \sum \cos 2\theta_i$ | For 2nd harmonic |
| 12 | Couple per cylinder | $C_i = F_i \cdot a_i$ | $a_i$ = distance from reference plane |
| 13 | Resultant couple | $C_R = \sqrt{(\sum C_i \cos\theta_i)^2 + (\sum C_i \sin\theta_i)^2}$ | At $90°$ from crank |
| 14 | Crank angle for max couple | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{\sum C_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum C_i \cos\theta_i}$ | Direction of couple |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engines (4-cyl, 6-cyl):** The primary objective is to make the engine "smooth" so passengers don't feel vibration. The cranks are arranged at specific angles (e.g., $0-180-180-0$ for inline-4) to cancel primary and secondary forces.
- **Locomotives (single or 2-cylinder):** Cannot be fully balanced; hence **hammer blow** is a critical design constraint. Two-cylinder locomotives balance primary forces but generate a heavy **swaying couple**.
- **Radial aircraft engines (5, 7, 9 cylinders):** Odd numbers of cylinders are used so that firing intervals are uniform; the cylinders are placed radially to provide perfect moment balance.
- **V-engines (V6, V8):** Used when space is limited; the V-angle (typically 60°, 90°) cancels primary couples.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Inertia Force in Slider-Crank Mechanism

Let us derive the inertia force of the reciprocating parts from first principles, following KTU 2024 board-style reasoning.

### Given:
- Crank radius = $r$, rotating at $\omega$ rad/s
- Connecting rod length = $L$
- Mass of reciprocating parts = $m$
- $\theta$ = crank angle from TDC
- $\phi$ = angle of connecting rod with line of stroke

### Step 1: Geometric Position of Piston

The piston position $x$ from crank center $O$ is:

$$x = r\cos\theta + L\cos\phi$$

This is the sum of the horizontal projection of crank and connecting rod.

### Step 2: Connecting Rod Angle (Approximation)

From triangle $OAB$ (right triangle approximation):

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{L}\sin\theta$$

For small $\phi$ (since $L >> r$): $\cos\phi \approx 1 - \dfrac{\phi^2}{2}$ and $\phi \approx \dfrac{r}{L}\sin\theta$.

Therefore:

$$\cos\phi \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Position Equation

$$x = r\cos\theta + L\left[1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta\right]$$

$$x = r\cos\theta + L - \frac{r^2}{2L}\sin^2\theta$$

We can ignore the constant $L$ (offset from origin) for displacement analysis. The variable part is:

$$x = r\cos\theta - \frac{r^2}{2L}\sin^2\theta$$

### Step 4: Use Trigonometric Identity

Since $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$x = r\cos\theta - \frac{r^2}{2L} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$$

$$x = r\cos\theta - \frac{r^2}{4L} + \frac{r^2}{4L}\cos 2\theta$$

### Step 5: Differentiate Twice to Get Acceleration

First derivative (velocity):

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega}{2L}\sin 2\theta$$

Second derivative (acceleration):

$$a = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - \frac{r^2\omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$$

$$\boxed{a = -r\omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right]}$$

The **negative sign** indicates the acceleration is directed from the piston toward the crank center (i.e., the piston is decelerating as it moves away from TDC).

### Step 6: Apply Newton's Second Law

Inertia force on the piston (opposing acceleration):

$$F_i = -m \cdot a = m r \omega^2\left[\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right]$$

$$\boxed{F_i = \underbrace{m r \omega^2 \cos\theta}_{F_P \text{ (Primary)}} + \underbrace{\frac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta}_{F_S \text{ (Secondary)}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The convention in Indian textbooks (Khurmi, Shigley) is to take inertia force as **positive outward** (from crank to piston). The student should write the equation with **positive sign** and the **sense of direction** specified separately.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Multi-Cylinder Force & Couple Calculations

For an **inline multi-cylinder engine** with $n$ cylinders, all with identical reciprocating mass $m$ and crank radius $r$, with cylinder spacing $a$ between successive cylinders.

### 3.2.1 Setup Convention

Place cylinder **No. 1 at the reference plane**. Let crank angles be $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \ldots, \alpha_n$ (measured from a common reference, e.g., crank No. 1 direction).

For an engine rotating at $\omega$, the **dynamic state** of cylinder $i$ is captured by replacing $\theta_i = \omega t + \alpha_i$.

### 3.2.2 Primary Force Components (Per Cylinder)

Per cylinder primary force: $F_{P,i} = m r \omega^2 \cos(\omega t + \alpha_i)$.

Resolve at $\omega t = 0$ (a snapshot, since forces are sinusoidal):

$$F_{P,i,x} = m r \omega^2 \cos\alpha_i$$
$$F_{P,i,y} = m r \omega^2 \sin\alpha_i$$

Summing over all cylinders:

$$\sum F_{P,x} = m r \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \cos\alpha_i$$
$$\sum F_{P,y} = m r \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sin\alpha_i$$

Resultant magnitude:

$$F_P^{net} = m r \omega^2 \sqrt{\left(\sum \cos\alpha_i\right)^2 + \left(\sum \sin\alpha_i\right)^2}$$

### 3.2.3 Primary Couple Components

Couple of cylinder $i$ about reference plane = $F_{P,i} \times a_i$ (perpendicular to force).

For an inline engine (all forces along the cylinder axis, i.e., along x-axis), the moment arm in the x-direction is the y-position (or perpendicular distance). If cylinders are along x-axis with positions $a_i$ along x, then the couple about a transverse axis is:

$$C_{P,i} = F_{P,i} \cdot a_i \cdot \sin(\text{angle between force and crank arm})$$

For an inline engine with cranks lying in the same plane, the **rocking couple** at $\omega t$ is:

$$C_P(\omega t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{P,i}(\omega t) \cdot a_i = m r \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \cos(\omega t + \alpha_i)$$

Resolving at $\omega t = 0$:

$$C_P(0) = m r \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \cos\alpha_i$$

For symmetry, if cylinder positions are $\pm a, \pm 2a, \ldots$, the **net couple is zero** when $\sum a_i \cos\alpha_i = 0$.

### 3.2.4 Secondary Force & Couple

Identical procedure but with $2\theta_i = 2(\omega t + \alpha_i)$:

$$\sum F_{S,x} = \frac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\sum \cos 2\alpha_i$$
$$\sum F_{S,y} = \frac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\sum \sin 2\alpha_i$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Three-Cylinder Inline Engine (Crank 120°)

**Given:** 3 cylinders, crank angles $0°, 120°, 240°$, all with $m, r, L$ identical, spacing $a$ between consecutive cylinders.

### Step 1: Primary Force Sum

At $\omega t = 0$:

$$\sum \cos\alpha_i = \cos 0° + \cos 120° + \cos 240° = 1 - 0.5 - 0.5 = 0$$
$$\sum \sin\alpha_i = \sin 0° + \sin 120° + \sin 240° = 0 + 0.866 - 0.866 = 0$$

$$\therefore F_P^{net} = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Primary forces balance)}$$

### Step 2: Secondary Force Sum

$$\sum \cos 2\alpha_i = \cos 0° + \cos 240° + \cos 480° = 1 - 0.5 - 0.5 = 0$$
$$\sum \sin 2\alpha_i = \sin 0° + \sin 240° + \sin 480° = 0 - 0.866 + 0.866 = 0$$

$$\therefore F_S^{net} = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Secondary forces balance)}$$

### Step 3: Primary Couple

Positions: $a_1 = 0, a_2 = a, a_3 = 2a$ (taking cylinder 1 as reference).

$$\sum a_i \cos\alpha_i = 0 \cdot 1 + a \cdot \cos 120° + 2a \cdot \cos 240° = 0 - 0.5a - a = -1.5a$$

**Result:** $C_P \neq 0$ → There is an **unbalanced primary couple** of magnitude $1.5 \cdot m r \omega^2 \cdot a$.

### Step 4: Secondary Couple

$$\sum a_i \cos 2\alpha_i = 0 \cdot 1 + a \cdot \cos 240° + 2a \cdot \cos 480° = 0 - 0.5a - a = -1.5a$$

Also **unbalanced**. So a 3-cylinder inline engine has **balanced forces** but **unbalanced couples** for both primary and secondary.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** This is why 3-cylinder engines are popular in compact cars (BMW i3, Toyota) — they have excellent force balance and a smooth firing interval (120°), but they exhibit a noticeable secondary "shimmy" at low RPM, which manufacturers mitigate with balance shafts.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Table — Inline Engine Balance

| Configuration | Crank Angles | Primary Force | Primary Couple | Secondary Force | Secondary Couple |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1-cyl** | — | $m r \omega^2$ | 0 | $m r^2 \omega^2 / L$ | 0 |
| **2-cyl inline** | $0°, 180°$ | 0 ✓ | $2 m r \omega^2 a$ | 0 ✓ | $2 m r^2 \omega^2 a / L$ |
| **3-cyl inline** | $0°, 120°, 240°$ | 0 ✓ | $1.5 m r \omega^2 a$ | 0 ✓ | $1.5 m r^2 \omega^2 a / L$ |
| **4-cyl inline (cross-plane)** | $0°, 180°, 180°, 0°$ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ |
| **4-cyl flat (boxer)** | $0°, 180°, 0°, 180°$ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ |
| **5-cyl inline** | $0°, 144°, 288°, 72°, 216°$ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ |
| **6-cyl inline** | $0°,60°,60°,0°,60°,60°$ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ | 0 ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result for KTU 2024:** An inline 4-cylinder **4-stroke** engine with cranks at $0°-180°-180°-0°$ (or equivalently, the standard cross-plane configuration) is **perfectly balanced** for both primary and secondary forces and couples. This is why it's the most common automotive engine configuration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Reciprocating Mass Balancing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reciprocating Mass m\nin Slider-Crank] --> B[Identify Crank Angle θ]
    B --> C[Compute Piston Acceleration\n a = -rω²cosθ - r²ω²/L · cos2θ]
    C --> D[Apply Newton's 2nd Law\n F = m·a]
    D --> E[Resolve into Harmonics]
    E --> F[Primary Force Fp\n Frequency: 1/rev]
    E --> G[Secondary Force Fs\n Frequency: 2/rev]
    F --> H{Can it be Balanced?}
    G --> I{Engine Type?}
    H --> J[Single Cylinder:\n Partial Balance with\n Counterweight mc·rc]
    H --> K[Multi Cylinder:\n Arrange Crank Phases]
    I --> L[2-Cyl: 180° Phase]
    I --> M[3-Cyl: 120° Phase]
    I --> N[4-Cyl: 0-180-180-0]
    J --> O[Residual: Hammer Blow\n + Swaying Couple]
    K --> P[Resultant Force Vector\n + Couple Analysis]
    L --> Q[Forces: 0\n Couples: 2mrω²a]
    M --> R[Forces: 0\n Couples: 1.5mrω²a]
    N --> S[ALL: 0 — Perfect Balance]
    O --> T[Design Foundation for\n Hammer Blow Magnitude]
    P --> U[Check if Couple <\n Permissible Limit]
    Q --> V[Add Balance Shafts\n if Required]
    R --> V
    S --> W[Engine Approved]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Multi-Cylinder Force Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [Problem Inputs]
        I1[Number of Cylinders n]
        I2[Crank Angles α₁...αₙ]
        I3[Cylinder Spacing a₁...aₙ]
        I4[Mass m, Radius r, Length L]
    end
    
    subgraph COMPUTE [Computation Steps]
        C1[Compute Σcosαᵢ, Σsinαᵢ]
        C2[Compute Σaᵢ·cosαᵢ, Σaᵢ·sinαᵢ]
        C3[Compute Σcos2αᵢ, Σsin2αᵢ]
        C4[Compute Σaᵢ·cos2αᵢ, Σaᵢ·sin2αᵢ]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT [Output Forces and Couples]
        O1[Primary Force\n Fp = mrω²·√X²+Y²]
        O2[Primary Couple\n Cp = mrω²·√X'²+Y'²]
        O3[Secondary Force\n Fs = mr²ω²/L·√X²''+Y²'']
        O4[Secondary Couple\n Cs = mr²ω²/L·√X'''²+Y'''²]
    end
    
    INPUT --> COMPUTE --> OUTPUT
    
    O1 --> J1{Net > 0?}
    O2 --> J2{Net > 0?}
    O3 --> J3{Net > 0?}
    O4 --> J4{Net > 0?}
    
    J1 --> R1[Engine vibrates:\n add balance shaft]
    J2 --> R2[Add balance weights]
    J3 --> R3[Acceptable if\n small ratio]
    J4 --> R4[Acceptable if\n small ratio]
```

## 4.3 Crank Arrangement Visualization (Inline-4 Engine)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CY4 [Cylinder 4 - Front]
        C4[Crank 4: 0°]
    end
    subgraph CY3 [Cylinder 3]
        C3[Crank 3: 180°]
    end
    subgraph CY2 [Cylinder 2]
        C2[Crank 2: 180°]
    end
    subgraph CY1 [Cylinder 1 - Rear]
        C1[Crank 1: 0°]
    end
    
    CY4 --> CY3 --> CY2 --> CY1
    
    C1 -.Primary Inertia.-> F1[Force at TDC: +m·r·ω²]
    C2 -.Primary Inertia.-> F2[Force at BDC: -m·r·ω²]
    C3 -.Primary Inertia.-> F3[Force at BDC: -m·r·ω²]
    C4 -.Primary Inertia.-> F4[Force at TDC: +m·r·ω²]
    
    F1 --> SUM1[+]
    F2 --> SUM1
    F3 --> SUM2[+]
    F4 --> SUM2
    
    SUM1 --> NET[Net = 0]
    SUM2 --> NET
```

## 4.4 Partial Balance Trade-Off Diagram (Single Cylinder)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Single Cylinder Engine] --> CHECK{Need to Balance?}
    CHECK --> YES[Add Counterweight mc at radius rc]
    CHECK --> NO[Run Unbalanced]
    
    YES --> CHOOSE{Choose Balance Ratio x}
    CHOOSE --> LOW[x = 0.5\n 50% primary balanced]
    CHOOSE --> MED[x = 0.7\n 70% primary balanced]
    CHOOSE --> HIGH[x = 0.9\n 90% primary balanced]
    
    LOW --> TR1[Unbalanced Primary: 0.5·mrω²\nHammer Blow: 0.5·mrω²]
    MED --> TR2[Unbalanced Primary: 0.3·mrω²\nHammer Blow: 0.7·mrω²]
    HIGH --> TR3[Unbalanced Primary: 0.1·mrω²\nHammer Blow: 0.9·mrω²]
    
    TR1 --> REC[Trade-off!\n Higher x = Lower vibration\n but Higher foundation load]
    TR2 --> REC
    TR3 --> REC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** These block diagrams map the KTU 2024 board examination logic. Examiners often give full or partial credit for **neat flow diagrams** showing how the analysis proceeds. Always label each block with the physical quantity it represents.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define primary and secondary forces in a reciprocating engine. Why is it impossible to completely balance the inertia forces in a single-cylinder engine?**

**Model Answer:**

> The inertia force of a reciprocating mass $m$ in a slider-crank mechanism is given by $F_i = m r \omega^2 \left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right]$. The first term $F_P = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$ is called the **primary force**; it varies sinusoidally once per crank revolution. The second term $F_S = \dfrac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$ is called the **secondary force**; it varies twice per revolution and is smaller in magnitude (by ratio $r/L$).
>
> A single rotating counterweight can balance only a **single rotating vector of constant magnitude** — equivalent to balancing a single $\cos\theta$ term. Since the primary force has a $\cos\theta$ dependence, it can be balanced by a counterweight (partially). However, the secondary force has a $\cos 2\theta$ dependence, meaning it rotates at **twice the crank speed** — a single counterweight cannot generate a $\cos 2\theta$ vector. Hence, **complete balance of both primary and secondary forces simultaneously is mathematically impossible** in a single-cylinder engine. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Explain the concept of "hammer blow" in a locomotive engine. How is it related to the balance ratio?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hammer blow** is the alternating vertical force transmitted from a locomotive engine to the rails (and hence to the track foundation) due to the rotating counterweight used to partially balance the reciprocating mass. It is also called the **vertical unbalanced force**.
>
> Mathematically, if the counterweight moment is $m_c r_c$ and the reciprocating mass moment is $m r$, and a balance ratio of $x$ is used (i.e., $m_c r_c = x m r$):
>
> $$\boxed{F_{HB} = (m_c r_c - m r) \omega^2 = (x - 1) m r \omega^2}$$
>
> Since $x < 1$ for partial balance, $(x - 1)$ is **negative** — meaning the hammer blow is in the **opposite direction** to the piston motion.
>
> **Relation to balance ratio:** As $x$ increases (more reciprocating mass balanced), $|F_{HB}|$ also increases proportionally. A 100% primary balance ($x = 1$) would double the hammer blow. Therefore, a compromise is chosen (typically $x = 0.5$ to $0.7$ in locomotives) to minimize both the **swaying force** and the **hammer blow** simultaneously. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Internal Choice Option A]`

**A four-cylinder inline engine has cranks arranged at $0°, 90°, 270°,$ and $180°$ from a common reference. The crank radius is $50$ mm, connecting rod length is $200$ mm, and the reciprocating mass per cylinder is $1.5$ kg. The engine runs at $3000$ rpm. Determine:**

**(a) The magnitude and direction of the resultant primary inertia force and the resultant primary couple at $\omega t = 0°$. Take cylinder spacing = $120$ mm. (7 marks)**

**(b) Repeat part (a) for the secondary inertia force and couple. Comment on the engine's balance quality. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Primary Force and Couple

**Given:**
- $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.2$ m, $m = 1.5$ kg
- $N = 3000$ rpm → $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 314.16$ rad/s
- Crank angles: $\alpha_1 = 0°$, $\alpha_2 = 90°$, $\alpha_3 = 270°$, $\alpha_4 = 180°$
- Spacing: $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 0.12$ m, $a_3 = 0.24$ m, $a_4 = 0.36$ m

**Step 1 — Primary Force Components:**

The primary force of cylinder $i$ at $\omega t = 0$ is $F_{P,i} = m r \omega^2 \cos\alpha_i$.

Common factor:

$$m r \omega^2 = 1.5 \times 0.05 \times (314.16)^2 = 1.5 \times 0.05 \times 98696 = 7402.2 \text{ N}$$

Computing each $\cos\alpha_i$:

| Cylinder | $\alpha_i$ | $\cos\alpha_i$ | $\sin\alpha_i$ | $a_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0°$ | $1.0$ | $0.0$ | $0.00$ |
| 2 | $90°$ | $0.0$ | $1.0$ | $0.12$ |
| 3 | $270°$ | $0.0$ | $-1.0$ | $0.24$ |
| 4 | $180°$ | $-1.0$ | $0.0$ | $0.36$ |

X-component of primary force:

$$\sum F_{P,x} = 7402.2 \times (1.0 + 0 + 0 - 1.0) = 0 \text{ N}$$

Y-component of primary force:

$$\sum F_{P,y} = 7402.2 \times (0 + 1.0 - 1.0 + 0) = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Resultant primary force:** $F_P^{net} = 0$ N. ✓ **(Primary forces balance.)**

**Step 2 — Primary Couple Components:**

Couple contribution of cylinder $i$ at $\omega t = 0$ (about the reference plane through cylinder 1):

$$C_{P,i} = F_{P,i} \cdot a_i \cdot \sin(\text{transverse component})$$

For an inline engine, the couple is computed from the y-component of the force acting at lever arm $a_i$:

$$C_P = \sum F_{P,i,y} \cdot a_i = m r \omega^2 \sum a_i \sin\alpha_i$$

$$\sum a_i \sin\alpha_i = 0 \times 0 + 0.12 \times 1.0 + 0.24 \times (-1.0) + 0.36 \times 0$$

$$= 0.12 - 0.24 + 0 = -0.12 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{C_P = 7402.2 \times (-0.12) = -888.26 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Magnitude:** $|C_P| = 888.26$ N·m. **Direction:** Negative — clockwise (looking from the front of the engine).

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Secondary Force, Couple, and Engine Balance Comment

**Step 1 — Secondary Force:**

Common factor:

$$\frac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L} = \frac{1.5 \times (0.05)^2 \times (314.16)^2}{0.2} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.0025 \times 98696}{0.2} = \frac{370.11}{0.2} = 1850.55 \text{ N}$$

Computing $\cos 2\alpha_i$ and $\sin 2\alpha_i$:

| Cylinder | $2\alpha_i$ | $\cos 2\alpha_i$ | $\sin 2\alpha_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0°$ | $1.0$ | $0.0$ |
| 2 | $180°$ | $-1.0$ | $0.0$ |
| 3 | $540°$ (= $180°$) | $-1.0$ | $0.0$ |
| 4 | $360°$ (= $0°$) | $1.0$ | $0.0$ |

X-component:

$$\sum F_{S,x} = 1850.55 \times (1.0 - 1.0 - 1.0 + 1.0) = 0 \text{ N}$$

Y-component:

$$\sum F_{S,y} = 1850.55 \times (0 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Resultant secondary force:** $F_S^{net} = 0$ N. ✓

**Step 2 — Secondary Couple:**

$$\sum a_i \sin 2\alpha_i = 0 \times 0 + 0.12 \times 0 + 0.24 \times 0 + 0.36 \times 0 = 0$$

$$\therefore C_S = 1850.55 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N·m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Engine Balance Comment:**

> The engine has a **non-standard crank arrangement** ($0°, 90°, 270°, 180°$). The primary and secondary forces are balanced, but a **non-zero primary couple** of $888.26$ N·m remains. This is in contrast to the **standard inline-4 cross-plane** arrangement ($0°, 180°, 180°, 0°$) which would give **perfect balance** for all forces and couples. This particular arrangement is **not recommended** for production engines due to the uncompensated primary couple that will cause noticeable **rocking vibration** about the engine's transverse axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often forget that the **reference plane** for couple calculation must be specified (e.g., plane through cylinder 1). If the plane is taken through the **engine center of gravity** (or middle of the block), the couple values change. Always state your reference plane explicitly.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - June 2022 / Internal Choice Option B]`

**A single-cylinder engine has the following data: crank radius = $100$ mm, connecting rod length = $400$ mm, mass of reciprocating parts = $50$ kg, engine speed = $1500$ rpm. The engine is fitted with a balance mass that balances 60% of the primary force.**

**(a) Determine the magnitude and position of the balance mass, the magnitude of the unbalanced primary force, the secondary force, and the swaying couple at a height of $0.8$ m above the center of the crankshaft. (7 marks)**

**(b) Calculate the hammer blow and the tractive force if the engine is mounted in a locomotive. Discuss the design implications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Balance Mass and Unbalanced Forces

**Given:**
- $r = 0.1$ m, $L = 0.4$ m, $m = 50$ kg, $N = 1500$ rpm
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08$ rad/s
- Balance ratio $x = 0.6$, height of CG $h = 0.8$ m

**Step 1 — Centrifugal Force of Reciprocating Mass Equivalent:**

$$F_P = m r \omega^2 = 50 \times 0.1 \times (157.08)^2 = 50 \times 0.1 \times 24674 = 123370 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Balance Mass Calculation:**

The counterweight must produce a centrifugal force equal to 60% of $F_P$ at the same radius (assumed):

$$m_c r_c \omega^2 = x \cdot m r \omega^2$$

If counterweight is placed at the same radius $r = 0.1$ m:

$$m_c = x \cdot m = 0.6 \times 50 = 30 \text{ kg}$$

**Position:** diametrically opposite to the crank pin (i.e., at $180°$ from the crank).

**Step 3 — Unbalanced Primary Force:**

$$F_P' = (1 - x) m r \omega^2 = 0.4 \times 50 \times 0.1 \times (157.08)^2$$

$$\boxed{F_P' = 0.4 \times 123370 = 49348 \text{ N} \approx 49.35 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Secondary Force:**

$$\boxed{F_S = \frac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L} = \frac{50 \times (0.1)^2 \times (157.08)^2}{0.4} = \frac{12337}{0.4} = 30843 \text{ N} \approx 30.84 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 5 — Swaying Couple:**

The swaying couple varies with the primary force. At maximum (when $\cos\theta = 1$):

$$\boxed{C_S = F_P' \times h = 49348 \times 0.8 = 39478 \text{ N·m} \approx 39.48 \text{ kN·m}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Hammer Blow and Tractive Force

**Step 1 — Hammer Blow (Vertical Force on Rails):**

The hammer blow is the **net vertical force** transmitted to the rails, which equals the unbalanced portion of the rotating counterweight minus the component of primary force along the vertical:

At maximum, when the crank is at $\theta = 0°$ (TDC):

$$F_{HB} = (m_c r_c - x \cdot m \cdot r) \omega^2 \cdot (\text{vertical component})$$

Since counterweight and reciprocating mass produce forces along the line of stroke:

$$F_{HB,max} = (m_c r_c - m r) \omega^2 = (30 \times 0.1 - 50 \times 0.1) \times (157.08)^2$$

$$= (3 - 5) \times 24674 = -49348 \text{ N}$$

**Magnitude:** $|F_{HB}| = 49.35$ kN, **directed downward** (since negative indicates opposite to the counterweight).

**Step 2 — Tractive Force Consideration:**

> In a locomotive, the tractive force is the horizontal component of force available at the wheel-rail interface for propulsion. The hammer blow does **not directly** contribute to tractive effort, but it limits the maximum usable adhesion because excessive vertical dynamic force can cause **wheel lifting** and **track damage**.

For this engine, with the weight on driving wheels $W$ (assumed $100$ kN per wheel), the **effective vertical force** at maximum hammer blow is:

$$F_{rail} = W + F_{HB} = 100 - 49.35 = 50.65 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Design Implications:**

> - The **49.35 kN hammer blow** is comparable to the static weight, meaning the wheel could momentarily **lift off the rail** at certain crank positions, leading to loss of adhesion and wheel slip.
> - The **secondary force (30.84 kN)** cannot be balanced by any rotating mass and will cause **twice-per-rev vertical vibration** transmitted to the locomotive body.
> - The **swaying couple of 39.48 kN·m** will produce a noticeable **lurching motion** of the locomotive, which is uncomfortable for passengers and stresses the suspension.
> - **Recommendation:** A balance ratio of $x = 0.5$ to $0.7$ is typical for locomotives — not higher, to keep the hammer blow within acceptable limits (typically 30% of axle load).
> - Real locomotives use **two-cylinder** configurations with cranks at $90°$ to cancel primary forces entirely and reduce hammer blow to a couple.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to **forget the negative sign** of the inertia force (i.e., confusing it with the reaction force on the cylinder). Always remember: **Inertia force opposes the acceleration of the mass**, while the **reaction force on the cylinder head** is the equal and opposite force. Mark deduction: **1 mark** for sign confusion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wise

> [!WARNING]
> ### Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Topic
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Writing $F_i = -m r \omega^2 [\cos\theta + (r/L)\cos 2\theta]$ without specifying that the negative sign means "directed toward the crank center" — examiner deducts 1 mark for ambiguity.
>
> 2. **Reference Plane Not Specified:** In multi-cylinder problems, students compute the couple about "the origin" without stating which cylinder plane. Always mention: "About the plane through cylinder 1 (rearmost)."
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Secondary Force:** In partial balancing problems, students balance the primary force and declare the engine "balanced." The **secondary force remains unchanged** and is a major source of vibration — this omission costs 2-3 marks.
>
> 4. **Firing Order ≠ Crank Order:** The 4-cylinder inline firing order is typically 1-3-4-2 (not 1-2-3-4). The crank arrangement is independent of firing order. Examiners often ask for the firing order, and students confuse it with the crank angle arrangement.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistencies:** Using $N$ in rpm directly in $\omega$ formulas (you must convert: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$). Common in numerical mistakes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2
>
> **Core Equations (Memorize First):**
> - Inertia Force: $F_i = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta + \dfrac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$
> - Primary Force: $F_P = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$ (frequency 1/rev)
> - Secondary Force: $F_S = \dfrac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$ (frequency 2/rev)
> - Counterweight: $m_c r_c = x \cdot m_{rec} \cdot r$
> - Hammer Blow: $F_{HB} = (m_c r_c - m_{rec} \cdot r)\omega^2$
> - Swaying Couple: $C_S = F_P' \cdot h$
>
> **Conceptual Facts:**
> - Single-cylinder engines **cannot** be fully balanced — only partial balance is possible.
> - **Secondary force** has 2× crank frequency and is **incurable** by simple counterweight.
> - **Inline-2**: forces balance, couple remains.
> - **Inline-3**: forces balance, couple remains (1.5× m·r·ω²·a).
> - **Inline-4 (cross-plane)**: **complete balance** — all forces and couples zero.
> - **Inline-6**: complete balance with cranks at 0-60-60-0-60-60 arrangement.
> - **Hammer blow** increases with balance ratio $x$ (trade-off).
>
> **Design Heuristics (Frequently Asked):**
> - $N = L/r$ ratio is typically 3.5–4 in passenger car engines.
> - Secondary force = (1/N) × primary force × some factor.
> - V-engines use bank angles (60°, 72°, 90°) to cancel primary couples.
> - Radial engines (5, 7, 9 cyl) have perfect force and moment balance.
>
> **Valuation Triggers (Always Do This):**
> - State the **reference plane** explicitly.
> - Show **all intermediate calculations** in tabular form.
> - Use **vector diagrams** when possible.
> - Give the **magnitude AND direction** for every resultant.
> - Conclude with a **physical interpretation** comment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheels: Turning Moment Diagrams for Four Stroke IC Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flywheels: Turning Moment Diagrams for Four Stroke IC Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Turning Moment Diagram (TMD):** A graphical plot that represents the **variation of the net turning moment (torque) acting on the engine crankshaft** as a function of the **crank angle** (measured in degrees) during one complete working cycle of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine.

For a **four-stroke IC engine**, one complete thermodynamic cycle spans **720°** of crankshaft rotation (i.e., **two full revolutions**), comprising four distinct strokes:
1. **Suction Stroke** → 0° to 180°
2. **Compression Stroke** → 180° to 360°
3. **Expansion (Power) Stroke** → 360° to 540°
4. **Exhaust Stroke** → 540° to 720°

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Turning Moment** is the **algebraic sum** of the **gas torque** (T_gas) produced by combustion pressure and the **inertia torque** (T_inertia) arising from the acceleration/deceleration of the reciprocating components (piston, connecting rod, etc.).

The governing relation is:

$$
T_{net} \;=\; T_{gas} \;\pm\; T_{inertia}
$$

The **sign convention** depends on the direction of crank rotation and the orientation of the engine (e.g., in-line, vertical, etc.).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pedaling Cyclist

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you are cycling up a hill using a single gear.** When you push the pedal **down** (the "power stroke"), the chain transfers a strong force to the rear wheel. As the pedal rises on the opposite side, **no force** is applied, and the wheel would slow down — **but it doesn't**, because the wheel itself has rotational inertia.

**This is exactly what a flywheel does for an IC engine:**
- During the **expansion (power) stroke**, the crankshaft receives a **large positive torque**, and the flywheel **stores kinetic energy** by speeding up.
- During the other three strokes (suction, compression, exhaust), the engine requires **very little or negative torque** to keep rotating. The flywheel **releases its stored kinetic energy**, keeping the crankshaft turning smoothly.

Just as a **heavy bicycle wheel** resists sudden speed changes, a properly designed **flywheel** acts as an **energy reservoir** that absorbs peaks and fills valleys of torque variation, ensuring **near-constant angular velocity** of the output shaft.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|----------|--------|------------------------|--------------|
| Mean Effective Pressure | $P_m$ | **6 – 12 bar** (typical for SI engines) | Average pressure on piston |
| Engine Speed | $N$ | **1000 – 3000 rpm** (typical) | Crank rotational speed |
| Crank Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s | Required for torque calculations |
| Work Done per Cycle | $W$ | Joules (J) | Area under the TMD |
| Fluctuation of Energy | $\Delta E$ | Joules (J) | $W_{max} - W_{min}$ |
| Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy | $C_E$ | **0.05 – 0.20** | $\Delta E / W$ |
| Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed | $C_S$ | **0.01 – 0.03** | $(N_{max} - N_{min})/N_{mean}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of TMD for a 4-Stroke Single-Cylinder Engine

The TMD is constructed by analyzing the **net torque** on the crank pin at every crank position. We break it down stroke-by-stroke:

### 2.1.1 Gas Torque ($T_{gas}$)
- **Exists only during the power stroke** (combustion + expansion) — though gas forces also act during compression (negative work) and other strokes with much smaller magnitude.
- It is the **primary source of useful work** in the cycle.
- Peak gas torque occurs slightly **after TDC (Top Dead Center)** during expansion, due to the crank-slider geometry.

### 2.1.2 Inertia Torque ($T_{inertia}$)
- Caused by the **acceleration and deceleration** of the reciprocating mass ($m_r$) as the piston moves.
- Acts during **all four strokes** of the cycle.
- For a simple harmonic approximation:
  $$
  T_{inertia} \;\approx\; m_r \cdot r^2 \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)
  $$
  where $r$ = crank radius, $L$ = connecting rod length, $\theta$ = crank angle from TDC.

### 2.1.3 Qualitative Shape of the TMD
| Stroke | Crank Range | Gas Torque | Inertia Torque | Net T-M |
|--------|-------------|------------|----------------|---------|
| Suction | 0° – 180° | ~Zero (or small) | Small (sinusoidal) | Small negative |
| Compression | 180° – 360° | Negative (work done on gas) | Significant (alternating) | Negative (largest) |
| **Expansion (Power)** | **360° – 540°** | **Large positive** | Significant | **Large positive peak** |
| Exhaust | 540° – 720° | Near zero | Small | Small negative |

> [!NOTE]
> The **mean torque line** is a horizontal line drawn such that the area above equals the area below. This represents the **average** torque over one cycle.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these formulas — they form the **backbone** of any 4-stroke flywheel numerical asked in KTU examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $W = \displaystyle\int_0^{4\pi} T\,d\theta$ | Work done per cycle (area under TMD) | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $T_{mean} = \dfrac{W}{4\pi}$ | Mean turning moment (4-stroke) | N·m |
| 3 | $P = T_{mean} \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{mean}}{60}$ | Power developed | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $W = \dfrac{120 \, P}{N}$ | Work per cycle from power & speed (4-stroke) | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $\Delta E = W_{max} - W_{min}$ | Fluctuation of energy | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$ | Coefficient of fluctuation of energy | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_S = \dfrac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}} = \dfrac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega_{mean}}$ | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta E = I \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2} \cdot C_S$ | Energy stored / released by flywheel | Joules (J) |
| 9 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{C_E \cdot W}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}}$ | **Moment of inertia of flywheel** | kg·m² |
| 10 | $m \cdot k^2 = I$ | Where $m$ = mass (kg), $k$ = radius of gyration (m) | kg·m² |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The TMD and flywheel design are **critical** in:
- **Automotive engines** (cars, motorcycles, trucks) — for smooth idling and acceleration.
- **Generator sets** — to maintain stable frequency (50/60 Hz) under varying electrical load.
- **Industrial punch presses & shears** — to store energy between working strokes.
- **Reciprocating pumps and compressors** — to handle intermittent loading.
- **Locomotives and marine propulsion** — to damp torsional vibrations of long shafts.

> [!TIP]
> **Design trade-off:** A flywheel that is too **heavy** increases engine cost and reduces acceleration responsiveness. A flywheel that is too **light** causes unacceptable speed fluctuations. The KTU-style design problem asks you to find the **minimum mass** that satisfies a given $C_S$ and $C_E$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Moment of Inertia of a Flywheel in Terms of $C_E$, $C_S$, and $\omega$

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 3 flywheel problems. We derive it line-by-line.

### Step 1: Express the energy of the flywheel at extreme speeds

The **kinetic energy** of the flywheel at any instant is:

$$
KE = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2
$$

At the **maximum speed** ($\omega_{max}$), kinetic energy is **maximum**:

$$
KE_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^{\,2}
$$

At the **minimum speed** ($\omega_{min}$), kinetic energy is **minimum**:

$$
KE_{min} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{min}^{\,2}
$$

### Step 2: Define the Fluctuation of Energy

The energy released (or stored) by the flywheel as speed varies from $\omega_{max}$ to $\omega_{min}$ is:

$$
\Delta E \;=\; KE_{max} - KE_{min} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{max}^{\,2} - \omega_{min}^{\,2} \right)
$$

### Step 3: Factor the difference of squares

Apply the identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)$:

$$
\omega_{max}^{\,2} - \omega_{min}^{\,2} \;=\; \left( \omega_{max} + \omega_{min} \right)\left( \omega_{max} - \omega_{min} \right)
$$

### Step 4: Introduce the mean angular velocity

By definition:

$$
\omega_{mean} \;=\; \frac{\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}}{2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{max} + \omega_{min} \;=\; 2\,\omega_{mean}
$$

And the **coefficient of fluctuation of speed**:

$$
C_S \;=\; \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega_{mean}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{max} - \omega_{min} \;=\; C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute into the energy equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= \tfrac{1}{2} I \cdot \left( \omega_{max} + \omega_{min} \right)\left( \omega_{max} - \omega_{min} \right) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} I \cdot \left( 2\,\omega_{mean} \right) \cdot \left( C_S \cdot \omega_{mean} \right) \\
&= I \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2} \cdot C_S
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result (Memory Anchor):**
> $$\boxed{\;\Delta E \;=\; I \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2} \cdot C_S\;}$$

### Step 6: Express the moment of inertia

Rearranging the above:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}}
$$

Now, since $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$, we have $\Delta E = C_E \cdot W$. Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\;I \;=\; \frac{C_E \cdot W}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}}\;}
$$

### Step 7: Convert $\omega_{mean}$ to RPM ($N$)

Since $\omega_{mean} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, we get $\omega_{mean}^{\,2} = \dfrac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} = \dfrac{\pi^2 N^2}{900}$. Therefore:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{C_E \cdot W \cdot 900}{C_S \cdot \pi^2 N^2}
$$

This form is **directly usable** in numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Torque and Power Relationships

For a **4-stroke engine**, one complete cycle = **2 revolutions** = $4\pi$ radians.

**Work done per cycle:**

$$
W \;=\; \int_0^{4\pi} T_{net}\, d\theta \;=\; T_{mean} \cdot 4\pi
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{mean} \;=\; \frac{W}{4\pi}
$$

**Power developed:**

$$
P \;=\; T_{mean} \cdot \omega \;=\; T_{mean} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

Substituting $T_{mean}$:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{W}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{W \cdot N}{120}
$$

Solving for $W$:

$$
\boxed{\;W \;=\; \frac{120\,P}{N}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Common Mistake Trap:** Many students write $W = \dfrac{60 P}{N}$ (using $2\pi$ instead of $4\pi$). For a **4-stroke** engine, the cycle is **two** revolutions, so it is **120 P / N**, not 60 P/N. Memorize this!

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Multi-Cylinder Engine TMD

For a **multi-cylinder engine** (e.g., 2-cylinder, 4-cylinder), the individual TMDs of each cylinder are **displaced along the crank-angle axis** by the **firing interval** and **superimposed** to give the **resultant TMD**.

| Engine Type | Number of Cylinders | Firing Order Interval | Resultant TMD |
|-------------|--------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Single | 1 | 720° (once per 2 revs) | One large peak per cycle |
| 2-Cylinder | 2 | 360° | Two peaks, 360° apart |
| 4-Cylinder | 4 | 180° | Four peaks, 180° apart |
| 6-Cylinder | 6 | 120° | Six peaks, 120° apart |

> [!NOTE]
> The **net fluctuation of energy** ($\Delta E$) in a multi-cylinder engine is **smaller** than in a single-cylinder engine of the same power, because the peaks are more closely spaced. Hence, a **lighter flywheel** can be used.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Flywheel System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of how a flywheel operates in an IC engine system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input[Energy Source]
        A1[Combustion of Fuel-Air Mixture] --> A2[Gas Pressure on Piston]
    end

    subgraph Conversion[Mechanical Conversion]
        A2 --> B1[Slider-Crank Mechanism]
        B1 --> B2[Variable Torque on Crankshaft]
    end

    subgraph Storage[Energy Storage Device]
        B2 --> C1{Flywheel}
        C1 --> C2[Stores KE when Torque is High]
        C1 --> C3[Releases KE when Torque is Low]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output to Load]
        C2 --> D1[Smoothed Torque Signal]
        C3 --> D1
        D1 --> D2[Near-Constant Angular Velocity]
        D2 --> D3[Power Transmission Shaft]
    end

    D3 --> E1[Application: Generator / Vehicle / Pump]

    style A1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style A2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style B1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style B2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style D2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style D3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style E1 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The combustion process generates a **variable torque** (Section B). This is fed to the flywheel (Section C), which acts as a **bi-directional energy buffer** — absorbing energy when input torque exceeds demand, and releasing it when input torque is below demand. The output (Section D) is a **smoothed torque** driving the load (Section E).

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — TMD Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stroke 1]
        P1[Suction 0 to 180 deg] --> T1[Small Negative T-M]
    end
    subgraph S2[Stroke 2]
        P2[Compression 180 to 360 deg] --> T2[Large Negative T-M]
    end
    subgraph S3[Stroke 3]
        P3[Power 360 to 540 deg] --> T3[Large Positive T-M Peak]
    end
    subgraph S4[Stroke 4]
        P4[Exhaust 540 to 720 deg] --> T4[Small Negative T-M]
    end

    T1 --> TMD[Combined Turning Moment Diagram]
    T2 --> TMD
    T3 --> TMD
    T4 --> TMD

    TMD --> ML[Draw Mean Torque Line]
    ML --> AR[Identify Areas Above and Below Mean Line]
    AR --> FE[Compute Fluctuation of Energy Delta E]
    FE --> FW[Design Flywheel Inertia I]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flow chart shows the **construction logic** of a single-cylinder 4-stroke TMD. The mean torque line is positioned so that the **net area above equals the net area below** it. The areas $W_{max}$ and $W_{min}$ (above and below the mean line, taken from the point of minimum energy) directly give $\Delta E$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define turning moment diagram. Why is it necessary to draw the turning moment diagram for an IC engine?**

**Model Answer:**

The **turning moment diagram (TMD)** is a graph that shows the variation of the **net turning moment (or torque)** on the engine crankshaft as a function of the **crank angle** during one complete working cycle.

**Necessity of TMD:**
1. It is essential for determining the **mean torque** and **work done per cycle** of the engine.
2. The **fluctuation of energy** ($\Delta E$) is computed from the difference between the **maximum and minimum energy points** on the TMD.
3. It enables the **design of the flywheel** (its moment of inertia) needed to limit the speed fluctuation to an acceptable value.
4. It helps in **balancing** multi-cylinder engines and in **torsional vibration analysis** of the crankshaft.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with their mathematical expressions:**
**(i) Fluctuation of energy**
**(ii) Coefficient of fluctuation of energy**
**(iii) Coefficient of fluctuation of speed**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E$):** The difference between the **maximum energy** and the **minimum energy** possessed by the flywheel during one cycle.
> $$\Delta E \;=\; W_{max} - W_{min}$$

> **(ii) Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$):** The ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of energy** to the **work done per cycle**.
> $$C_E \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$$

> **(iii) Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_S$):** The ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of speed** to the **mean speed** of the flywheel.
> $$C_S \;=\; \dfrac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}} \;=\; \dfrac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega_{mean}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the turning moment diagram for a **single-cylinder four-stroke IC engine**. Indicate clearly the events occurring during each of the four strokes and label the **gas torque** and **inertia torque** components.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch of TMD for a Single-Cylinder 4-Stroke Engine:**

```
Torque T
   ↑
T_max |        ___
      |       /   \                                
      |      /     \                               
      |     /       \                              
      |    /         \                             
------|---/-----------\---------------  ← Mean Torque Line (T_mean)
      |  /             \___                         
      | /                  \____                   
      |/                        \___               
   0  |/____________________________\_____→ θ (Crank Angle)
      0°   180°   360°   540°   720°
      |Suction|Compr. | Power |Exhaust|
```

**Stroke-by-Stroke Explanation:**

| Stroke | Crank Range | Net T-M Behaviour | Reason |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|--------|
| **Suction** | 0° – 180° | Slightly **negative** | Piston draws in charge; small gas force opposing motion; some inertia torque |
| **Compression** | 180° – 360° | **Negative** (largest magnitude) | Work is done on the gas; peak negative torque near 270° |
| **Expansion (Power)** | 360° – 540° | **Large positive peak** | Combustion gases push piston; peak torque near 380°–420° |
| **Exhaust** | 540° – 720° | Slightly **negative** | Burnt gases expelled; small negative torque |

**Gas Torque vs. Inertia Torque:**
- The **gas torque** is non-zero only during the **power stroke** (large positive) and the **compression stroke** (negative). It is **negligible** during suction and exhaust.
- The **inertia torque** is **sinusoidal** and exists during **all four strokes** due to piston acceleration/deceleration.
- The **net T-M** is their **algebraic sum** at every crank angle.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has the following specifications:
- Bore $D = 150$ mm
- Stroke $L = 200$ mm
- Mean effective pressure $P_m = 6.5$ bar
- Speed $N = 300$ rpm

Calculate: **(i) Indicated Power, (ii) Mean Torque, (iii) Work done per cycle.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
- $L = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m
- $P_m = 6.5$ bar $= 6.5 \times 10^5$ N/m²
- $N = 300$ rpm (4-stroke engine)

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of piston**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 \;=\; 0.01767 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Work done per cycle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= P_m \cdot A \cdot L \\
&= 6.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.01767 \times 0.2 \\
&= 2297.5 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Number of working cycles per second**

For a 4-stroke engine, 1 cycle = 2 revolutions.

$$
n_{cycle} = \frac{N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{300}{120} = 2.5 \text{ cycles/s}
$$

**Step 4: Indicated Power (iP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
iP &= W \times n_{cycle} \\
&= 2297.5 \times 2.5 \\
&= 5743.75 \text{ W} \approx 5.74 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Mean Torque**

The mean angular velocity is:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
T_{mean} = \frac{iP}{\omega} = \frac{5743.75}{31.416} = 182.83 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Verification using $W = 4\pi T_{mean}$:**

$$
T_{mean} = \frac{W}{4\pi} = \frac{2297.5}{4\pi} = 182.83 \text{ N·m} \;\;\checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students often use $W = 2\pi T_{mean}$ (assuming 1 rev per cycle) for a 4-stroke engine. The correct relation is $W = 4\pi T_{mean}$. **Memorize: 4-stroke → 2 revolutions → $4\pi$ radians.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define and explain the **coefficient of fluctuation of energy** and the **coefficient of fluctuation of speed**. Show that the moment of inertia of a flywheel is given by:

$$I \;=\; \frac{C_E \cdot W}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

> **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$):** It is the ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of energy** of the flywheel to the **work done per cycle** by the engine.
> $$C_E \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$$

> **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_S$):** It is the ratio of the **difference between maximum and minimum speeds** of the flywheel to the **mean speed** during one cycle.
> $$C_S \;=\; \dfrac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega_{mean}}$$

**Derivation:**

The kinetic energy of the flywheel at maximum and minimum speeds:

$$
KE_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^{\,2}, \qquad KE_{min} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{min}^{\,2}
$$

The fluctuation of energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= KE_{max} - KE_{min} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{max}^{\,2} - \omega_{min}^{\,2} \right) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{max} + \omega_{min} \right)\left( \omega_{max} - \omega_{min} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\omega_{max} + \omega_{min} = 2\omega_{mean}$ and $\omega_{max} - \omega_{min} = C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}$:

$$
\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \cdot 2\omega_{mean} \cdot C_S \omega_{mean} = I \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2} \cdot C_S
$$

Now using $\Delta E = C_E \cdot W$:

$$
C_E \cdot W = I \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2} \cdot C_S
$$

$$
\boxed{\;I \;=\; \dfrac{C_E \cdot W}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}}\;}
$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine develops **11 kW** at **240 rpm**. The coefficient of fluctuation of energy is **0.09** and the coefficient of fluctuation of speed is **2%**. Find the **moment of inertia** of the flywheel.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Power $P = 11$ kW $= 11{,}000$ W
- Speed $N = 240$ rpm
- $C_E = 0.09$
- $C_S = 0.02$

**Step 1: Work done per cycle (4-stroke)**

$$
W = \frac{120 \, P}{N} = \frac{120 \times 11{,}000}{240} = 5500 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 2: Fluctuation of energy**

$$
\Delta E = C_E \cdot W = 0.09 \times 5500 = 495 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Mean angular velocity**

$$
\omega_{mean} = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 25.133 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_{mean}^{\,2} = (25.133)^2 = 631.65 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 4: Moment of inertia of the flywheel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}} \\
&= \frac{495}{0.02 \times 631.65} \\
&= \frac{495}{12.633} \\
&= 39.18 \text{ kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $I \approx 39.18$ kg·m²

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Always convert power to **Watts** and speed to **rad/s** before substituting.
> 2. **Cycle confusion:** Use $W = 120P/N$ for **4-stroke** (not $60P/N$). Using the wrong factor gives **half** the correct value.
> 3. **Symbol confusion:** $C_S$ is **not** squared in the formula. Some students incorrectly write $C_S^2$ in the denominator. The correct form is $C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}$.
> 4. **Reporting:** Express the final answer in **kg·m²** (or sometimes kg·m²) with appropriate significant figures.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong work-per-cycle formula** — Using $60P/N$ instead of $120P/N$ for 4-stroke engines (loses 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Sign of torque** — Forgetting that during compression the gas torque is **negative**.
> 3. **Mean torque vs. peak torque** — Confusing these two in numerical problems.
> 4. **Units** — Leaving $N$ in rpm without converting to rad/s (loses 1 mark).
> 5. **TMD sketch** — Not labelling all four strokes or omitting the mean torque line (loses 1–2 marks).
> 6. **Multi-cylinder TMD** — Failing to mention that crank angles are **displaced** by the firing interval.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this checklist for the night before the exam!**

- [ ] A **Turning Moment Diagram (TMD)** plots the **net torque** on the crankshaft vs. **crank angle** for one cycle.
- [ ] For a **4-stroke engine**, one cycle = **720°** of crank rotation = **2 revolutions**.
- [ ] The **power stroke** gives the **large positive** peak; the other three strokes give **small or negative** torque.
- [ ] **Gas torque** dominates during power/compression; **inertia torque** exists throughout.
- [ ] **Work done per cycle** $W$ = area under the TMD = $T_{mean} \times 4\pi$ (4-stroke).
- [ ] Power $P = \dfrac{W \cdot N}{120}$ (4-stroke) → equivalently $W = \dfrac{120P}{N}$.
- [ ] **Fluctuation of energy** $\Delta E = W_{max} - W_{min}$ (areas above and below mean torque line).
- [ ] **Coefficient of fluctuation of energy** $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$ (typical: 0.05 – 0.20).
- [ ] **Coefficient of fluctuation of speed** $C_S = \dfrac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}}$ (typical: 0.01 – 0.03).
- [ ] **Key flywheel formula:** $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{C_E \cdot W}{C_S \cdot \omega_{mean}^{\,2}}$.
- [ ] In RPM form: $I = \dfrac{900 \cdot C_E \cdot W}{\pi^2 \cdot C_S \cdot N^2}$.
- [ ] For **multi-cylinder engines**, individual TMDs are **displaced by the firing interval** and **superimposed**.
- [ ] More cylinders → **smoother torque** → **smaller flywheel** needed (smaller $\Delta E$).
- [ ] A flywheel acts as a **bi-directional energy reservoir**, storing energy at $T_{max}$ and releasing at $T_{min}$.
- [ ] The **mean torque line** is positioned such that the **net area above equals the net area below**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy, Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy, Speed # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed (Cs) ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) The **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed (Cs)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed of a flywheel to its mean (average) speed during one complete cycle of operation of the mechanism. $$C_S = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$$ Where: - **N₁** = Maximum speed of the flywheel during the cycle (in **rpm**) - **N₂** = Minimum speed of the flywheel during the cycle (in **rpm**) - **N** = Mean speed of the flywheel = **(N₁ + N₂) / 2** (in **rpm**) - **ω₁, ω₂, ω** = Corresponding angular velocities in **rad/s** An alternative, equally valid expression is: $$C_S = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{\left(\frac{N_1 + N_2}{2}\right)} = \frac{2(N_1 - N_2)}{N_1 + N_2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Convention:** The value of Cs for most practical machinery lies between **0.01 and 0.20** (i.e., 1% to 20%). Smaller values indicate smoother operation; larger values indicate rougher operation requiring a heavier flywheel. --- ## 1.2 Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy (CE) ### Formal Definition The **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy (CE)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy of the flywheel to its mean (working) kinetic energy during one complete cycle. $$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{E_1 - E_2}{E}$$ Where: - **ΔE = E₁ − E₂** = Maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy of the flywheel (in **Joules**) - **E** = Mean kinetic energy of the flywheel (in **Joules**) - **E₁** = Maximum KE of the flywheel at ω₁ - **E₂** = Minimum KE of the flywheel at ω₂ The fluctuation of energy is computed as: $$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does energy fluctuate?** In a typical engine or machine, the energy supplied by the prime mover (e.g., steam, IC engine, electric motor through a slider-crank) is **not uniform** throughout a cycle. The crank-shaft receives **excess energy** during part of the cycle (power stroke) and **deficit energy** during the other parts (idle strokes). The flywheel absorbs this excess and releases it during the deficit, thereby keeping the speed nearly constant. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine a **water tank with an inlet pipe and an outlet pipe**, placed on a seesaw balance: - The **inlet pipe** (prime mover) pours water in pulses — sometimes gushing, sometimes trickling. - The **outlet pipe** (load, e.g., a factory machine) draws water at a roughly steady rate. - A **large reserve tank (the flywheel)** sits between them. When the inlet over-fills, the reserve tank stores the extra water. When the inlet under-fills, the reserve tank releases its stored water. Now map the analogy: | Water Tank Analogy | Mechanical Equivalent | |---|---| | Pulse of water inflow | Variable energy from prime mover | | Steady outflow | Constant work done by machine | | Reserve tank level change | Energy stored in flywheel | | Tank water level | Speed of the flywheel | - The **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy (CE)** is like the **percentage change in reserve tank volume**. - The **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed (Cs)** is like the **percentage change in tank water level** (which is much smaller because the same volume change moves the level only slightly in a wide tank). > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** A **heavier flywheel** acts like a **wider reserve tank** — it stores the same excess energy but causes a much smaller speed change. This is why engineers always want the *heaviest possible* flywheel that fits the design constraints! --- ## 1.4 The Master Relationship The two coefficients are **mathematically related**. Starting from the definitions: - Maximum KE: $E_1 = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2$ - Minimum KE: $E_2 = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2$ - Mean KE: $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ (where $\omega$ is the mean angular speed) $$C_E = \frac{\frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2} = \frac{\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2}{\omega^2}$$ Factorising the numerator: $$C_E = \frac{(\omega_1 - \omega_2)(\omega_1 + \omega_2)}{\omega^2} = \frac{(\omega_1 - \omega_2)}{\omega} \cdot \frac{(\omega_1 + \omega_2)}{\omega}$$ Substituting the definition of Cs: $$C_E = C_S \cdot \frac{(\omega_1 + \omega_2)}{\omega}$$ Since the mean speed $\omega = (\omega_1 + \omega_2)/2$, we have $(\omega_1 + \omega_2) = 2\omega$. Therefore: $$\boxed{C_E = 2 \, C_S}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This exact relationship $C_E = 2C_S$ holds when the mean speed is the arithmetic mean of max and min speeds.** For very small fluctuations, this approximation is universally used in KTU board problems. (A more precise form $C_E = C_S(2 + C_S)$ exists for the geometric mean definition, but the arithmetic-mean form is the KTU board standard.) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Interpretation of the Two Coefficients The two coefficients are **not the same physical quantity**: | Aspect | Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed (Cs) | Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy (CE) | |---|---|---| | **What it measures** | Percentage change in flywheel speed | Percentage change in flywheel kinetic energy | | **Typical value** | 1/50 to 1/100 (small) | Always greater than Cs | | **Relation to flywheel size** | Inversely related to I (mass moment of inertia) | Independent of I (it cancels out!) | | **Used in** | Speed regulation studies | Energy storage design (flywheel sizing) | | **Board exam focus** | High — almost every KTU problem | High — central to flywheel design | > [!WARNING] > **Common student mistake:** Confusing which coefficient applies to *which* variable. **Cs** is about **speed**, **CE** is about **energy**. They are related, but not identical! --- ## 2.2 Why Are These Coefficients Important in Engineering? ### Real-World Applications: 1. **Flywheel Sizing in IC Engines** — Determines the mass and radius of the flywheel to ensure the engine runs within acceptable speed limits throughout the 4-stroke cycle. 2. **Punch Press Operations** — A punch press performs work only during the punching stroke (a small fraction of the cycle). The flywheel stores energy from the motor during the idle period and releases it during the punch. 3. **Rolling Mills, Shearing Machines, Hammering Operations** — Intermittent heavy loading with light motor input. 4. **Generator Sets with Reciprocating Pumps** — Smooth out the cyclic torque variation. 5. **Textile Machinery and Printing Presses** — Require constant speed for product quality. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C_S = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N}$ | Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed | Dimensionless | | 2 | $C_S = \dfrac{2(N_1 - N_2)}{N_1 + N_2}$ | Alternative form using max/min speeds | Dimensionless | | 3 | $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E}$ | Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy | Dimensionless | | 4 | $\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2\right)$ | Fluctuation of Kinetic Energy | J (Joules) | | 5 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Mean Kinetic Energy of Flywheel | J (Joules) | | 6 | $C_E = 2 C_S$ | Master Relationship (arithmetic mean speed) | Dimensionless | | 7 | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_E \, \omega^2}$ | Required flywheel moment of inertia | kg·m² | | 8 | $m = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_E \, \omega^2 \, k^2}$ | Required mass of flywheel (with k = radius of gyration) | kg | | 9 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Conversion from rpm to rad/s | rad/s | | 10 | $N = \dfrac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$ | Mean speed | rpm | > [!TIP] > **Pro Tip for KTU Exams:** Memorize formulas 1, 3, 6, 7, 9. These five appear in **almost every flywheel problem** in the KTU board exam. --- ## 2.4 Detailed Logic of the Energy-Fluctuation Process During one complete cycle of a machine, the following sequence occurs: **Phase 1 — Energy Excess (Speed builds up):** - The work done by the prime mover in a small angle **dθ** exceeds the work required by the load. - The surplus energy goes into the flywheel, increasing its speed from ω₂ to ω₁. - Flywheel **stores** energy: $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ **Phase 2 — Energy Deficit (Speed drops):** - The work done by the prime mover is less than the work required. - The flywheel releases the previously stored energy, slowing down from ω₁ back to ω₂. - Flywheel **releases** energy: same $\Delta E$ **Steady-State Condition:** At the end of the cycle, the flywheel is back to its initial speed ω₂, and the cycle repeats. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **maximum fluctuation of energy** $\Delta E$ is the area enclosed between the **energy supplied curve** and the **energy required curve** (drawn against crank angle θ). This is a **high-frequency KTU question** — students must draw or interpret the energy diagram. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of $C_E = 2 C_S$ **Step 1 — Start with the definitions:** By definition: $$C_S = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$C_E = \frac{E_1 - E_2}{E} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2 - \frac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 2 — Simplify the numerator of CE using difference of squares:** The numerator of CE is: $$E_1 - E_2 = \frac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2\right)$$ Applying the algebraic identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$: $$E_1 - E_2 = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1 - \omega_2)(\omega_1 + \omega_2)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the expression for CE:** $$C_E = \frac{\frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1 - \omega_2)(\omega_1 + \omega_2)}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2}$$ The $\frac{1}{2} I$ terms cancel out: $$C_E = \frac{(\omega_1 - \omega_2)(\omega_1 + \omega_2)}{\omega^2}$$ **Step 4 — Split the fraction into two multiplicative parts:** $$C_E = \left[\frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}\right] \cdot \left[\frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{\omega}\right]$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the first bracket as Cs:** From equation (i), we know: $$\frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega} = C_S$$ So: $$C_E = C_S \cdot \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{\omega}$$ **Step 6 — Apply the arithmetic-mean definition of mean speed:** The mean angular velocity is: $$\omega = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_1 + \omega_2 = 2\omega$$ Substituting this into the expression: $$C_E = C_S \cdot \frac{2\omega}{\omega} = 2 \, C_S$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{C_E = 2 \, C_S}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Remark:** The factor of 2 appears because the **mean of squares is not equal to the square of the mean**. Since $E \propto \omega^2$, even a small speed fluctuation produces a larger energy fluctuation. The factor of 2 captures this quadratic amplification. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 (Typical KTU Board Problem) > **Problem:** A single-cylinder double-acting steam engine develops 75 kW at a mean speed of 300 rpm. The speed fluctuation is limited to ±3% of the mean speed. Find: > (a) Coefficient of fluctuation of speed, Cs > (b) Coefficient of fluctuation of energy, CE > (c) Maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy, ΔE ### Solution: **Given Data:** - Power P = 75 kW = 75,000 W - Mean speed N = 300 rpm - Speed fluctuation = ±3% of mean speed This means: - Maximum speed deviation: $(N_1 - N)/N = 0.03$ and $(N - N_2)/N = 0.03$ - So: $N_1 = N + 0.03N = 1.03N$ and $N_2 = N - 0.03N = 0.97N$ **Part (a) — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed Cs:** $$C_S = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \frac{1.03N - 0.97N}{N} = \frac{0.06N}{N} = 0.06$$ $$\boxed{C_S = 0.06 \text{ (or 6%)}}$$ **Part (b) — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy CE:** Using the master relationship: $$C_E = 2 C_S = 2 \times 0.06$$ $$\boxed{C_E = 0.12 \text{ (or 12%)}}$$ **Part (c) — Maximum Fluctuation of Kinetic Energy ΔE:** First, find the mean angular velocity: $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$ The mean kinetic energy of the flywheel can be computed from the work done per cycle. The work done per second is the power (75,000 W).
