The time per revolution is: $$T = \frac{60}{N} = \frac{60}{300} = 0.2 \text{ s/rev}$$ For a single-cylinder engine, one cycle = 2 revolutions (so T_cycle = 0.4 s). However, the *mean KE* of the flywheel is given by $\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$, which we can also write as the energy stored at mean speed. > In standard KTU problems, the **mean kinetic energy E** is computed from the **work done per cycle divided by the number of revolutions per cycle**. For a single-cylinder engine, the cycle is 2 revolutions. Work per cycle = Power × Time per cycle $$W_{cycle} = 75{,}000 \times 0.4 = 30{,}000 \text{ J}$$ Assuming the mean KE E is computed using the cycle: $$E = \frac{W_{cycle}}{2} = 15{,}000 \text{ J}$$ *(this depends on the convention used in your textbook)* A more standard approach: For a single-cylinder double-acting engine with 1 power stroke per revolution (approximation), the mean KE may be derived from the design. The KTU answer convention uses: $$E = \frac{P \times 60}{2 \times N} = \frac{75{,}000 \times 60}{2 \times 300} = 7{,}500 \text{ J}$$ Therefore: $$\Delta E = C_E \times E = 0.12 \times 7{,}500$$ $$\boxed{\Delta E = 900 \text{ J}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note to student:** The exact value of $E$ depends on whether the engine is single-cylinder or double-acting, and on the textbook's convention. The principle is always $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ and $\Delta E = C_E \times E$. Always verify with the data given in your specific problem. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 (Flywheel Mass Calculation) > **Problem:** The turning moment diagram for a multi-cylinder engine has a maximum fluctuation of energy of 5000 J. The mean speed of the flywheel is 250 rpm. The coefficient of fluctuation of speed is 0.04. Find the mass of the flywheel if the radius of gyration is 0.5 m. ### Solution: **Given Data:** - Maximum fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$ = 5000 J - Mean speed N = 250 rpm - $C_S$ = 0.04 - Radius of gyration k = 0.5 m **Step 1 — Compute CE using the master relationship:** $$C_E = 2 \, C_S = 2 \times 0.04 = 0.08$$ **Step 2 — Compute the mean angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 250}{60} = \frac{500\pi}{60} = \frac{25\pi}{3} \approx 26.18 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 3 — Find the required moment of inertia I:** From the formula: $$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_E \, \omega^2}$$ Substituting values: $$I = \frac{5000}{0.08 \times (26.18)^2} = \frac{5000}{0.08 \times 685.39} = \frac{5000}{54.83} \approx 91.19 \text{ kg·m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Convert moment of inertia to mass using k²:** $$I = m k^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{I}{k^2} = \frac{91.19}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{91.19}{0.25}$$ $$\boxed{m \approx 364.76 \text{ kg}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Scenario | Cs | CE | Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Smooth-running precision lathe | 0.01 | 0.02 | Small flywheel, high precision | | Typical IC engine | 0.02 – 0.05 | 0.04 – 0.10 | Moderate flywheel | | Heavy punch press | 0.10 – 0.20 | 0.20 – 0.40 | Massive flywheel required | | Hypothetical case (max speed = min speed) | 0 | 0 | Infinite flywheel mass (impossible to fluctuate) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Flywheel Energy Management System This block diagram maps the complete energy and speed flow from prime mover through the flywheel to the load, showing where the coefficient of fluctuation comes into play. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PRIME MOVER
Steam Engine / IC Engine / Motor] -->|Variable Torque Tp| B[CRANKSHAFT
Converts reciprocating to rotary motion] B -->|Cyclic Energy Supply Es| C[FLYWHEEL
Energy Storage Unit
Stores KE when Es greater than El
Releases KE when Es less than El] C -->|Smoothed Energy Output| D[POWER TRANSMISSION
Gears / Belts / Couplings] D -->|Constant Torque TL| E[LOAD
Machine Tool / Pump / Generator] C -->|Speed fluctuates between omega1 and omega2| F[SPEED REGULATION LOOP
Governors and Sensors] F -->|Feedback Signal| A G[Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed Cs
= omega1 - omega2 divided by omega] -.-> C H[Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy CE

Es minus El versus crank angle theta] -.-> C style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px style I fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px ``` **Interpretation of the Diagram:** 1. **Block A (Prime Mover):** Supplies energy in pulses — the input torque varies cyclically. 2. **Block B (Crankshaft):** Converts the variable input into a rotating output with cyclic energy. 3. **Block C (Flywheel — the heart of the system):** Acts as a buffer. The **green highlighting** signifies its critical role in this Module 3 topic. 4. **Block D (Transmission):** Smooths and delivers energy to the load. 5. **Block E (Load):** Receives (ideally) constant energy. 6. **Block F (Speed Regulation Loop):** Provides feedback for system control. 7. **G, H, I:** The three analytical tools we use in this Module: - **G:** Cs quantifies how much the speed varies. - **H:** CE quantifies how much the energy varies (and is related to Cs). - **I:** The energy diagram visually represents where the flywheel stores/releases energy. --- ## 4.2 The Energy Diagram Concept (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR J[Energy Supplied by Prime Mover Es
Curve vs Crank Angle theta] --> K{Compare at each theta} L[Energy Required by Load El
Constant horizontal line] --> K K -->|Es greater than El| M[Flywheel ACCELERATES
Absorbs surplus energy Delta E
Speed increases from omega2 to omega1] K -->|Es less than El| N[Flywheel DECELERATES
Releases stored energy Delta E
Speed decreases from omega1 to omega2] K -->|Es equals El| O[Steady State
No energy exchange
Speed constant at omega] style J fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style N fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style O fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the concept of energy fluctuation," this three-state logic (Es > El, Es < El, Es = El) is the most common KTU board answer structure. Always illustrate with crank angle θ on the x-axis. --- ## 4.3 Conceptual Speed-Time Waveform (Text Schematic) Although Mermaid cannot render a true time-domain plot, the following ASCII schematic illustrates the **speed of the flywheel** through one complete cycle: ``` ω₁ ────────────────────────────────────────── (max speed) ╲ ↑ Phase 1: Energy absorbed ╲ │ (Es > El, flywheel accelerates) ╲ │ ╲ │ ω ──────●──●───────●─────── (mean speed, the horizontal line) ╱ │ ╲ ╱ │ ╲ ╱ │ ╲ ╱ ↓ ╲ Phase 2: Energy released ╱ (Es < El, flywheel decelerates) ω₂ ──────────────────────── (min speed) ↑ ↑ Cycle Start Cycle End → Crank angle θ increases → ``` The shaded area between the curve and the mean line represents the **kinetic energy fluctuation** $\Delta E$. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the terms:** (a) Coefficient of fluctuation of speed (b) Coefficient of fluctuation of energy **Model Answer:** (a) **Coefficient of fluctuation of speed (Cs):** It is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed of the flywheel to the mean speed during one complete cycle. $$C_S = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N}$$ where N₁ and N₂ are the maximum and minimum speeds, and N is the mean speed. (b) **Coefficient of fluctuation of energy (CE):** It is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy of the flywheel to its mean kinetic energy. $$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{E_1 - E_2}{E}$$ where E₁ and E₂ are the maximum and minimum kinetic energies, and E is the mean kinetic energy. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the relationship between the coefficient of fluctuation of energy and the coefficient of fluctuation of speed. Hence, define the term "mean kinetic energy" of a flywheel.** **Model Answer:** The relationship is: $$C_E = 2 \, C_S$$ **Derivation sketch:** Starting from $C_E = \frac{\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2}{\omega^2}$ and using $C_S = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$ with $\omega = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$, we obtain $C_E = 2 C_S$. **Mean kinetic energy of a flywheel** is the kinetic energy of the flywheel when it is rotating at its mean (average) angular velocity ω: $$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$ where I is the mass moment of inertia of the flywheel about its axis of rotation, and ω is the mean angular velocity in rad/s. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Every Part B question in the End Semester Examination carries 14 marks, typically split into sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. The student must answer **either Option A or Option B** (internal choice). --- ### Option A (14 Marks) #### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]` **Q. A (a) [7 Marks]:** The turning moment diagram for a multi-cylinder engine has been drawn to a scale of 1 mm = 600 N·m of torque and 1 mm = 6° of crank angle. The mean speed is 200 rpm. The total fluctuation of energy is found to be 36,000 J. The radius of gyration of the flywheel is 0.5 m. Find: (i) The coefficient of fluctuation of energy (ii) The mass of the flywheel (iii) The coefficient of fluctuation of speed if the fluctuation of speed is to be limited to 1.5% of the mean speed. **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Mean speed N = 200 rpm - $\Delta E$ = 36,000 J - Radius of gyration k = 0.5 m - $C_S$ required = 1.5% = 0.015 (assumed from problem statement) **Mean angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 200}{60} = \frac{400\pi}{60} = \frac{20\pi}{3} \approx 20.944 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 1 — To find CE, we need E (mean kinetic energy):** This requires I, which is what we need to find. So we approach it differently using the relationship: $$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_E \cdot \omega^2}$$ **Step 2 — Use the given Cs to find CE:** $$C_E = 2 \, C_S = 2 \times 0.015 = 0.03$$ **Step 3 — Compute I:** $$I = \frac{36{,}000}{0.03 \times (20.944)^2} = \frac{36{,}000}{0.03 \times 438.65} = \frac{36{,}000}{13.16} \approx 2{,}736 \text{ kg·m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Compute mass of flywheel:** $$m = \frac{I}{k^2} = \frac{2{,}736}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{2{,}736}{0.25} = 10{,}944 \text{ kg}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $C_E = 0.03$ - (ii) m ≈ **10,944 kg** - (iii) $C_S = 0.015$ --- **Q. A (b) [7 Marks]:** A 4-stroke single-cylinder IC engine develops 25 kW at a mean speed of 1000 rpm. The turning moment diagram shows a coefficient of fluctuation of energy of 0.10. Determine: (i) The maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy (ii) The mean kinetic energy of the flywheel (iii) The mass of the flywheel if the radius of gyration is 0.4 m **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Power P = 25 kW = 25,000 W - N = 1000 rpm - $C_E$ = 0.10 - k = 0.4 m **Step 1 — Mean angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = \frac{2000\pi}{60} = \frac{100\pi}{3} \approx 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Work done per cycle:** For a 4-stroke engine, one cycle = 2 revolutions. $$T_{cycle} = \frac{2 \times 60}{1000} = 0.12 \text{ s}$$ $$W_{cycle} = P \times T_{cycle} = 25{,}000 \times 0.12 = 3000 \text{ J}$$ **Step 3 — Mean kinetic energy E:** Conventionally, the mean KE of the flywheel is half of the work per cycle (for single-cylinder IC engine): $$E = \frac{W_{cycle}}{2} = 1500 \text{ J}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy:** $$\Delta E = C_E \times E = 0.10 \times 1500 = 150 \text{ J}$$ **Step 5 — Moment of inertia of flywheel:** $$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_E \cdot \omega^2} = \frac{150}{0.10 \times (104.72)^2} = \frac{150}{0.10 \times 10{,}966} = \frac{150}{1096.6} \approx 0.1368 \text{ kg·m}^2$$ **Step 6 — Mass of flywheel:** $$m = \frac{I}{k^2} = \frac{0.1368}{(0.4)^2} = \frac{0.1368}{0.16} \approx 0.855 \text{ kg}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $\Delta E = 150$ J - (ii) E = 1500 J - (iii) m ≈ **0.855 kg** --- ### Option B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]` **Q. B (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the concept of **coefficient of fluctuation of energy** and **coefficient of fluctuation of speed** with neat energy diagram. Establish the relationship $C_E = 2 C_S$. **Model Solution Outline:** **1. Definitions (2 marks):** - CE = ΔE/E — ratio of maximum KE fluctuation to mean KE - Cs = (N₁ − N₂)/N — ratio of speed fluctuation to mean speed **2. Energy Diagram Description (2 marks):** - Plot of energy supplied by prime mover (Es) vs. crank angle θ — wavy/irregular curve - Plot of energy required by load (El) vs. θ — straight line (mean line) - Positive loops (above mean) → energy stored in flywheel - Negative loops (below mean) → energy released from flywheel - Maximum fluctuation ΔE = maximum positive area + maximum negative area **3. Derivation of CE = 2Cs (3 marks):** Show the full derivation as in **Section 3.1** of these notes: - $C_E = \frac{\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2}{\omega^2} = \frac{(\omega_1 - \omega_2)(\omega_1 + \omega_2)}{\omega^2} = C_S \cdot \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{\omega}$ - Using $\omega = (\omega_1 + \omega_2)/2$, we get $C_E = 2 C_S$ --- **Q. B (b) [7 Marks]:** A punching machine performs 25 punching operations per minute. Each operation requires 18 kJ of work. The flywheel runs at a mean speed of 240 rpm. The coefficient of fluctuation of speed is 0.20. Determine: (i) The maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy of the flywheel (ii) The mean kinetic energy of the flywheel (iii) The mass of the flywheel if the radius of gyration is 0.6 m **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Punching operations per minute = 25 - Work per operation = 18 kJ = 18,000 J - N = 240 rpm - $C_S$ = 0.20 - k = 0.6 m **Step 1 — Total work done per minute:** $$W_{min} = 25 \times 18{,}000 = 450{,}000 \text{ J/min}$$ **Step 2 — Power required:** $$P = \frac{450{,}000}{60} = 7{,}500 \text{ W} = 7.5 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Time per punching cycle:** $$T_{cycle} = \frac{60}{25} = 2.4 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4 — Energy supplied per cycle (uniform motor power):** $$E_{supplied} = P \times T_{cycle} = 7{,}500 \times 2.4 = 18{,}000 \text{ J}$$ This equals the work per operation since energy is balanced over the cycle. **Step 5 — Maximum fluctuation of energy (occurs during the punching):** For a punching machine, the entire work is done in a small fraction of the cycle, and the flywheel releases all of this energy: $$\Delta E = 18{,}000 \text{ J}$$ **Step 6 — Mean angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi \approx 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 7 — Coefficient of fluctuation of energy:** $$C_E = 2 \, C_S = 2 \times 0.20 = 0.40$$ **Step 8 — Mean kinetic energy of flywheel:** $$E = \frac{\Delta E}{C_E} = \frac{18{,}000}{0.40} = 45{,}000 \text{ J}$$ **Step 9 — Moment of inertia of flywheel:** $$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_E \cdot \omega^2} = \frac{18{,}000}{0.40 \times (25.133)^2} = \frac{18{,}000}{0.40 \times 631.65} = \frac{18{,}000}{252.66} \approx 71.24 \text{ kg·m}^2$$ **Step 10 — Mass of flywheel:** $$m = \frac{I}{k^2} = \frac{71.24}{(0.6)^2} = \frac{71.24}{0.36} \approx 197.9 \text{ kg}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $\Delta E = 18{,}000$ J - (ii) E = 45,000 J - (iii) m ≈ **197.9 kg** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert N (rpm) to ω (rad/s)** — losing 2 marks immediately. Always use $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ for any kinetic energy calculation. > 2. **Using Cs instead of CE in $\Delta E = C \cdot E$** — students often substitute the wrong coefficient. Remember: $\Delta E = C_E \cdot E$, not $C_S \cdot E$. > 3. **In punching machine problems**, the maximum fluctuation of energy equals the work done per operation. This is a frequently tested concept. > 4. **Watch the sign convention**: Speed fluctuation is given as ±x% of mean speed, so $N_1 = N(1 + x/100)$ and $N_2 = N(1 - x/100)$. Then $C_S = 2x/100$. > 5. **For IC engines**, remember the cycle is **2 revolutions for 4-stroke** and **1 revolution for 2-stroke**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize** - [ ] **Cs** = $(N_1 - N_2)/N$ — measures **speed** fluctuation (dimensionless) - [ ] **CE** = $\Delta E / E$ — measures **energy** fluctuation (dimensionless) - [ ] **Master Equation:** $C_E = 2 \, C_S$ (when mean speed is arithmetic mean of N₁ and N₂) - [ ] **Fluctuation of KE:** $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ - [ ] **Mean KE:** $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ - [ ] **Flywheel Sizing Formula:** $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_E \, \omega^2}$ and $m = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_E \, \omega^2 \, k^2}$ - [ ] **Conversion Formula:** $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ (essential — used in every problem) - [ ] **Typical Cs values:** Lathe = 0.01; IC engine = 0.02–0.05; Punch press = 0.10–0.20 - [ ] **Physical interpretation:** A larger flywheel → smaller Cs for the same ΔE - [ ] **CE > Cs always** (factor of 2 for arithmetic mean) - [ ] **For punching machines:** ΔE = Work per operation (all energy released in one shot) - [ ] **For IC engines:** ΔE = Max area between energy supplied and required curves - [ ] **KTU board formula for flywheel mass:** $$m = \frac{\Delta E \times 3600}{C_E \times \pi^2 \times N^2 \times k^2}$$ (using $\omega^2 = (2\pi N/60)^2 = \pi^2 N^2/900$, so the 900 cancels with 3600, giving $4\pi^2 N^2$ in the denominator) - [ ] **In the energy diagram:** Positive loops (Es > El) → flywheel accelerates; Negative loops (Es < El) → flywheel decelerates - [ ] **Mean speed** N = $(N_1 + N_2)/2$ — used to compute mean ω and mean KE - [ ] **Units to remember:** Power in Watts (W) = N·m/s, Energy in Joules (J) = N·m, I in kg·m², ω in rad/s, k in m --- > **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy, Speed** > *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech examination standards.*

topics.title: Energy Stored in a Flywheel, Design of Flywheels, Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Stored in a Flywheel, Design of Flywheels, Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **flywheel** is a massive rotating mechanical energy-storage device, typically in the form of a heavy disc or rim, that stores **rotational kinetic energy** and smooths out the fluctuations in speed of a prime mover or driven machine operating under variable load conditions. The energy stored is purely kinetic and is given by the work-energy theorem applied to a rotating rigid body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exact Definition:** A flywheel is a rotating mass that acts as an *energy reservoir* — it stores excess energy (called the *maximum fluctuation of energy*, $\Delta E$) when the driving torque exceeds the resisting torque, and releases this stored energy when the resisting torque exceeds the driving torque. The flywheel itself does **not** add or create energy; it merely **redistributes** it in time.

The relevant governing equation for the energy stored is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

where:
- $E$ = kinetic energy stored (Joules, J)
- $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the flywheel about its axis of rotation ($\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle up a small hill, then coasting down. To make pedaling effortless on the up-hill, you build up speed on a flat stretch — your bicycle's wheels and your own momentum carry you over the rise without extra effort. The faster you were going, the more easily the hill is climbed.

A **flywheel does exactly this for a machine**:
- When the engine (say, a single-cylinder IC engine) is in its "power stroke," it delivers a strong burst of torque. Some of that energy is used for actual work, and the **excess** is used to *spin up* the flywheel (storing it as kinetic energy).
- When the engine is in its "idle" stroke (intake, compression, exhaust) where it delivers almost no torque, the load is still demanding work. Now the flywheel **gives back** its stored kinetic energy, keeping the shaft spinning smoothly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a flywheel as a **mechanical battery**. A battery stores energy in chemical form; a flywheel stores energy in **rotational kinetic form**. Both deliver energy on demand and accept surplus energy during idle periods.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities & Units

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $E$ | Kinetic energy stored | J (Joule) |
| $I$ | Mass moment of inertia | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$ |
| $\omega$ | Angular speed | rad/s |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | rpm |
| $C_S$ | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | dimensionless |
| $C_E$ | Coefficient of fluctuation of energy | dimensionless |
| $m$ | Mass of flywheel | kg |
| $k$ | Radius of gyration | m |
| $D$ or $R$ | Mean diameter/radius of rim | m |
| $T$ | Torque | N·m |
| $P$ | Power | W (Watt) |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU conversion:** $1 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s}$, so $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi N}{30}$. This conversion is the **#1 source of unit mistakes** in KTU exam answers — guard against it.

## 1.4 Why Flywheels are Needed (Engineering Context)

In machines like **single-cylinder IC engines**, **punch presses**, **rolling mills**, **crank-shafts of reciprocating pumps**, and **textile machinery**, the resisting torque and driving torque are **not uniform** over one cycle. This causes:
1. **Fluctuation of speed** (the shaft speeds up and slows down cyclically).
2. **Fluctuation of energy** (the kinetic energy of the system rises and falls).

Without a flywheel, the speed fluctuations would be so large that the machinery would vibrate, fail, or operate unreliably. The flywheel acts as a **mechanical low-pass filter** for torque disturbances.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fluctuation of Energy — The Core Concept

Consider a machine operating between two extreme angular speeds: $\omega_1$ (maximum) and $\omega_2$ (minimum). The corresponding kinetic energies are:

$$E_1 = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2 \quad \text{(maximum energy stored)}$$

$$E_2 = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2 \quad \text{(minimum energy stored)}$$

The **maximum fluctuation of energy** is the difference:

$$\boxed{\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)}$$

This $\Delta E$ is the amount of energy the flywheel must **absorb or release** in each half-cycle to maintain acceptable speed variation.

## 2.2 Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_S$)

This is a **dimensionless** parameter that expresses the relative speed variation. The KTU-syllabus definition is:

$$\boxed{C_S = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega} = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N}}$$

where $\omega$ (or $N$) is the **mean angular speed** of the flywheel:

$$\omega = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}, \quad N = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$$

Typical $C_S$ values (memorize these — KTU loves them):

| Machine | $C_S$ |
|---|---|
| Punch press, shearing machine | $\approx 0.20$ to $0.30$ |
| Rolling mills | $\approx 0.05$ to $0.10$ |
| Textile machinery | $\approx 0.025$ to $0.05$ |
| Electric motors, generators | $\approx 0.01$ to $0.02$ |
| Crushing machines | $\approx 0.10$ to $0.20$ |
| IC engines (single cylinder) | $\approx 0.05$ to $0.10$ |

## 2.3 Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$)

The fractional change in kinetic energy:

$$\boxed{C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2}{\omega^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board examiner expects you to know that $C_E \neq C_S$. The relationship is derived by expressing $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ in terms of $\omega$ and $C_S$. Let $\omega_1 = \omega + \Delta\omega/2$ and $\omega_2 = \omega - \Delta\omega/2$, where $\Delta\omega = \omega_1 - \omega_2 = C_S \omega$. Substituting:
>
> $$C_E = \frac{(\omega + C_S\omega/2)^2 - (\omega - C_S\omega/2)^2}{\omega^2} = \frac{(2)(2)(\omega)(C_S\omega/2)}{\omega^2} = 2 C_S$$
>
> $$\boxed{C_E = 2 C_S}$$
>
> This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question** and students often confuse it with $C_E = C_S^2$ — DO NOT make that mistake.

## 2.4 Working Equation for Flywheel Mass

Combining $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ with $C_E = 2 C_S$:

$$\Delta E = E \cdot C_E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \cdot (2 C_S) = I \omega^2 C_S$$

Since $I = m k^2$ (where $k$ is the radius of gyration of the flywheel mass), we get:

$$\boxed{\Delta E = m k^2 \omega^2 C_S}$$

Solving for the mass:

$$\boxed{m = \frac{\Delta E}{k^2 \omega^2 C_S}}$$

This is the **master design equation** of a flywheel.

## 2.5 Energy Stored at Mean Speed (Alternative Expression)

KTU also asks for the energy stored *at the mean speed*, which appears frequently in problem statements:

$$E = \frac{\Delta E}{C_E} = \frac{\Delta E}{2 C_S}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Energy stored in rotating mass |
| 2 | $\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = \frac{1}{2} I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ | Max fluctuation of energy |
| 3 | $C_S = (\omega_1 - \omega_2)/\omega = (N_1 - N_2)/N$ | Coeff. of fluctuation of speed |
| 4 | $C_E = \Delta E / E$ | Coeff. of fluctuation of energy |
| 5 | $C_E = 2 C_S$ | **Key relationship** (linear, not quadratic) |
| 6 | $m = \Delta E / (k^2 \omega^2 C_S)$ | Mass of flywheel required |
| 7 | $E = \Delta E / C_E$ | Energy at mean speed |
| 8 | $\Delta E = T_{\text{excess}} \cdot t$ | Energy excess × time duration |
| 9 | $\omega = \pi N / 30$ | Conversion from rpm to rad/s |
| 10 | $P = 2 \pi N T / 60$ | Power from torque (P in W) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam pitfall:** In the formula $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$, NEVER substitute $I = m k^2$ and then mistakenly write $\frac{1}{2} m k^2 \omega^2 C_E$ — the half remains only when $\Delta E$ is expressed in terms of $I$ at the extremes, not the mean. When expressed via $C_S$ at the mean, the half disappears. See derivation in §3.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Prime-mover stabilizers** in IC engines, steam engines, and pumps.
- **Energy storage in hybrid/regenerative systems** (modern flywheel batteries — Williams Formula 1 KERS, Beacon Power grid-flywheels).
- **Process machinery buffers** (punch presses accumulate energy during the slow part of the cycle, then release it as a sudden high-power blow).
- **Inertia testing** in labs (a known flywheel mass and radius can serve as a dynamic calibrator).
- **Dead-stroke / overrun** applications: the flywheel ensures smooth operation even when the driving force briefly drops to zero (e.g., over-center in a four-bar mechanism).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Identity $C_E = 2 C_S$

We start from the definitions:

$$\omega_1 = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2} + \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{2} = \omega + \frac{\Delta\omega}{2}$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2} - \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{2} = \omega - \frac{\Delta\omega}{2}$$

By definition: $\Delta\omega = \omega_1 - \omega_2 = C_S \omega$, so $\frac{\Delta\omega}{2} = \frac{C_S \omega}{2}$.

Substituting into the fluctuation of energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I \left[ \left( \omega + \frac{C_S \omega}{2} \right)^2 - \left( \omega - \frac{C_S \omega}{2} \right)^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I \left[ \left( \omega^2 + C_S \omega^2 + \frac{C_S^2 \omega^2}{4} \right) - \left( \omega^2 - C_S \omega^2 + \frac{C_S^2 \omega^2}{4} \right) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I \left[ 2 C_S \omega^2 \right] \\
&= I \omega^2 C_S
\end{aligned}
$$

The energy at the mean speed is $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_E &= \frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{I \omega^2 C_S}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2} = 2 C_S
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \; C_E = 2 C_S} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $C_E$ is always **twice** $C_S$, never equal to $C_S^2$. This is because for small fluctuations the relationship is linear (Taylor series truncated at first order). Many students fall into the trap of writing $C_E = C_S^2$ — this is wrong.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass of the Flywheel in Terms of Power and Torque

A very common KTU problem gives the **power** of a machine and the work done per stroke (or the torque fluctuations), and asks for the flywheel mass.

For a machine running at $N$ rpm, the work done per cycle is:

$$W_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{P \times 60}{N} \quad \text{(Joules per cycle)}$$

where $P$ is in Watts. If the fluctuation of energy per cycle is $\Delta E$, then:

$$m = \frac{\Delta E}{C_E \cdot \frac{1}{2} k^2 \omega^2} = \frac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot k^2 \omega^2}$$

Now, $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi N}{30}$, so $\omega^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2 N^2}{900}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot k^2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2 N^2}{900}} = \frac{900 \, \Delta E}{C_S \cdot k^2 \cdot \pi^2 N^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{m = \frac{900 \, \Delta E}{\pi^2 C_S k^2 N^2} \approx \frac{91.2 \, \Delta E}{C_S k^2 N^2}}$$

This is the **standard KTU numerical answer** form for flywheel mass when $\Delta E$ is in Joules, $k$ in metres, and $N$ in rpm.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Design of a Solid Disc Flywheel

For a **solid uniform disc** of mass $m$ and outer radius $R$:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m R^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k^2 = \frac{R^2}{2}$$

The maximum tangential (hoop) stress in a rotating thin rim is derived from the centrifugal force on an element. For a solid disc, however, the stress is radial and tangential; the maximum tensile stress at the centre is given by:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{\rho \omega^2 R^2}{8} (3 + \nu)$$

where $\rho$ is density and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. For cast iron ($\nu \approx 0.25$):

$$\sigma_{\max} \approx \frac{3}{8} \rho \omega^2 R^2$$

For a typical design, this $\sigma_{\max}$ is limited to the **allowable stress** $\sigma_t$ of the material.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Design of a Rim (Ring-Type) Flywheel with Arms

A practical flywheel consists of a **rim** (heavy ring) connected to a **hub** via **spokes/arms**. The rim is where most of the mass is concentrated.

### 3.4.1 Centrifugal Tension in the Rim

Consider a thin rim of mean radius $R$, cross-section $A$, density $\rho$. The centrifugal force on a small element $d\theta$ of the rim produces **tangential tension** in the rim. Summing forces on the half-rim (or by the standard "flywheel equation"):

$$T_{\text{rim}} = \frac{m v^2}{2 \pi R} = \frac{m \omega^2 R}{2 \pi}$$

where $m$ is the rim mass. In terms of rim cross-section $A$ and density $\rho$: $m = 2\pi R A \rho$, so:

$$T_{\text{rim}} = \frac{2\pi R A \rho \cdot \omega^2 R}{2 \pi R} = A \rho \omega^2 R^2$$

The tangential (hoop) stress is therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \rho \omega^2 R^2}$$

This is the **most famous KTU formula for flywheel rim design** and is asked as both a 3-mark and 14-mark problem.

### 3.4.2 Stress in the Arms (Spokes)

Each arm carries a portion of the rim's centrifugal load. With $n$ arms, the load per arm is:

$$F_{\text{arm}} = \frac{T_{\text{rim}}}{n} = \frac{A \rho \omega^2 R^2}{n}$$

Considering the arm as a beam fixed at the hub and loaded at the rim, with bending + direct stress, the **maximum bending moment** at the hub end of the arm is:

$$M = F_{\text{arm}} \cdot b \quad (\text{where } b = R - r_{\text{hub}})$$

For an elliptical arm cross-section with semi-axes $a$ (in plane of rotation) and $b'$ (axial):

$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 M b'}{\pi a^3}, \quad \sigma_d = \frac{4 F_{\text{arm}}}{\pi a b'}$$

Total maximum stress in the arm (using the KTU board-exam approach with a bending moment coefficient $K$):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_d + K \cdot \sigma_b$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact value of $K$ depends on whether the arm cross-section is elliptical, rectangular, or I-shaped. The KTU board typically uses an **elliptical arm** with $K = 1$ (i.e., direct summation of stresses).

### 3.4.3 Effect of Shrinkage / Interference Fit

In built-up flywheels, the rim is shrunk onto the arms. This creates a **pre-tension** in the rim ($T_{\text{shrink}}$) that **reduces** the net tensile stress at low speeds but the full effect is complex. The KTU-syllabus equation (often quoted):

$$T_{\text{net}} = T_{\text{centrifugal}} - T_{\text{shrink}}$$

In most KTU numericals, **shrinkage is neglected** unless explicitly mentioned.

## 3.5 Derivative Result — Torque Fluctuation from Turning Moment Diagram

KTU frequently asks: *"Given a turning moment diagram (crank-angle vs. turning moment), find the flywheel mass."* The procedure:

1. From the T-M diagram and the **mean resisting torque line**, identify the area of maximum energy excess $\Delta E$ (in J, since the diagram's integral is in N·m·rad = J, provided the crank-angle axis is in **radians**).
2. Apply $m = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S k^2 \omega^2}$.

> [!TIP]
> If the crank-angle axis is in **degrees**, divide the area by $\dfrac{180}{\pi} = 57.296$ to convert to J/rad. Alternatively, work in N·m·degrees throughout and convert at the end.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Flywheel Design

| Design Parameter | Symbol | Typical KTU Range | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean speed | $N$ | 100–1000 rpm | Given |
| $C_S$ | — | 0.01 to 0.30 | From machine type |
| $\Delta E$ | J | 100 to 10,000 J | From T-M diagram |
| Rim radius | $R$ | 0.1 to 1.0 m | $R = k\sqrt{2}$ (approx) |
| Rim thickness | $t$ | 0.02 to 0.1 m | $\sigma_t = \rho \omega^2 R^2$ |
| Rim width (axial) | $b$ | 0.05 to 0.3 m | $A = m_{\text{rim}} / (2\pi R \rho)$ |
| Number of arms | $n$ | 4, 6, or 8 | From practical convention |
| Arm cross-section | — | Elliptical | $\sigma_b + \sigma_d \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flywheel Block-Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Prime Mover"]:::prime --> node2["Crank Shaft"]
    node2 --> node3["Flywheel Shaft"]
    node3 --> node4["Rim / Outer Ring"]
    node3 --> node5["Arms / Spokes"]
    node3 --> node6["Hub"]
    node5 --> node4
    node6 --> node5
    node4 --> node7["Energy Reservoir KE = 1/2 I w^2"]
    node5 --> node8["Centrifugal Load Path"]
    node6 --> node9["Torque Transfer to Load"]
    node9 --> node10["Driven Machine / Load"]
    node7 --> node11["Smooths Speed Fluctuations"]
    node11 --> node12["Maintains N within N1 and N2"]
    classDef prime fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Flywheel Operating Cycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Cycle] --> B{Compare Driving Torque Td and Resisting Torque Tr}
    B -->|Td greater than Tr| C[Flywheel Accelerates]
    C --> D[Stores Excess Energy DE positive]
    B -->|Tr greater than Td| E[Flywheel Decelerates]
    E --> F[Releases Stored Energy DE negative]
    D --> G[Cycle Continues]
    F --> G
    G --> A
```

## 4.3 Flywheel Component Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RimModule [Rim Module]
        rimA[Mean Radius R]
        rimB[Cross Section Area A]
        rimC[Hoop Stress sigma_t = rho w^2 R squared]
    end
    subgraph ArmModule [Arms Module]
        armA[Number of Arms n]
        armB[Centrifugal Load per Arm F]
        armC[Bending + Direct Stress Check]
    end
    subgraph HubModule [Hub Module]
        hubA[Key / Shrink Fit on Shaft]
        hubB[Transmits Torque to Load]
    end
    RimModule --> ArmModule
    ArmModule --> HubModule
```

## 4.4 Energy Fluctuation vs. Crank Angle (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    diagram[Turning Moment vs Crank Angle Diagram] --> extract[Identify Area of Max Excess above Mean Line]
    extract --> de[Maximum Fluctuation of Energy DE in Joules]
    de --> mass[Compute Mass m = DE / Cs k squared w squared]
    mass --> result[Flywheel Mass Required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These four diagrams collectively represent the **operational logic**, **mechanical structure**, **stress flow path**, and **design workflow** of a flywheel assembly. Together they map every interaction a KTU examiner may ask about in a 14-mark analytical question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: Define the term "coefficient of fluctuation of speed" as applied to a flywheel. State the typical values of this coefficient for (i) a punch press, and (ii) a rolling mill.**

**Model Answer:**

The coefficient of fluctuation of speed, $C_S$, is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed of the flywheel. Mathematically:

$$C_S = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega} = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N}$$

Typical values:
- Punch press / shearing machine: $C_S \approx 0.20$ to $0.30$.
- Rolling mill: $C_S \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$.

(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for values.)

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Establish the relationship between the coefficient of fluctuation of energy ($C_E$) and the coefficient of fluctuation of speed ($C_S$).**

**Model Answer:**

Starting from $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E}$ with $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ and $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$:

$$C_E = \frac{\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2}{\omega^2}$$

Writing $\omega_1 = \omega + \dfrac{C_S \omega}{2}$ and $\omega_2 = \omega - \dfrac{C_S \omega}{2}$ and substituting:

$$C_E = \frac{(\omega + C_S \omega/2)^2 - (\omega - C_S \omega/2)^2}{\omega^2} = 2 C_S$$

$$\boxed{C_E = 2 C_S}$$

(3 marks — 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final result.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q(a) [7 marks]:** A 4-stroke single-cylinder IC engine develops 12 kW at 240 rpm. The turning moment diagram has a maximum fluctuation of energy of 12,000 J above the mean and a deficit of 9,000 J below the mean. Find the mass of the flywheel required if the radius of gyration is 0.5 m. Take $C_S = 0.05$.

**Q(b) [7 marks]:** With the flywheel mass obtained in (a), calculate the maximum and minimum speeds during the cycle, and the coefficient of fluctuation of energy.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Identify the *critical* energy fluctuation. Use the **maximum** of the excess and deficit (since the flywheel must handle the worst case):

$$\Delta E = \max(12{,}000, \; 9{,}000) = 12{,}000 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi \approx 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the flywheel mass formula:

$$m = \frac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot k^2 \cdot \omega^2} = \frac{12{,}000}{0.05 \times (0.5)^2 \times (25.133)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$m = \frac{12{,}000}{0.05 \times 0.25 \times 631.65} = \frac{12{,}000}{7.8957} \approx 1519.8 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 1520 \text{ kg}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** From $\Delta E = I(\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2) = m k^2 (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$:

$$12{,}000 = 1519.8 \times 0.25 \times (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$$

$$\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 = \frac{12{,}000}{379.95} = 31.58 \; \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 2:** Use the mean-speed definition. $\omega = (\omega_1 + \omega_2)/2 = 8\pi \approx 25.133$ rad/s, so $\omega_1 + \omega_2 = 50.265$ rad/s.

**Step 3:** Solve the linear system:

$$\omega_1 - \omega_2 = \frac{\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2}{\omega_1 + \omega_2} = \frac{31.58}{50.265} = 0.6283 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4:** Add and subtract:

$$\omega_1 = 25.133 + 0.314 = 25.447 \text{ rad/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_1 = \frac{25.447 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 243.0 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_2 = 25.133 - 0.314 = 24.819 \text{ rad/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_2 = \frac{24.819 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 237.0 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5:** Coefficient of fluctuation of energy:

$$C_E = 2 C_S = 2 \times 0.05 = 0.10 \quad \text{(i.e., 10%)}$$

$$\boxed{N_1 \approx 243 \text{ rpm}, \quad N_2 \approx 237 \text{ rpm}, \quad C_E = 0.10}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**Q(a) [7 marks]:** Derive the expression for the **centrifugal (hoop) tensile stress** in the rim of a flywheel running at $N$ rpm, in terms of the rim's mean radius $R$, density $\rho$, and cross-sectional area $A$.

**Q(b) [7 marks]:** A rim-type cast-iron flywheel has a mean radius of 0.6 m and a rim cross-section of 75 mm × 100 mm. The flywheel runs at 360 rpm. Density of cast iron is 7200 kg/m³. Calculate (i) the hoop stress in the rim, and (ii) the safety factor if the ultimate tensile strength of cast iron is 200 MPa.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Consider a thin rim of mean radius $R$, cross-sectional area $A$, and material density $\rho$. The rim rotates with angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 2:** For an elemental arc of angle $d\theta$, the mass is:

$$dm = A \cdot R \cdot d\theta \cdot \rho$$

**Step 3:** The centrifugal force on this element is directed radially outward:

$$dF = dm \cdot \omega^2 R = A R^2 \rho \omega^2 \, d\theta$$

**Step 4:** To find the tangential (hoop) tension $T$ in the rim, consider a semicircular half of the rim. The horizontal components of all the $dF$ forces balance the tangential tension at the cut section. Integrating from $-\pi/2$ to $+\pi/2$:

$$2T = \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} dF \cdot \cos\theta = A R^2 \rho \omega^2 \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \, d\theta = A R^2 \rho \omega^2 \cdot [\sin\theta]_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2}$$

$$2T = A R^2 \rho \omega^2 \cdot (1 - (-1)) = 2 A R^2 \rho \omega^2$$

$$T = A R^2 \rho \omega^2$$

**Step 5:** Dividing by the cross-sectional area, the hoop stress is:

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \rho \omega^2 R^2}$$

This is the **governing design equation** for a flywheel rim.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 360}{60} = 12\pi \approx 37.70 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\omega^2 R^2$:

$$\omega^2 R^2 = (12\pi)^2 \times (0.6)^2 = 144 \pi^2 \times 0.36 = 51.84 \pi^2 \approx 511.6 \; \text{(m/s)}^2 \cdot \text{dimensionless}$$

**Step 3:** Hoop stress:

$$\sigma_t = \rho \omega^2 R^2 = 7200 \times 511.6 = 3.683 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t \approx 3.68 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4:** Safety factor:

$$\text{SF} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}}{\sigma_t} = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{3.68 \times 10^6} \approx 54.3$$

$$\boxed{\text{Safety Factor} \approx 54.3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Such a high safety factor is acceptable for a **steady** centrifugal stress, since the loading is deterministic and fatigue accumulation is negligible. (7 + 7 = 14 marks total.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Errors in KTU 2024 Board Exam:**
> 1. **Wrong unit conversion:** Writing $\omega = N$ (treating rpm as rad/s). This single error propagates and may lose **4–6 marks**.
> 2. **Using the wrong fluctuation of energy:** Some students use the *minimum* of the energy excess and deficit instead of the *maximum*. The flywheel must be designed for the *worst* case.
> 3. **Confusing $C_E = 2 C_S$ with $C_E = C_S^2$.** Always derive or state $C_E = 2 C_S$ explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert crank-angle to radians** when $\Delta E$ is read off a turning moment diagram with $\theta$ in degrees.
> 5. **Neglecting the hub-arm stress check:** A flywheel is unsafe if the arms fail in bending even when the rim is safe. The KTU board gives full 7 marks for the arm-stress sub-question — do not skip it.
> 6. **Mixing up mean and maximum speed:** $N = (N_1 + N_2)/2$ is the **mean**, not the **maximum**. Use the mean in $\omega$ in the formula $m = \Delta E / (C_S k^2 \omega^2)$.

## 5.4 Applications of Flywheels (Quick Reference)

| Application | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Single-cylinder IC engines** | Smooths the power delivery between firing strokes |
| **Punch presses & shears** | Stores energy, releases it in a sudden high-power blow |
| **Rolling mills & crushers** | Buffers sudden load peaks, preventing motor stall |
| **Textile & printing machinery** | Maintains uniform speed despite cyclic load |
| **Generators (esp. dynamos)** | Ensures constant frequency output |
| **Hybrid/regenerative vehicles** | Modern KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery Systems) |
| **Energy storage in power grids** | Beacon Power, Amber Kinetics flywheel farms |
| **Dead-stroke mechanisms** | Drives a load over a portion of a cycle where the input is momentarily zero |
| **Gyroscopes & inertial systems** | Spacecraft attitude control, inertial measurement units |
| **Punch / blanking dies** | High-impact forming where the input source cannot deliver peak power |

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A flywheel is a rotating mass that stores kinetic energy $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ and smooths speed fluctuations.
- **Fluctuation of energy:** $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$.
- **Coefficient of fluctuation of speed:** $C_S = (\omega_1 - \omega_2)/\omega$ (dimensionless, depends on machine type).
- **Coefficient of fluctuation of energy:** $C_E = \Delta E / E$ (dimensionless).
- **KEY IDENTITY:** $C_E = 2 C_S$ (always — never $C_S^2$).
- **Master mass equation:** $m = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \, k^2 \, \omega^2}$.
- **Solid disc:** $I = \frac{1}{2} m R^2$, $k^2 = R^2/2$.
- **Rim design:** Centrifugal hoop stress $\sigma_t = \rho \omega^2 R^2$ (memorize — favourite 7-mark derivation).
- **Arms:** Distribute rim load; elliptical cross-section is common; max stress = direct stress + bending stress.
- **Unit conversion pitfall:** Always $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/s, never $N$ in rad/s.
- **Mean speed** is what appears in the design equation, not the maximum speed.
- **Shrinkage fit** reduces net rim tension; usually neglected unless specifically asked.
- **Practical $C_S$ values** (recall table): rolling mill ≈ 0.05–0.10, punch press ≈ 0.20–0.30, electric generator ≈ 0.01–0.02.
- **Applications:** IC engines, punch presses, rolling mills, KERS in vehicles, grid energy storage, gyroscopes.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision check:** If you can (1) write $C_E = 2 C_S$ from memory, (2) derive $\sigma_t = \rho \omega^2 R^2$ in 3 lines, and (3) solve a 14-mark flywheel mass numerical in under 8 minutes, you are fully prepared for any Module 3 question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Governor Mechanisms: Types of Governors, Gravity Controlled - Watt, Porter and Proell Governors, Spring Controlled - Hartnell Governor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Governor Mechanisms: Types of Governors, Gravity Controlled - Watt, Porter and Proell Governors, Spring Controlled - Hartnell Governor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Governor?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **governor** is a mechanical feedback device used to automatically regulate the mean speed of an engine, when there are variations in the load, by appropriately adjusting the fuel (or steam) supply to maintain a nearly constant speed regardless of the load disturbances.

In simple words, a governor is the **"cruise-control system"** of a steam engine or turbine. Imagine you are driving a car on a flat highway and then suddenly climb a steep hill — the engine load increases, and the car begins to slow down. Your foot automatically presses the accelerator to maintain speed. A governor performs the **exact same job** in an engine, but with **purely mechanical (centrifugal) intelligence** — no human or electronics required.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Centrifugal Swing"

Picture two kids sitting on a spinning playground swing. The **faster** the swing rotates, the more the chains **fly outward** due to centrifugal force. If we tie a sleeve (sliding ring) to these chains, the sleeve will **rise** when the engine speeds up and **fall** when it slows down. This rising/falling motion is linked to a fuel valve — more speed → less fuel → less speed. This is the **heart of every centrifugal governor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The Watt Governor (1788) was the very first feedback controller ever built in human history — it preceded electrical control systems by nearly 150 years!

## 1.3 Classification of Governors

Governors are classified based on two main criteria:

### (a) Based on the Control Element
| Control Type | Mechanism | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gravity Controlled** | Uses the **weight of the central load / balls** to provide restoring force | Watt, Porter, Proell |
| **Spring Controlled** | Uses an **elastic spring** to provide restoring force | Hartnell, Hartung, Wilson-Hartnell |
| **Inertia Controlled** | Uses **inertia of the flyballs** (combined with auxiliary weights) | Inertia Governor |

### (b) Based on Construction / Number of Arms
- **Pendulum Type (Simple)** – Arms are simple links pivoted on the governor axis (Watt).
- **Pendulum Type (With Central Load)** – A dead weight is added on the spindle to improve regulation (Porter).
- **Pendulum Type (Compound)** – Uses two arms per ball — a main arm hinged to the spindle and a supplementary arm hinged to the main arm (Proell).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The syllabus specifically covers **Watt, Porter, Proell (gravity-controlled)** and **Hartnell (spring-controlled)** governors. These four form the bedrock of any exam question.

## 1.4 Essential Terminology You Must Memorise

| Term | Meaning | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Equilibrium Speed ($N$)** | The steady speed at which the governor rotates in a given configuration. | The "target speed" of the system. |
| **Minimum Equilibrium Speed ($N_1$)** | Speed at the **minimum radius of rotation** (sleeve at lowest position). | Lowest stable running speed. |
| **Maximum Equilibrium Speed ($N_2$)** | Speed at the **maximum radius of rotation** (sleeve at highest position). | Highest stable running speed. |
| **Mean Equilibrium Speed ($N_m$)** | $(N_1 + N_2)/2$ | Average design speed. |
| **Sensitiveness ($S$)** | $(N_2 - N_1)/N_m$ | Smaller value → more sensitive governor. |
| **Stability** | Governor in which $N_2 > N_1$ | $S > 0$ implies stable. |
| **Isochronism** | $N_1 = N_2$ (i.e., $S = 0$) | Impossible in practice due to friction. |
| **Hunting** | Large, sustained oscillations of the sleeve about the mean position | Caused by over-sensitive governor. |
| **Effort ($F_E$)** | Mean force exerted on the sleeve at mean radius | Indicator of governor's "muscle". |
| **Power ($P$)** | Effort × sleeve lift per minute | $P = F_E \cdot h \cdot N$ |
| **Coefficient of Insensitiveness** | $1/S$ | Used for design comparisons. |
| **Sleeve Lift ($h$)** | Vertical movement of the sleeve between min and max positions | $h = r_2 - r_1$ (varies by governor type). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Centrifugal Force on the Flyball

Consider a ball of mass $m$ rotating about a vertical axis at a radius $r$ with angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s (where $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Equation of ALL Centrifugal Governors:**
> $$\boxed{F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = m \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2 \cdot r = \frac{m \cdot r \cdot N^2}{894.6} \;\; \text{N}}$$
> For quick calculation, KTU books use $894.6 \approx 895$.

This **centrifugal force tries to throw the ball outward**, while **gravity (or spring force) tries to pull it inward**. When these two are equal, the governor is in **equilibrium** — and the corresponding speed is the **equilibrium speed** of the governor for that radius.

## 2.2 Why "895" and Not "900"?

The number $895 = (60/2\pi)^2$ comes from converting the equation:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \implies \omega^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} = \frac{N^2}{894.6}$$

The factor $894.6$ (often rounded to **895** in Indian textbooks) is **universally used in KTU questions**. Always state the assumption clearly: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless otherwise specified.

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table as your single-source reference when solving any governor problem.**

| # | Governor | Governing Equation (N in rpm) | Sleeve Lift $h$ | Sensitiveness $S$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Watt Governor** (no central load) | $N^2 = \dfrac{895}{h}$ | $h = \text{height of governor}$ | $S = \dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N}$ |
| 2 | **Watt Governor** (general, with arm geometry) | $N^2 = \dfrac{895}{h}\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{R}\right)$ | $h = R \cos\alpha + r\cos\beta$ | — |
| 3 | **Porter Governor** (with central load $W$) | $N^2 = \dfrac{895}{h}\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{R}\right)$ | $h = (R + r)\cos\alpha$ when arms vertical at top | $S = \dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N_m}$ |
| 4 | **Porter Governor** (with angle of arms $\alpha$, $\beta$) | $N^2 = \dfrac{895}{h}\left(\dfrac{\tan\alpha + \tan\beta}{1 - \tan\alpha\tan\beta}\right)$ — combined | $h = R\cos\alpha + r\cos\beta$ | — |
| 5 | **Proell Governor** | $N^2 = \dfrac{895}{h}\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{R}\right)$ where $R$ is **upper arm length** | $h = (R+r)\cos\alpha$ | $S = \dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N_m}$ |
| 6 | **Hartnell Governor** (with spring $S_1$, $S_2$, sleeve lift $h_0$) | $N_1^2 = \dfrac{S_1 - W}{m}\cdot\dfrac{r_1 + r_2}{2 r_1^2}\cdot 895$ | $h_0 = r_2 - r_1$ | $S = \dfrac{2(N_2 - N_1)}{N_1 + N_2}$ |
| 7 | **Hartnell — Spring stiffness** | $k = \dfrac{S_2 - S_1}{h_0}$ | — | — |
| 8 | **Effort of Governor** | $F_E = T \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ (Porter) | — | Effort $\uparrow$ as $\alpha \uparrow$ |
| 9 | **Power of Governor** | $P = F_E \cdot h \cdot N$ (Watts) | — | — |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** In the **Hartnell Governor**, the masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ (mass of ball + mass of central load contribution) are **different** at $r_1$ and $r_2$ because of the lever-arm geometry of the bell-crank. Always derive $m_1$ and $m_2$ separately. Many students mistakenly use $m_1 = m_2$ and lose 4–5 marks.

---

## 2.4 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Equation

### Why does the height of the governor $h$ appear in the denominator?

If the engine speeds up, the balls fly out more, the **sleeve rises**, so $h$ **increases**, and from $N^2 \propto 1/h$, the new equilibrium speed **increases** too — perfect regulation! The engine settles at a higher $N$ that matches the new load demand.

### Why does the central load $W$ make the Porter governor "more stable"?

Without $W$ (Watt governor), the only restoring force is the ball's own weight — small. Adding a heavy central load $W$ (Porter) **amplifies the gravitational restoring moment**, so the governor can handle wider speed ranges without becoming unstable.

### Why is the Hartnell governor preferred in modern high-speed engines?

In a high-speed racing engine (cars, aircraft), **gravity becomes too weak** to act as a reliable restoring force (because the centrifugal force scales with $\omega^2$ and far exceeds $mg$ at high speeds). A **spring** can be designed to provide any desired restoring force and is **compact, isochronous-capable, and adjustable** — which is why Hartnell dominates modern governor design.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Watt Governor**: Historical; rarely used now. Found in **low-speed stationary steam engines** (textile mills, small pumps).
- **Porter Governor**: Used in **small-to-medium steam engines** (locomotives, marine engines of the 19th century).
- **Proell Governor**: Used where **higher sensitivity with compact size** is required (older high-speed steam plants).
- **Hartnell Governor**: Industry standard for **modern steam turbines, gas turbines, and high-speed diesel generators**. The **Woodward TG-series** electronic-hydraulic governors are based on the Hartnell principle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Simple Watt Governor

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A simple Watt governor has two balls of mass $m$ each, attached to arms of length $R$ pivoted on the spindle axis. The spindle rotates at $N$ rpm. There is **no central load** on the sleeve.

**Step 1 — Identify the two forces acting on each ball:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Centrifugal force (radial, outward):} \quad F_c &= m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = \frac{m \cdot N^2 \cdot r}{895} \;\; \text{N} \\
\text{Weight of ball (downward):} \quad W_b &= m \cdot g \;\; \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve the weight along and perpendicular to the arm:**

Let $\alpha$ be the angle the arm makes with the spindle axis. The component of weight **along the arm** (restoring) and the centrifugal force **perpendicular to the arm** together produce the equilibrium condition.

For the **vertical equilibrium of the sleeve + ball system**, equating the vertical component of centrifugal force to the weight of the balls:

$$F_c \cdot \cos\alpha = m \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Express the geometry:**

From the figure of the Watt governor, when the balls are at radius $r$ and the arm is at angle $\alpha$ to the vertical:

$$h = R \cos\alpha \quad \text{and} \quad r = R \sin\alpha$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c \cdot \cos\alpha &= m \cdot g \\
\frac{m \cdot N^2 \cdot r}{895} \cdot \cos\alpha &= m \cdot g
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing both sides by $m$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{N^2 \cdot r \cdot \cos\alpha}{895} &= g \\
N^2 &= \frac{895 \cdot g}{r \cdot \cos\alpha}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate $\alpha$ using geometry:**

From $r = R \sin\alpha$, we get $\sin\alpha = r/R$ and $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1 - r^2/R^2}$.

For a Watt governor, the **sleeve is at the pivot point**, so the "height of the governor" is $h = R\cos\alpha$ (the vertical distance from pivot to ball level).

If $r \ll R$ (small angle approximation, common in KTU), then $\cos\alpha \approx 1$, and $h \approx R$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{N^2 = \frac{895 \cdot g}{h} = \frac{895}{h} \quad \text{(since } g = 1 \text{ when using the 895 convention)}}$$

For the **general case** without the small-angle approximation:

$$\boxed{N^2 = \frac{895}{h}\left(1 + \frac{r}{R}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $(1 + r/R)$ in the Watt governor formula accounts for the weight component along the arm direction, which adds a small extra restoring force. In the simplified form, $N^2 = 895/h$ is used.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Porter Governor

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A Porter governor is a Watt governor **with a heavy central load $W$** on the sleeve. Let the arm length be $R$, ball mass $m$, ball radius $r$, and angle of arm with vertical = $\alpha$. The sleeve carries the load $W$ and has arms of length $r$ (extension) hinged to it.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of the Ball:**

The ball experiences:
- Centrifugal force $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r = m N^2 r / 895$ (horizontal, outward).
- Weight $mg$ (downward).

**Step 2 — Force Balance on the Sleeve (vertical equilibrium):**

The central load $W$ acts downward on the sleeve. The reaction on the sleeve comes from the vertical components of the tensions in the two arms.

When the system is in equilibrium, the vertical components of the two ball-forces equal the weight $(W + 2mg)$ on the sleeve.

**Step 3 — Resolution along the arm direction:**

The component of $F_c$ along the arm (which produces a compressive force in the arm) and the component of $mg$ along the arm produce a net force $T$ in the arm:

$$\begin{aligned}
T \cdot \cos\alpha &= \text{vertical force component from ball} \\
F_c \cdot \tan\alpha \cdot \cos\alpha + mg \cdot \sin\alpha &= \text{...} \quad \text{(resolving the components)}
\end{aligned}$$

A cleaner approach uses **moments about the spindle axis pivot point**. Taking moments about the spindle pivot for one ball:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Moment due to centrifugal force} &= F_c \cdot R \cos\alpha \\
\text{Moment due to weight of ball} &= mg \cdot R \sin\alpha \\
\text{Moment due to sleeve load } W &= W \cdot r \cos\alpha \quad \text{(acts on the sleeve arm)}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting net moment = 0 (for equilibrium):

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c \cdot R \cos\alpha &= mg \cdot R \sin\alpha + W \cdot r \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $F_c = m N^2 r / 895$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{m N^2 r}{895} \cdot R \cos\alpha &= mg \cdot R \sin\alpha + W \cdot r \cos\alpha
\end{aligned}$$

Divide through by $R \cos\alpha$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{m N^2 r}{895} &= mg \tan\alpha + W \cdot \frac{r}{R}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Use $h = (R + r)\cos\alpha$ and $\tan\alpha = r/h$:**

For the Porter governor, the **sleeve lift is** $h = (R + r)\cos\alpha$ (total vertical height from pivot to ball).

Therefore: $\tan\alpha = r/h$, and $r/R = r/R$.

After substitution and rearrangement:

$$\boxed{N^2 = \frac{895}{h}\left(1 + \frac{r}{R}\right) \quad \text{(identical form to Watt, but now with load } W \text{ in play)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Favourite):** The **sensitiveness** of a Porter governor can be shown to be:
> $$S = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N_m} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{r_2 - r_1}{h}$$
> Since the numerator $r_2 - r_1$ (sleeve lift contribution) is small compared to $h$, the Porter governor is **moderately sensitive** but rarely isochronous.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Proell Governor

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A Proell governor is a **compound lever governor** with two arms per ball:
> - **Upper arm** of length $R$, hinged on the spindle.
> - **Lower arm** of length $r$, hinged on the upper arm's outer end and on the sleeve.
> - The ball is at the junction of the two arms.
> - Angle of upper arm with vertical = $\alpha$.
> - Angle of lower arm with vertical = $\beta$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry:**

The ball is at radius $r_b = R\sin\alpha$ (measured from spindle axis).
The sleeve is connected via the lower arm, so the sleeve radius is $r_s = R\sin\alpha - r\sin\beta$.

**Step 2 — Force analysis on the ball:**

The ball is at the hinge between the two arms. Forces on the ball:
- Centrifugal force $F_c = m N^2 r_b / 895$ outward.
- Weight $mg$ downward.
- Tension from upper arm (along its length).
- Force from lower arm (along its length).

**Step 3 — Vertical equilibrium of the sleeve:**

The lower arm transmits a force to the sleeve. Taking moments about the hinge point of the upper arm on the spindle, the centrifugal force tries to rotate the upper arm outward, and the lower arm + sleeve load $W$ resist it.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_c \cdot R \cos\alpha &= mg \cdot R \sin\alpha + W \cdot (R\sin\alpha - r\sin\beta) \cdot \frac{\cos\beta}{\cos\alpha} \quad \text{(approx.)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final simplified equation (KTU standard form):**

After simplification, the Proell governor satisfies:

$$\boxed{N^2 = \frac{895}{h}\left(1 + \frac{r}{R}\right)}$$

where $R$ = upper arm length, $r$ = lower arm length, and $h$ = vertical distance from spindle pivot to ball = $R\cos\alpha$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Same algebraic form as Porter, but with the advantage:** The Proell governor is more **sensitive** because the sleeve can move a larger vertical distance for a given ball movement (since the lower arm acts as a lever amplifier). Also, the central load $W$ acts on a longer effective lever arm, increasing the **effort**.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Hartnell Governor (Spring Controlled)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** The Hartnell governor has:
> - Flyballs of mass $m$ each, attached to **bell-crank levers** pivoted on the governor frame.
> - The bell-crank has two arms: a **vertical arm** of length $a$ (acting on the spring) and a **horizontal arm** of length $b$ (carrying the ball).
> - A spring of stiffness $k$ provides the restoring force.
> - The sleeve has a **total lift of $h_0$** corresponding to ball radius change from $r_1$ to $r_2$.
> - At $r_1$ → spring compressed by amount $S_1/k$, ball at lower position.
> - At $r_2$ → spring compressed by $S_2/k$, ball at upper position.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the bell-crank lever:**

When the sleeve moves up by $h_0/2$, the ball moves **outward by $(r_2 - r_1)/2$** and the spring **compresses by $h_0/2$**.

The bell-crank rotates about the pivot, and by similar triangles:
$$\frac{r_2 - r_1}{a} = \frac{h_0}{b} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad h_0 = b \cdot \frac{(r_2 - r_1)}{a}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> In KTU notation, $a$ is the **vertical lever arm** and $b$ is the **horizontal lever arm** (or vice versa depending on the textbook). Always check the convention in your question. The relationship $h_0 = a \cdot (r_2 - r_1)/b$ is what matters.

**Step 2 — Spring force and stiffness:**

The spring force changes linearly with sleeve lift:
$$S_2 = S_1 + k \cdot h_0 \quad \implies \quad k = \frac{S_2 - S_1}{h_0}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium at minimum radius $r_1$:**

The spring force $S_1$ acts on the bell-crank's vertical arm (length $a$). The ball at radius $r_1$ generates centrifugal force $F_{c1} = m N_1^2 r_1 / 895$ acting on the horizontal arm (length $b$). The central sleeve reaction also acts.

By moment equilibrium of the bell-crank about its pivot, and including the sleeve load $W$ acting vertically:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{c1} \cdot b &= S_1 \cdot a + W \cdot a_1 \quad \text{(moments of all vertical/horizontal forces)}
\end{aligned}$$

For simplification, defining the equivalent mass of the ball + sleeve contribution as $m_1$ at radius $r_1$:

$$m_1 = m + \frac{W}{g} \cdot \frac{a}{b}$$

The standard KTU Hartnell equation (as printed in Bhavikatti, Khurmi, and KTU textbooks):

$$\boxed{N_1^2 = \frac{S_1 - \frac{W \cdot a}{b}}{m_1} \cdot \frac{r_1 + r_2}{2 r_1^2} \cdot 895}$$

Similarly for maximum radius:

$$\boxed{N_2^2 = \frac{S_2 - \frac{W \cdot a}{b}}{m_2} \cdot \frac{r_1 + r_2}{2 r_2^2} \cdot 895}$$

where $m_2 = m + \dfrac{W}{g} \cdot \dfrac{a}{b}$ (same as $m_1$ in standard treatment).

**Step 4 — Sensitiveness of Hartnell Governor:**

$$S = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N_m} = \frac{2(N_2 - N_1)}{N_1 + N_2}$$

**Step 5 — Effort of Hartnell Governor:**

The effort is the difference in centrifugal forces on the sleeve at the two extremes:
$$F_E = F_{c2} - F_{c1} = \frac{m(N_2^2 r_2 - N_1^2 r_1)}{895}$$

**Step 6 — Power of Hartnell Governor:**

$$P = F_E \cdot h_0 \cdot N_m = F_E \cdot (r_2 - r_1) \cdot \frac{(N_1 + N_2)}{2} \quad \text{(in W or appropriate units)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The "fingerprint" of a Hartnell problem is: **(i) given $r_1$, $r_2$, $W$, mass of ball, and either $S_1$ or $S_2$; (ii) asked to find the unknown spring force / speed / sensitiveness / effort**. The lever ratio $a/b$ is always given as part of the problem statement.

---

## 3.5 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX

| Property | Watt | Porter | Proell | Hartnell |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Restoring Force** | Gravity (ball weight) | Gravity (ball + central load) | Gravity (compound lever) | Spring |
| **Equation Form** | $N^2 = 895/h$ | $N^2 = (895/h)(1+r/R)$ | $N^2 = (895/h)(1+r/R)$ | Two different eqns. for $N_1$, $N_2$ |
| **Sensitiveness** | Low | Moderate | Higher than Porter | Highest (adjustable via $k$) |
| **Effort** | Small | Medium | Larger (compound lever) | Highest (spring + bell-crank) |
| **Sleeve Lift** | Negligible (no sleeve) | $(R+r)\cos\alpha$ variation | Larger (lower arm amplifies) | $h_0 = (r_2 - r_1) \cdot b/a$ |
| **Modern Use** | Obsolete | Obsolete | Rare | **Industry Standard** |
| **Is it Isochronous?** | No (in practice) | No | No | **Yes (if frictionless)** |
| **Hunting Tendency** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | **High (needs dashpot)** |

## 4.1 Classification Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GOVERNOR MECHANISMS] --> B[Centrifugal Governors]
    A --> C[Inertia Governors]
    A --> D[Axial or Static Governors]

    B --> E[Gravity Controlled]
    B --> F[Spring Controlled]

    E --> G[Watt Governor - Simple Pendulum]
    E --> H[Porter Governor - With Central Load]
    E --> I[Proell Governor - Compound Lever]

    F --> J[Hartnell Governor - Bell Crank]
    F --> K[Hartung Governor]
    F --> L[Wilson Hartnell Governor]

    G --> M[Low Speed Steam Engines Historical]
    H --> N[Locomotives and Marine Engines]
    I --> O[High Speed Steam Plants]
    J --> P[Modern Turbines and Diesel Generators]
    K --> Q[Industrial Compressors]
    L --> R[High Precision Marine Governors]
```

## 4.2 Force Analysis Flow — Hartnell Governor (Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Spindle rotates at N rpm] --> B[Balls fly outward<br/>due to centrifugal force F_c]
    B --> C[Bell crank lever rotates about pivot]
    C --> D[Sleeve moves up or down]
    D --> E{Spring Compression changes}
    E -->|Compresses more| F[Restoring force increases]
    E -->|Relaxes| G[Restoring force decreases]
    F --> H[Equilibrium restored at new N]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Sleeve position linked to fuel valve]
    I --> J[Fuel supply adjusted to engine]
    J --> K[Engine speed stabilises to desired value]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Sensitivity Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SENSITIVENESS COMPARISON] --> B[Watt: S is very low]
    A --> C[Porter: S is moderate]
    A --> D[Proell: S is moderate to high]
    A --> E[Hartnell: S is high and adjustable]
    A --> F[Isochronous: S equals 0<br/>only in ideal Hartnell]
    A --> G[Unstable: S less than 0<br/>never used in practice]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Block — Sensitiveness Categories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Governors] --> B{N2 compared to N1}
    B -->|N2 greater than N1| C[STABLE<br/>S greater than 0]
    B -->|N2 equals N1| D[ISOCHRONOUS<br/>S equals 0<br/>Ideal but impossible with friction]
    B -->|N2 less than N1| E[UNSTABLE<br/>S less than 0<br/>Hunting occurs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** Each block above corresponds to a specific category that examiners love to ask in Part A (3-mark) questions — *"Classify governors based on sensitiveness with examples"* is a **guaranteed KTU question**.
