# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are short, direct, definition-or-formula-based. No derivation needed, but a precise 3–4 line answer is expected.

---

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define natural frequency of vibration. State the expression for natural frequency of an undamped SDOF system in terms of equivalent mass and equivalent stiffness.**

**Model Answer:**

The natural frequency of a vibrating system is the frequency at which the system vibrates freely when displaced from its equilibrium position and released, under the action of restoring forces only (no external excitation).

For an undamped SDOF system with equivalent mass $m_{eq}$ and equivalent stiffness $k_{eq}$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_{eq}}{m_{eq}}} \quad \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{eq}}{m_{eq}}} \quad \text{Hz}$$

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Distinguish between longitudinal, transverse, and torsional vibrations with one practical example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Direction of Motion | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal | Along the axis of the member | Axial vibration of a connecting rod in an engine |
| Transverse | Perpendicular to the axis | Bending vibration of a cantilever beam supporting a turbine blade |
| Torsional | Rotational about the axis | Twisting vibration of a propeller shaft in a marine engine |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions have sub-parts (typically a: 7 marks + b: 7 marks). Either Option A or Option B must be answered. Full derivations and final boxed answers are mandatory.

---

### **Question B.1 — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**A uniform steel shaft of length 1.2 m and diameter 40 mm is fixed at one end. A disc of mass moment of inertia 12 kg·m² is keyed at the free end. Take $G = 80$ GPa.**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for torsional vibration of a single degree of freedom system using Newton's method. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Calculate the natural frequency of torsional vibration of the above shaft. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**System:** Shaft fixed at one end, disc at free end, twisting by angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Stiffness of shaft:**

Polar moment of inertia of shaft:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.040)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

Torsional stiffness:

$$k_t = \frac{GJ}{L} = \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times 2.513 \times 10^{-7}}{1.2} = 1.676 \times 10^4 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Step 2 — Equation of motion (Newton's law for rotation):**

The restoring torque in the shaft is $T = -k_t \theta$. Equating to rate of change of angular momentum:

$$I\ddot{\theta} = -k_t \theta$$

$$I\ddot{\theta} + \frac{GJ}{L}\theta = 0$$

**Step 3 — Assume harmonic solution:**

$$\theta(t) = \Theta \sin(\omega_n t + \phi) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ddot{\theta} = -\omega_n^2 \theta$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve:**

$$-I\omega_n^2 \theta + \frac{GJ}{L}\theta = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{GJ}{LI}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{GJ}{LI}}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $L = 1.2$ m, $d = 0.040$ m, $I = 12$ kg·m², $G = 80 \times 10^9$ Pa

**Step 1 — Polar moment of inertia of shaft:**

$$J = \frac{\pi (0.040)^4}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Natural angular frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{GJ}{LI}} = \sqrt{\frac{80 \times 10^9 \times 2.513 \times 10^{-7}}{1.2 \times 12}}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{20106.2}{14.4}} = \sqrt{1396.3} = 37.37 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Natural frequency in Hz:**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{37.37}{2\pi} = 5.95 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Time period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_n} = 0.168 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = 37.37 \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_n = 5.95 \text{ Hz}, \quad T = 0.168 \text{ s}}$$

---

### **Question B.1 — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A simply supported steel beam of length 2 m carries a concentrated mass of 50 kg at its mid-span. Take $E = 200$ GPa and $I = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m⁴. Neglect beam mass.**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the natural frequency of transverse vibration of a simply supported beam with a central concentrated load using the energy method. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Determine the natural frequency of transverse vibration of the above beam. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**System:** Simply supported beam, length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, central point mass $M$.

**Step 1 — Stiffness:**

For a simply supported beam with central point load $P$, the static deflection at the center is:

$$\delta = \frac{PL^3}{48EI}$$

Therefore, the equivalent spring stiffness is:

$$k = \frac{P}{\delta} = \frac{48EI}{L^3}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Potential Energy (Strain Energy):**

For central deflection $X$ (amplitude):

$$U_{max} = \frac{1}{2}kX^2 = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{48EI}{L^3}\cdot X^2 = \frac{24EI}{L^3}X^2$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Kinetic Energy:**

Assuming $y(t) = X\cos(\omega_n t)$ at the center, $\dot{y}_{max} = \omega_n X$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{1}{2}M\omega_n^2 X^2$$

(Beam mass neglected as per problem statement.)

**Step 4 — Apply conservation $T_{max} = U_{max}$:**

$$\frac{1}{2}M\omega_n^2 X^2 = \frac{24EI}{L^3}X^2$$

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{48EI}{ML^3}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{48EI}{ML^3}}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $L = 2$ m, $M = 50$ kg, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $I = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m⁴

**Step 1 — Compute equivalent stiffness:**

$$k = \frac{48EI}{L^3} = \frac{48 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{(2)^3} = \frac{48 \times 10^6}{8} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 2 — Natural angular frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 10^6}{50}} = \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^5} = 346.41 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Natural frequency in Hz:**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{346.41}{2\pi} = 55.13 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Time period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_n} = 0.01814 \text{ s} = 18.14 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = 346.41 \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_n = 55.13 \text{ Hz}, \quad T = 18.14 \text{ ms}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Diameter must be in **meters** (not mm) when computing $J$. $G$ must be in **Pa** (not GPa). A common error is $G = 80$ GPa being used directly — this gives a wrong answer by a factor of $10^9$.
> 2. **Mixing up $I$ and $J$:** In torsion problems, $I$ refers to the **mass moment of inertia of the disc** (kg·m²), and $J$ refers to the **polar moment of inertia of the shaft cross-section** (m⁴). These are entirely different quantities.
> 3. **Wrong formula for stiffness:** Students often write $k = AE/L$ for transverse problems. The correct stiffness for a simply supported beam with central load is $48EI/L^3$, not $AE/L$.
> 4. **Missing amplitude in energy method:** When equating $T_{max} = U_{max}$, the amplitude $X^2$ cancels — students must explicitly show this cancellation step to earn full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to compute $f_n$ from $\omega_n$:** The question asks for natural frequency in Hz. Students often stop at $\omega_n$ (rad/s) and lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **SDOF** = Single Degree of Freedom = system defined by **one** independent coordinate.
- ☐ **Free vibration** = no external force after initial disturbance.
- ☐ **Undamped** = no energy loss; energy is conserved ($T_{max} = U_{max}$).
- ☐ **Universal natural frequency formula:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_{eq}/m_{eq}}$. **Memorize this.**
- ☐ **Three vibration types:**
  - Longitudinal: $k = \frac{AE}{L}$
  - Transverse (cantilever): $k = \frac{3EI}{L^3}$
  - Transverse (simply supported): $k = \frac{48EI}{L^3}$
  - Transverse (fixed-fixed): $k = \frac{192EI}{L^3}$
  - Torsional: $k_t = \frac{GJ}{L}$
- ☐ **Polar moment of inertia for solid shaft:** $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$.
- ☐ **Mass moment of inertia of disc:** $I = \frac{MR^2}{2}$ (for solid disc) — given as data in most problems.
- ☐ **Two solution methods:** Newton's Method (force/moment balance) and Energy Method (Rayleigh's $T_{max} = U_{max}$). **Both must give the same $\omega_n$ for a given system.**
- ☐ **Conversion:** $f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi}$ Hz, $T = \frac{1}{f_n}$ seconds.
- ☐ **Units check:** $k$ in N/m, $m$ in kg → $\omega_n$ in rad/s. $k_t$ in N·m/rad, $I$ in kg·m² → $\omega_n$ in rad/s.
- ☐ **Energy method advantage:** handles distributed mass systems easily via integration of $m(x) y^2(x)$ and $EI [y''(x)]^2$.
- ☐ **Rayleigh's method limitation:** Slightly overestimates $\omega_n$ (because assumed mode shape is not exact), but converges to correct value as mode shape improves.
- ☐ **Resonance avoidance:** Operating frequency $\omega$ should be at least 20% away from $\omega_n$ (i.e., frequency ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$ should be $< 0.8$ or $> 1.2$).
- ☐ **KTU standard mark split:** Part (a) of a 14-mark question = 7 marks (derivation), Part (b) = 7 marks (numerical). Always show **Given Data**, **Formula**, **Substitution**, **Final Answer** in a box.
- ☐ **Common SI prefix pitfalls:** $E$ for steel = $200$ GPa = $2 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $G$ for steel = $80$ GPa = $8 \times 10^{10}$ Pa.

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — UNDAMPED FREE VIBRATIONS OF SDOF SYSTEMS**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — Damped Vibratory Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Damped System?

A **damped vibrating system** is a single degree of freedom (SDOF) system in which energy is progressively dissipated to the surroundings by an external resistive force, called a **damping force**. The damping force always acts opposite to the velocity of the moving mass and is mathematically proportional to the velocity raised to a power (commonly, the first power — *viscous damping*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (SDOF Damped Free Vibration):**
> *"A damped system is a spring-mass system in which a damper (dashpot) is introduced to dissipate vibrational energy, causing the amplitude of oscillation to decay exponentially with time until the system comes to rest at the equilibrium position."*

The standard SDOF damped model consists of three elements:

| Element | Symbol | Function |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Mass (inertia) | $m$ | Stores kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ |
| Spring (stiffness) | $k$ | Stores potential energy $\tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$ |
| Damper (dashpot) | $c$ | Dissipates energy as heat, force = $c\dot{x}$ |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Push on a Soggy Mattress"

Imagine you push a child sitting on a swing. Without friction, the swing goes back and forth forever. Now imagine the swing is immersed in a pool of thick oil (the **damper**). Every time the swing moves, the oil resists the motion, sapping energy bit by bit. Eventually, the swing settles at the bottom. The "**oil resistance**" is exactly analogous to a **dashpot**, and the rate at which the oscillation dies is governed by how thick (viscous) the oil is — the **damping coefficient $c$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of dampers:**
> - Car shock absorbers (hydraulic dashpots)
> - Door closers (pneumatic dampers)
> - Earthquake dampers in skyscrapers (tuned mass dampers)
> - Rubber/elastomer bushings in machinery mounts

### 1.3 Why Damping Matters in Engineering

Without damping, every machine foundation, vehicle suspension, and building would resonate catastrophically at its natural frequency. Damping is **engineered intentionally** to:
1. Limit resonance amplitudes.
2. Reduce noise and wear.
3. Provide comfort (vehicle suspension).
4. Ensure safe shutdown of vibrating equipment.

The **standard dimensionless measure** of damping is the **damping ratio** $\zeta$ (zeta), which compares actual damping to a critical reference value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two key constants to memorize for KTU exams:**
> - **Damping coefficient ($c$)** — units: **N·s/m**
> - **Critical damping coefficient ($c_c$)** — units: **N·s/m**, equals $2\sqrt{km}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Equation of Motion for a Damped SDOF System

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $c$, free to move on a frictionless horizontal surface.

Applying Newton's second law in the direction of motion:

$$\sum F = m \ddot{x}$$

The forces acting on the mass are:
- Spring restoring force: $F_s = -kx$ (acts opposite to displacement)
- Damping force: $F_d = -c\dot{x}$ (acts opposite to velocity)
- Applied external force: $F(t)$ (zero for free vibration)

Therefore, the **equation of motion** for a damped free vibrating system is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

This is a **second-order linear homogeneous ordinary differential equation** with constant coefficients.

## 2.2 Non-Dimensionalization — Why We Divide by Mass

Dividing the entire equation by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

We now define two pivotal constants:

- **Natural (undamped) angular frequency** $\omega_n$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

- **Damping ratio** $\zeta$ (the *dimensionless* measure of damping):

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \frac{c}{c_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $c_c$ is the **critical damping coefficient** — the precise value of $c$ that makes the system return to equilibrium in the *shortest possible time* without oscillating.

Substituting these into the equation:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

This is the **canonical KTU form** of the damped free vibration equation.

## 2.3 Solution via the Characteristic Equation

Assume a solution of the form $x(t) = e^{st}$. Substituting into the canonical equation:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

The nature of the roots is dictated by the **sign inside the square root**, which depends entirely on $\zeta$.

## 2.4 Classification of Damped Systems

| Regime | Condition | Roots $s_{1,2}$ | Behaviour |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Undamped** | $\zeta = 0$ | Purely imaginary $\pm i\omega_n$ | Sustained oscillation, constant amplitude |
| **Under-damped** | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Complex conjugates | Decaying sinusoidal oscillation |
| **Critically damped** | $\zeta = 1$ | Real, equal: $-\omega_n$ (double) | Fastest return **without oscillation** |
| **Over-damped** | $\zeta > 1$ | Real, distinct, negative | Slow non-oscillatory return |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The vast majority of textbook problems in Module 4 deal with the **under-damped** case, because real engineering structures (machines, vehicles) are intentionally designed with $\zeta \ll 1$ so that they oscillate briefly but safely before settling.

## 2.5 The Damped Natural Frequency $\omega_d$

For the **under-damped** case, define:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

This is the angular frequency at which the system *actually* oscillates while decaying. The general solution is:

$$x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

where:
- $X_0$ = initial amplitude (m)
- $\phi$ = phase angle (rad), determined by initial conditions
- The factor $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ is the **decay envelope**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** Even though damping reduces the oscillation frequency from $\omega_n$ to $\omega_d$, the difference is negligible for small $\zeta$ (e.g., $\zeta = 0.1$ gives $\omega_d \approx 0.995\omega_n$).

## 2.6 Critical Damping in Depth

The **critical damping coefficient** $c_c$ is defined as the *minimum* damping that prevents any oscillation. From $\zeta = 1$:

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n = \frac{2k}{\omega_n}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- If $c < c_c$: the system oscillates (under-damped).
- If $c = c_c$: the system returns to equilibrium as fast as possible *without* overshooting.
- If $c > c_c$: the system is "sluggish" and returns even more slowly (over-damped).

**Engineering applications of critical damping:**
- Precision instrument galvanometers (need fast, non-oscillatory pointer return).
- Gun recoil mechanisms (must reach target position quickly without rebound).
- Door-closing mechanisms (avoiding the "slam" or oscillating stop).

## 2.7 Logarithmic Decrement — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logarithmic Decrement ($\delta$):** The natural logarithm of the ratio of any two successive peak amplitudes (in the same direction) of an under-damped free vibration.

Mathematically, if $x_1$ and $x_2$ are two successive peaks separated by one time period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right)$$

Equivalently, if $x_n$ and $x_{n+1}$ are amplitudes $n$ cycles apart:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

## 2.8 Derivation of the Logarithmic Decrement Formula

The amplitude of the $n$-th peak is:

$$x_n = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n n T_d}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{x_0}{x_n} = e^{\zeta\omega_n n T_d} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \delta = \zeta\omega_n T_d$$

Since $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

Inverse relation (for small $\zeta$, $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$):

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Experimental Use:** $\delta$ is measured in the lab by recording two successive amplitudes on a strip-chart recorder. From it, $\zeta$ and hence $c$ can be calculated — this is the **standard method** to find the damping coefficient of a real machine.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Equation of motion | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ | — |
| 2 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Period (undamped) | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ | s |
| 4 | Critical damping coeff. | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | N·s/m |
| 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = c/c_c$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Damped period | $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ | s |
| 8 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Inverse relation | $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2+\delta^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | $n$-cycle decay | $x_n = x_0 \cdot e^{-n\delta}$ | m |
| 11 | Quality factor (Q) | $Q = 1/(2\zeta) = \pi/\delta$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Logarithmic decrement is a *direct, experimentally measurable* parameter. It allows practicing engineers to characterize the damping of a structure (e.g., a building, a turbine blade, a vehicle suspension) without ever knowing $m$, $k$, or $c$ individually.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the General Under-Damped Solution

### Step 1: Start with the canonical differential equation
We begin with the KTU standard form:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

### Step 2: Propose a trial exponential solution
For linear constant-coefficient ODEs, the trial solution is:

$$x(t) = e^{st} \quad \text{where } s \in \mathbb{C}$$

Differentiating:

$$\dot{x} = s e^{st}, \qquad \ddot{x} = s^2 e^{st}$$

### Step 3: Substitute back into the ODE
Plugging these into the canonical equation:

$$s^2 e^{st} + 2\zeta\omega_n (s e^{st}) + \omega_n^2 (e^{st}) = 0$$

Factoring out the common non-zero term $e^{st}$:

$$e^{st}\left(s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2\right) = 0$$

Since $e^{st} \neq 0$ for any finite $t$, the bracket must vanish:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve the characteristic equation
Apply the quadratic formula:

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{(2\zeta\omega_n)^2 - 4\omega_n^2}}{2}$$

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{4\omega_n^2(\zeta^2 - 1)}}{2}$$

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

### Step 5: Interpret the under-damped case ($\zeta < 1$)
For $\zeta < 1$, $\zeta^2 - 1 < 0$, so the square root is imaginary. Writing $\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} = i\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\,\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d$$

### Step 6: Construct the real general solution
The general complex solution is:

$$x(t) = C_1 e^{s_1 t} + C_2 e^{s_2 t}$$

Substituting $s_1$ and $s_2$:

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[C_1 e^{i\omega_d t} + C_2 e^{-i\omega_d t}\right]$$

### Step 7: Apply Euler's formula and use real initial conditions
Using $e^{i\omega_d t} = \cos(\omega_d t) + i\sin(\omega_d t)$ and combining constants, the **real solution** becomes:

$$\boxed{x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)}$$

where:

$$X_0 = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\dot{x}_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}{\omega_d}\right)^2 + x_0^2}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x_0 \omega_d}{\dot{x}_0 + \zeta\omega_n x_0}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Logarithmic Decrement Formula

### Step 1: Write the under-damped solution again
$$x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

### Step 2: Identify the peak amplitudes
Peaks occur when $\cos(\omega_d t - \phi) = \pm 1$. The $n$-th peak occurs at $t = nT_d + t_1$ where $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ and $t_1$ is the time of the first peak. At each peak, the cosine magnitude is 1, so the peak amplitude is purely the exponential envelope value:

$$x_n = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n (nT_d + t_1)} = x_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n n T_d}$$

### Step 3: Form the ratio of two successive peaks
$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = \frac{X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n (nT_d + t_1)}}{X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n ((n+1)T_d + t_1)}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

### Step 4: Take the natural logarithm
$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d$$

### Step 5: Substitute $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$
$$\delta = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$$

### Step 6: Substitute $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
$$\delta = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

The $\omega_n$ terms cancel cleanly:

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

### Step 7: Derive the inverse formula (to extract $\zeta$ from measured $\delta$)

Starting from $\delta^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2\zeta^2}{1-\zeta^2}$:

$$\delta^2 (1-\zeta^2) = 4\pi^2\zeta^2$$

$$\delta^2 - \delta^2\zeta^2 = 4\pi^2\zeta^2$$

$$\delta^2 = \zeta^2(\delta^2 + 4\pi^2)$$

$$\zeta^2 = \frac{\delta^2}{\delta^2 + 4\pi^2}$$

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{\delta^2 + 4\pi^2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For small $\zeta$** (which is the engineering reality, $\zeta \le 0.1$): $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$, so $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$. This linear approximation is widely used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Four Damping Regimes

| Feature | Undamped | Under-damped | Critically Damped | Over-damped |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\zeta$ | 0 | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | $\zeta = 1$ | $\zeta > 1$ |
| Roots $s$ | $\pm i\omega_n$ | $-\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d$ | $-\omega_n$ (double) | $-\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2-1}$ |
| Solution form | $\cos(\omega_n t - \phi)$ | $X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$ | $(A+Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$ | $A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}$ |
| Oscillates? | Yes, forever | Yes, decays | No | No |
| Settling speed | Never settles | Moderate | **Fastest non-oscillatory** | Slowest |
| Energy | Conserved | Decays exponentially | Decays fastest without overshoot | Decays slowly |
