# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define logarithmic decrement. Derive the expression $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ for a damped vibrating system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Logarithmic decrement $\delta$ is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes of a damped free vibration:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right)$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** For an under-damped SDOF system, the displacement is:

$$x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

The peak at cycle $n$ is $x_n = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n nT_d}$ and the next is $x_{n+1} = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n (n+1)T_d}$.

Their ratio:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

Taking natural log:

$$\delta = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between critically damped and over-damped systems. Why is critical damping preferred in precision instruments?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Critically Damped | Over-Damped |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = 1$ | $\zeta > 1$ |
| Nature of roots | Real, equal ($-\omega_n$, double) | Real, distinct, negative |
| Response | Returns to equilibrium in **shortest time** | Returns **slower** (sluggish) |
| Overshoot? | **No** | No |

**Why preferred in precision instruments (1 mark):** Instruments like galvanometers and ammeters must show the correct reading *as fast as possible* without oscillating back and forth past the true value. Critical damping provides the **fastest non-oscillatory** return, giving accurate readings in minimum time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** The equation of motion of a damped vibrating system is $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$. Define **damping ratio** and **critical damping coefficient**. Derive the expression for the critical damping coefficient in terms of mass and stiffness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine of mass 50 kg is mounted on a spring and damper system. The stiffness of the spring is $2 \times 10^4$ N/m and the damping coefficient is 400 N·s/m. Determine:
- (i) the natural frequency,
- (ii) the critical damping coefficient,
- (iii) the damping ratio,
- (iv) the damped natural frequency, and
- (v) the logarithmic decrement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Damping ratio ($\zeta$):** The damping ratio is the ratio of the actual damping coefficient $c$ to the critical damping coefficient $c_c$:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c}$$

It is a **dimensionless number** that classifies the damping regime.

**Critical damping coefficient ($c_c$):** It is the *minimum* value of damping that makes a system return to equilibrium **without any oscillation**, in the shortest possible time. Mathematically, it is the value of $c$ for which the characteristic equation has **equal real roots**.

Starting from the characteristic equation:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

For equal real roots, the discriminant must be zero:

$$(2\zeta\omega_n)^2 - 4\omega_n^2 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 4\omega_n^2(\zeta^2 - 1) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \zeta = 1$$

From the definition $\zeta = c/c_c$, at $\zeta = 1$:

$$c_c = c_{\text{actual}}\big|_{\zeta=1}$$

Substituting $s = -\omega_n$ (the repeated root) into the characteristic equation $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$ with $\zeta=1$:

$$s^2 + 2\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = (s+\omega_n)^2 = 0 \quad\checkmark$$

Now, from $\zeta = 1$:

$$\frac{c}{2m\omega_n} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad c = 2m\omega_n = 2m\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

$$\boxed{c_c = 2\sqrt{km}} \qquad \text{(3 marks for derivation)}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 50$ kg, $k = 2 \times 10^4$ N/m, $c = 400$ N·s/m.

**(i) Natural frequency (1 mark):**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^4}{50}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Critical damping coefficient (1 mark):**
$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 10^4 \times 50} = 2\sqrt{10^6} = 2 \times 1000 = 2000 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**(iii) Damping ratio (1 mark):**
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{400}{2000} = 0.2$$

Since $\zeta < 1$, the system is **under-damped** (oscillatory with decay).

**(iv) Damped natural frequency (2 marks):**
$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1 - 0.04} = 20\sqrt{0.96} = 20 \times 0.9798 = 19.596 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(v) Logarithmic decrement (2 marks):**
$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.2}{\sqrt{0.96}} = \frac{1.2566}{0.9798} \approx 1.2825$$

**Verification using the small-$\zeta$ approximation:** $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta = 2\pi(0.2) = 1.2566$ — close to the exact value, as expected for small $\zeta$.

---

### 📝 Question B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define **logarithmic decrement**. The amplitude of a vibrating system reduces from 20 mm to 0.5 mm in 8 cycles. Calculate the **(i)** logarithmic decrement, **(ii)** damping ratio, and **(iii)** the quality factor $Q$ of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of mass 100 kg is supported on two springs, each of stiffness $2 \times 10^5$ N/m. The system has a damping coefficient of $1.5 \times 10^3$ N·s/m. Determine whether the system is under-damped, critically damped, or over-damped. If under-damped, find the **damped period** of vibration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Logarithmic Decrement Problem (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Logarithmic decrement is the natural log of the ratio of successive peak amplitudes in a damped free vibration:
$$\delta = \frac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x_n}\right)$$

**Given:** $x_0 = 20$ mm, $x_n = 0.5$ mm, $n = 8$ cycles.

**(i) Logarithmic decrement (2 marks):**
$$\delta = \frac{1}{8}\ln\!\left(\frac{20}{0.5}\right) = \frac{1}{8}\ln(40) = \frac{1}{8}(3.6889) = 0.4611$$

**(ii) Damping ratio (2 marks):**
Using $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$, solving for $\zeta$:
$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}} = \frac{0.4611}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + 0.4611^2}}$$

$$4\pi^2 = 39.4784, \qquad 0.4611^2 = 0.2126$$

$$\zeta = \frac{0.4611}{\sqrt{39.6910}} = \frac{0.4611}{6.3001} = 0.0732$$

So the system is **lightly damped** ($\zeta \approx 7.3\%$). *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Quality factor (2 marks):**
$$Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.0732} = 6.83$$

Or equivalently, $Q = \pi/\delta = 3.1416/0.4611 = 6.81$ ✓ (matches within rounding).

---

**Part (b) — Damping Regime Classification (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 100$ kg, total stiffness $k = 2 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 4 \times 10^5$ N/m (springs in parallel), $c = 1.5 \times 10^3$ N·s/m.

**Step 1: Natural frequency (1 mark):**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^5}{100}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.246 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Critical damping coefficient (1 mark):**
$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{4 \times 10^5 \times 100} = 2\sqrt{4 \times 10^7} = 2 \times 6324.6 = 12649.1 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Step 3: Damping ratio (1 mark):**
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1500}{12649.1} = 0.1186$$

**Step 4: Classify (1 mark):** Since $\zeta = 0.1186 < 1$, the system is **under-damped**.

**Step 5: Damped natural frequency (1 mark):**
$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 63.246\sqrt{1 - 0.01406} = 63.246\sqrt{0.9859} = 63.246 \times 0.9929 = 62.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6: Damped period (2 marks):**
$$T_d = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{2\pi}{62.80} = \frac{6.2832}{62.80} = 0.1001 \text{ s} \approx 100 \text{ ms}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to double the spring stiffness** when two springs are in *parallel* (as in Question B part b). Students often use $k = 2 \times 10^5$ instead of $4 \times 10^5$, giving the wrong $\omega_n$ and cascading errors.
> 2. **Mixing up the periods** — $T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$ is the *undamped* period; $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ is the *damped* period. The question may ask for either; read carefully!
> 3. **Using the small-$\zeta$ approximation** $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ when $\zeta$ is *not* small. Always check if $\zeta > 0.3$ — then you must use the exact formula.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in the final answer. $\omega_n$ must be in **rad/s**, $T_d$ in **seconds**, and $c_c$ in **N·s/m**.
> 5. **Not classifying the system** before solving. Always state whether the system is under-, critically, or over-damped. Examiners allocate a *specific mark* for this step.
> 6. **Sign errors in the characteristic equation** — remember the equation is $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$, **not** $s^2 - 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$. The damping term is *positive* in the canonical form.
> 7. **Confusing $\delta$ with $\zeta$** in final answers. $\delta$ is the *logarithmic decrement* (dimensionless, typically 0.1–1.5); $\zeta$ is the *damping ratio* (dimensionless, typically 0.01–0.3).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Equation of motion:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ (free damped vibration). ✅
- **Canonical form:** $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$. ✅
- **Natural frequency** (always): $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — **independent of damping**. ✅
- **Critical damping coefficient:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$. ✅
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/c_c$. Four regimes: $0$ (undamped), $0<\zeta<1$ (under), $1$ (critical), $>1$ (over). ✅
- **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ — only meaningful for under-damped. ✅
- **General under-damped solution:** $x(t) = X_0 e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$. ✅
- **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = \ln(x_n/x_{n+1}) = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$. ✅
- **Inverse relation:** $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$; for small $\zeta$, $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$. ✅
- **$n$-cycle amplitude:** $x_n = x_0 e^{-n\delta}$ — useful for "amplitude reduces to Y% after N cycles" problems. ✅
- **Quality factor:** $Q = 1/(2\zeta) = \pi/\delta$ — a quick cross-check. ✅
- **Critical damping** = fastest *non-oscillatory* return; preferred in precision instruments. ✅
- **Springs in parallel** add up: $k_{\text{eq}} = k_1 + k_2$. **Springs in series**: $1/k_{\text{eq}} = 1/k_1 + 1/k_2$. ✅
- **Always classify** the system using $\zeta$ *before* choosing the solution form. ✅
- **Unit check** is a free 0.5 mark — never skip the final dimensional verification. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forced Harmonic Vibration, Response of Reciprocating and Rotating Unbalance, Magnification Factor, Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Forced Harmonic Vibration, Response of Reciprocating and Rotating Unbalance, Magnification Factor, Resonance # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Forced Harmonic Vibration** is a sustained oscillatory motion of a mechanical system in which a time-varying external force (called the *forcing function* or *excitation*) continuously supplies energy to the system, causing it to respond at the frequency of the excitation rather than at its own natural frequency. Mathematically, for a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) viscous damped system, the equation of motion is: $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ Where: - $m$ = equivalent mass of the system (**kg**) - $c$ = equivalent viscous damping coefficient (**Ns/m**) - $k$ = equivalent stiffness of the system (**N/m**) - $F_0$ = amplitude of the external harmonic force (**N**) - $\omega$ = angular frequency of excitation (**rad/s**) - $x$ = instantaneous displacement from equilibrium position (**m**) - $\dot{x}$ = instantaneous velocity (**m/s**) - $\ddot{x}$ = instantaneous acceleration (**m/s²**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Forced vibration is **distinct** from free vibration because the system is *not* allowed to oscillate at its natural frequency; instead, the **forcing frequency ω dictates the response frequency**. The system reaches a **steady state** after the transient free-vibration part dies out due to damping. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pushed Swing" Imagine a child sitting on a swing. Three scenarios can occur: | Scenario | Analogy | Engineering Equivalent | |----------|---------|------------------------| | Child swings naturally after one push | Free vibration (decays due to air drag) | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ | | Parent pushes the swing **at any random pace** | Forced vibration, off-resonance | Small amplitude response, fighting the motion | | Parent pushes **at the natural swinging pace** | **Forced vibration at RESONANCE** | Huge, dangerous amplitude buildup | > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** The "natural pace" of the swing is the **natural frequency $\omega_n$**. When the push frequency matches $\omega_n$, every push adds energy in phase with the motion, and the amplitude grows uncontrollably. This is **resonance** — the most catastrophic phenomenon in machine design. --- ## 1.3 The Three Critical Concepts Introduced in This Module ### A. Magnification Factor (MF) The ratio of the dynamic amplitude of vibration to the static deflection produced by the same force. It quantifies **how much the system amplifies the input**. $$MF = \frac{x_{max}}{x_{static}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ Where $r = \omega / \omega_n$ is the **frequency ratio** and $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio**. ### B. Resonance The condition when the **frequency ratio $r = 1$** (i.e., excitation frequency equals natural frequency). At perfect resonance with no damping, the magnification factor becomes **infinite** — physically impossible, leading to structural failure. ### C. Response to Unbalance A special but extremely common form of forced harmonic excitation arising in rotating machinery (turbines, motors) and reciprocating engines (IC engines, compressors) due to the **centrifugal force of unbalanced mass**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The unbalance force $F_0 = m_0 e \omega^2$ is **frequency-dependent** — it increases with the **square** of the operating speed. This is why balancing becomes critical at high RPM. --- ## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Examples 1. **Turbogenerator rotors:** Blade-pass frequency, unbalance, and misalignment are major forced vibration sources. 2. **Automotive suspension:** The road profile excites the sprung mass; the suspension must isolate high-frequency road noise. 3. **IC Engine crankshaft:** Reciprocating mass of piston + connecting rod produces primary and secondary inertial forces. 4. **Building floors:** Footfall and machinery induce forced vibration in office floors. 5. **Helicopter rotors:** Rotating unbalance of blades produces cabin vibration. 6. **Hard disk drives:** Head-positioning actuator under periodic servo forces. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Generalized SDOF Forced Vibration Model Consider a mass $m$ suspended on a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper $c$, subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$. ### Free-Body Diagram Logic (Step-by-Step): - **Inertial force** (opposing acceleration): $F_i = m\ddot{x}$ - **Damping force** (opposing velocity): $F_d = c\dot{x}$ - **Spring force** (opposing displacement): $F_s = kx$ - **External force** (driving): $F(t) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$ Applying Newton's Second Law (or D'Alembert's principle): $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ ### Why the "Steady-State" Solution Matters The complete solution has two parts: 1. **Complementary (transient) part:** $x_c = e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}[A \cos(\omega_d t) + B \sin(\omega_d t)]$ - Decays exponentially due to damping 2. **Particular (steady-state) part:** $x_p = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ - Persists as long as the force is applied - **This is what KTU problems ask for.** > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In the **steady state**, the system oscillates **at the forcing frequency $\omega$**, not at $\omega_n$. Amplitude $X$ and phase lag $\phi$ depend on $\omega$, $\omega_n$, and $\zeta$. --- ## 2.2 Key Derived Quantities ### 2.2.1 Natural Frequency (Undamped) $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$ $$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$ ### 2.2.2 Damping Ratio $$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{2 m \omega_n}$$ Where $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ is the **critical damping coefficient** (the minimum damping to prevent oscillation). ### 2.2.3 Frequency Ratio $$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}$$ ### 2.2.4 Static Deflection $$x_{static} = \frac{F_0}{k}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|----------|---------|-------|------------------| | 1 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Natural pace of oscillation | | 2 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = c / (2m\omega_n)$ | dimensionless | Fraction of critical damping | | 3 | Frequency Ratio | $r = \omega / \omega_n$ | dimensionless | Excitation speed relative to natural | | 4 | Static Deflection | $x_{st} = F_0 / k$ | m | Deflection if force applied slowly | | 5 | Dynamic Amplitude | $X = \dfrac{x_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | m | Actual vibrating amplitude | | 6 | **Magnification Factor (MF)** | $MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless | How much the system amplifies input | | 7 | Phase Angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right)$ | rad | Lag between force and response | | 8 | Resonance Condition | $r = 1 \Rightarrow \omega = \omega_n$ | — | Maximum danger point | | 9 | MF at Resonance | $MF_{r=1} = 1 / (2\zeta)$ | — | Finite only if damping exists | | 10 | Rotating Unbalance Force | $F_0 = m_0 e \omega^2$ | N | Centrifugal force of eccentric mass | | 11 | Response to Rotating Unbalance | $X = \dfrac{m_0 e}{m} \cdot \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | m | Amplitude due to rotor unbalance | | 12 | Transmissibility | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless | Force transmitted to foundation | | 13 | Primary Force (Recip.) | $F_1 = m_1 r \omega^2$ | N | First-order inertial force | | 14 | Secondary Force (Recip.) | $F_2 = m_2 r \omega^2 \cos(2\theta)$ approx. | N | Second-order inertial force | | 15 | Primary Balancing | $m_1 r_1 = m_2 r_2$ (opposite sides) | kg·m | Static balance condition | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Board Rule:** When writing $MF$ formula, **never** confuse the absolute value bars with table syntax. Use $\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$ or $\sqrt{\,}$ explicitly when needed. In the magnification factor, the expression under the square root is always **positive** because both terms are squared. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | Why Forced Vibration Matters | |-------|-------------|------------------------------| | Automotive | Engine mounts, suspension tuning | Isolate cabin from engine firing frequency | | Civil | Bridges, floors, tall buildings | Footfall, wind, seismic isolation | | Aerospace | Helicopter rotors, jet engines | Rotating unbalance, blade-pass vibration | | Manufacturing | CNC machines, spindles | Chatter avoidance at cutting frequencies | | Power | Turbogenerators | Critical speeds, lateral vibrations | | Marine | Propeller shafts | Whirling of shafts at running speed | --- ## 2.5 Physical Insight: What is "Damping" Doing? Without damping ($\zeta = 0$): - $MF = 1 / \vert 1 - r^2 \vert$ - $MF \to \infty$ as $r \to 1$ (perfect resonance → catastrophic) - $MF = 1$ at $r = 0$ and $r = \sqrt{2}$ (interesting crossover) With damping ($\zeta > 0$): - $MF$ is **finite at all frequencies** - $MF_{max}$ occurs slightly **before** $r = 1$ (shifted to $r = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$) - $MF_{max} = 1 / (2\zeta \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2})$ - Higher damping → lower peak but **less isolation** at high $r$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Trade-off:** Damping reduces resonance peak (good), but **increases transmitted force at high frequencies** (bad for vibration isolators). This is why high-precision instruments use *low damping* — they need isolation, not amplitude suppression. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Response (Magnification Factor) ### Goal Solve $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ for the steady-state amplitude $X$ and phase $\phi$. --- ### Step 1: Assume the Form of the Particular Solution Since the input is sinusoidal, the steady-state output must also be sinusoidal at the **same frequency $\omega$**, but **lagging** the force by a phase angle $\phi$: $$x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$ > **Reasoning:** A linear time-invariant system driven by $\sin(\omega t)$ responds with $\sin(\omega t - \phi)$. The lag $\phi$ exists because damping delays energy transfer. --- ### Step 2: Compute Derivatives $$\dot{x}_p(t) = \omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$ $$\ddot{x}_p(t) = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$ --- ### Step 3: Substitute into the Governing Equation $$m[-\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi)] + c[\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)] + k[X \sin(\omega t - \phi)] = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ Grouping $\sin$ and $\cos$ terms: $$X(k - m\omega^2)\sin(\omega t - \phi) + X c \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ --- ### Step 4: Expand $\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ and $\cos(\omega t - \phi)$ Using Angle Subtraction Formulas $$\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \sin(\omega t)\cos(\phi) - \cos(\omega t)\sin(\phi)$$ $$\cos(\omega t - \phi) = \cos(\omega t)\cos(\phi) + \sin(\omega t)\sin(\phi)$$ Substituting back: $$X(k - m\omega^2)[\sin\omega t \cos\phi - \cos\omega t \sin\phi] + Xc\omega[\cos\omega t \cos\phi + \sin\omega t \sin\phi] = F_0 \sin\omega t$$ --- ### Step 5: Collect Coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ **Coefficient of $\sin(\omega t)$:** $$X(k - m\omega^2)\cos\phi + Xc\omega \sin\phi = F_0$$ **Coefficient of $\cos(\omega t)$:** $$-X(k - m\omega^2)\sin\phi + Xc\omega \cos\phi = 0$$ --- ### Step 6: Solve the System of Two Equations From the second equation: $$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}$$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$: $$\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}$$ Where $r = \omega / \omega_n$ and $\zeta = c / (2m\omega_n)$. **Phase angle result:** $$\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right)}$$ From the first equation, squaring and adding both equations: $$X^2[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2] = F_0^2$$ $$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$ --- ### Step 7: Convert to Non-Dimensional Form Divide numerator and denominator by $k$: $$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - m\omega^2/k)^2 + (c\omega/k)^2}}$$ Recognize: - $F_0/k = x_{st}$ (static deflection) - $m\omega^2/k = r^2$ - $c\omega/k = 2\zeta r$ Therefore: $$X = \frac{x_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ --- ### Step 8: Define the Magnification Factor $$MF = \frac{X}{x_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ $$\boxed{MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$ > **Result:** The amplitude of forced vibration is $X = MF \cdot x_{st}$, where $x_{st}$ is the static deflection produced by the same force. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Resonance Condition ### Step 1: Identify the Denominator Resonance occurs when the amplitude $X$ is **maximum**, i.e., the denominator is **minimum**. Denominator: $D(r) = (1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$ ### Step 2: Differentiate with Respect to $r$ and Set to Zero $$\frac{dD}{dr} = 2(1 - r^2)(-2r) + 2(2\zeta r)(2\zeta) = 0$$ $$-4r(1 - r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$ $$4r[-(1 - r^2) + 2\zeta^2] = 0$$ $$4r[-1 + r^2 + 2\zeta^2] = 0$$ ### Step 3: Solve for $r$ For $r \neq 0$: $$r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$$ $$r_{peak} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute Back for Peak Magnification Factor $$MF_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r_{peak}^2)^2 + (2\zeta r_{peak})^2}}$$ Using $1 - r_{peak}^2 = 2\zeta^2$ and $2\zeta r_{peak} = 2\zeta\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$: $$MF_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\zeta^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2(1 - 2\zeta^2)}}$$ $$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2 - 8\zeta^4}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^2 - 4\zeta^4}} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$ $$\boxed{MF_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \approx \frac{1}{2\zeta} \text{ for small } \zeta}$$ > **Special Case — Undamped System ($\zeta = 0$):** Peak occurs at $r = 1$ and $MF_{max} = \infty$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Response to Rotating Unbalance ### Setup A rotor of mass $m$ carries a small unbalanced mass $m_0$ at eccentricity $e$, rotating at angular speed $\omega$. The centrifugal force is: $$F(t) = m_0 e \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$ Thus, $F_0 = m_0 e \omega^2$. ### Substituting into the General Forced Vibration Solution The steady-state amplitude becomes: $$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{m_0 e \omega^2 / k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ Now, $k = m \omega_n^2$, so: $$\frac{m_0 e \omega^2}{m \omega_n^2} = \frac{m_0 e}{m} \cdot r^2$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{X = \frac{m_0 e}{m} \cdot \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$ ### Key Observation At $r = 1$ (resonance), $X = \dfrac{m_0 e}{m} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ — the **same finite value** as a regular forced system at resonance. At $r \gg 1$ (high-speed running), the amplitude approaches the constant $\dfrac{m_0 e}{m}$ — the **"whirl amplitude"** of the rotor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Speed:** The rotor amplitude peaks at $r = 1$, i.e., $\omega = \omega_n$. This speed is called the **critical speed** of the rotor. Machines must pass through critical speed quickly during start-up and shut-down to avoid catastrophic whirling. --- ## 3.4 Reciprocating Unbalance (IC Engine Perspective) For a single-cylinder reciprocating engine, the piston-connecting rod assembly produces inertia forces. Approximating the connecting rod as point mass: **Primary (1st-order) inertia force:** $$F_1 = m_1 r \omega^2 \cos(\theta)$$ **Secondary (2nd-order) inertia force:** $$F_2 = m_2 r \omega^2 \cos(2\theta)$$ Where $m_1$ = reciprocating mass, $m_2$ = effective rotating component, $r$ = crank radius. > **Important KTU Note:** The secondary force has **twice the frequency** of the primary. This means secondary forces resonate at $\omega = \omega_n / \sqrt{2}$, not at $\omega = \omega_n$. Multi-cylinder engines use **firing order** to cancel primary and secondary forces. --- ## 3.5 Transmissibility Derivation The **transmissibility** is the ratio of force transmitted to the foundation to the applied force: $$F_T = kx + c\dot{x} = \text{force on foundation}$$ Substituting $x = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ and $\dot{x} = \omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)$: $$F_T = kX \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$ The amplitude of transmitted force is: $$F_T = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$ Dividing by $F_0$: $$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{F_0}$$ Using $X = F_0 / \sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}$: $$TR = \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$ Dividing by $k$: $$\boxed{TR = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$ > **Isolation Region:** $TR < 1$ only when $r > \sqrt{2}$. For good vibration isolation, the operating frequency must be at least $\sqrt{2}$ times the natural frequency. --- ## 3.6 Special Case Summary Table (KTU-Favorite Problems) | Condition | $r$ | $MF$ | $\phi$ | Physical Meaning | |-----------|-----|------|--------|------------------| | Static (very low $\omega$) | $r \to 0$ | $MF = 1$ | $0$ | Force is "felt" with full static effect | | $r = 1$ (Resonance, $\zeta = 0$) | $1$ | $\infty$ | $90°$ | Pure resonance, no lag damping | | $r = 1$ (Resonance, $\zeta > 0$) | $1$ | $1 / (2\zeta)$ | $90°$ | Finite peak, response lags by 90° | | $r = \sqrt{2}$ | $\sqrt{2}$ | $1$ | $90°$ to $180°$ (crossover) | Dynamic = Static | | $r \to \infty$ | $\infty$ | $0$ | $180°$ | Mass is "shaken" but doesn't follow; isolation | | $MF = 1$ boundary | $r = \sqrt{2}$ | $1$ | — | Transition from amplification to isolation | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Gold:** The point $r = \sqrt{2}$ where $MF = 1$ is **independent of damping**. This is often a question on KTU exams. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — SDOF Forced Vibration System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Harmonic Force
F of t equals F0 sin omega t] --> nodeB[Mass m
Inertia] nodeB --> nodeC[Dashpot c
Viscous Damping] nodeC --> nodeD[Spring k
Stiffness] nodeD --> nodeE[Foundation] nodeF[Response x of t] -.-> nodeB nodeG[Transmitted Force FT] -.-> nodeE style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeC fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeE fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeF fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Forced Vibration Analysis Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Step 1: Identify SDOF system parameters
m, c, k, F0, omega] --> stepB[Step 2: Compute derived quantities
omega_n, zeta, r] stepB --> stepC{Is unbalance the
forcing source?} stepC -- Yes --> stepD[Step 3a: Use F0 equals m0 e omega squared
Apply unbalance formula] stepC -- No --> stepE[Step 3b: Use generic F0 sin omega t
Apply standard formula] stepD --> stepF[Step 4: Compute Magnification Factor MF] stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG[Step 5: Compute Dynamic Amplitude
X equals MF times x_static] stepG --> stepH[Step 6: Check Resonance
r equals 1 triggers danger] stepH --> stepI[Step 7: Compute Phase Angle phi
phi equals arctan of 2 zeta r over 1 minus r squared] stepI --> stepJ[Step 8: Final Response
x of t equals X sin omega t minus phi] style stepA fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepB fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px stepC fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepD fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepE fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepF fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepG fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepH fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepI fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style stepJ fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.3 Resonance Curve — Magnification Factor vs Frequency Ratio ```mermaid flowchart LR regionA[LOW FREQUENCY REGION
r less than 1] --> regionB[RESONANCE PEAK
r equals 1, MF equals 1 over 2 zeta] regionB --> regionC[ISOLATION REGION
r greater than square root of 2, MF less than 1] regionA --> note1[Amplification Zone
MF greater than 1] regionC --> note2[Vibration Isolation Zone
MF less than 1] note3[CROSSOVER POINT
r equals square root of 2, MF equals 1
Independent of damping zeta] -.-> regionA note3 -.-> regionC style regionA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style regionB fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style regionC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style note1 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style note2 fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style note3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Rotating Unbalance Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR rotorM[Unbalanced Mass m0] --> ecc[Eccentricity e] ecc --> omega[Angular Speed omega] omega --> centF[Centrifugal Force
F0 equals m0 e omega squared] centF --> sysF[SDOF System
m, c, k] sysF --> resP[Response Amplitude
X equals m0 e over m times r squared over denominator] resP --> critS[Critical Speed
omega_crit equals omega_n] style rotorM fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ecc fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style omega fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style centF fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style sysF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style resP fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style critS fill:#FF4500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the term "Magnification Factor" in forced harmonic vibration. What is its value at resonance for a damped system?** **Model Answer:** The **Magnification Factor (MF)** is defined as the ratio of the dynamic amplitude of vibration ($X$) to the static deflection ($x_{st}$) produced by the same harmonic force: $$MF = \frac{X}{x_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ At **resonance** ($r = 1$) for a damped system: $$MF_{resonance} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$ For an **undamped** system ($\zeta = 0$), $MF \to \infty$, which represents catastrophic amplitude growth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for definition + formula, 1.5 marks for resonance value derivation. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is meant by resonance in forced vibration? Why must operating speed be kept away from resonance?** **Model Answer:** **Resonance** is the condition in forced vibration where the excitation frequency $\omega$ matches the natural frequency $\omega_n$ of the system, i.e., the **frequency ratio $r = \omega / \omega_n = 1$**. At resonance, the magnification factor becomes maximum ($MF = 1 / 2\zeta$ for damped, $\infty$ for undamped). The system experiences the **largest possible amplitude** for a given forcing amplitude. Resonance must be avoided because: 1. **Large amplitudes** can cause structural failure (fatigue, fracture). 2. **Excessive noise** is generated. 3. **Machinery accuracy** is lost (e.g., tool chatter, instrument errors). 4. **Human discomfort** in vehicles and buildings. Engineering practice: machines are designed so that the operating frequency is **at least 1.4 to 1.5 times** the natural frequency (i.e., $r > \sqrt{2}$) for **isolation**, or far below $r = 1$ for **amplification** (e.g., shakers). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] #### (a) Derive the expression for the magnification factor of a damped single-degree-of-freedom system subjected to harmonic excitation. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Equation of Motion** For a damped SDOF system with mass $m$, damping $c$, and stiffness $k$ subjected to harmonic force $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$: $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 2: Assume Steady-State Solution** The steady-state response will be sinusoidal at the forcing frequency, lagging by phase $\phi$: $$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi) \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 3: Compute Derivatives** $$\dot{x}(t) = \omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi) \quad \text{...(3)}$$ $$\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi) \quad \text{...(4)}$$ **Step 4: Substitute into Equation (1)** $$-m\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi) + kX \sin(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ Rearranging: $$X(k - m\omega^2)\sin(\omega t - \phi) + Xc\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{...(5)}$$ **Step 5: Expand Trigonometric Functions** Using $\sin(A - B)$ and $\cos(A - B)$ identities: $$X(k - m\omega^2)[\sin\omega t \cos\phi - \cos\omega t \sin\phi] + Xc\omega[\cos\omega t \cos\phi + \sin\omega t \sin\phi] = F_0 \sin\omega t$$ **Step 6: Collect Coefficients** *Coefficient of $\sin(\omega t)$:* $$X(k - m\omega^2)\cos\phi + Xc\omega \sin\phi = F_0 \quad \text{...(6)}$$ *Coefficient of $\cos(\omega t)$:* $$-X(k - m\omega^2)\sin\phi + Xc\omega \cos\phi = 0 \quad \text{...(7)}$$ **Step 7: Solve the System** From equation (7): $$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2} \quad \text{...(8)}$$ Squaring and adding (6) and (7): $$X^2[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2] = F_0^2$$ $$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} \quad \text{...(9)}$$ **Step 8: Divide by $k$ and Introduce Damping Ratio and Frequency Ratio** Divide numerator and denominator of (9) by $k$: $$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - m\omega^2/k)^2 + (c\omega/k)^2}}$$ Recognizing $x_{st} = F_0/k$, $r^2 = m\omega^2/k$, and $2\zeta r = c\omega/k$: $$X = \frac{x_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ **Step 9: Magnification Factor** $$MF = \frac{X}{x_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ **QED** --- #### (b) A machine of mass 50 kg is mounted on springs of total stiffness 200 kN/m with a damping ratio of 0.2. The machine operates at 1500 rpm and is subjected to a harmonic force of amplitude 100 N. Find: (i) Natural frequency, (ii) Frequency ratio, (iii) Magnification factor, (iv) Dynamic amplitude, (v) Phase angle. (7 marks) **Given Data:** - $m = 50$ kg - $k = 200 \times 10^3$ N/m = $2 \times 10^5$ N/m - $\zeta = 0.2$ - $N = 1500$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi N / 60 = 2\pi(1500)/60 = 157.08$ rad/s - $F_0 = 100$ N **Solution:** **(i) Natural Frequency:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{50}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{63.25}{2\pi} = 10.07 \text{ Hz}$$ **(ii) Frequency Ratio:** $$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{157.08}{63.25} = 2.483$$ **(iii) Magnification Factor:** $$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ Compute $r^2 = 2.483^2 = 6.165$: $$1 - r^2 = 1 - 6.165 = -5.165$$ $$(1 - r^2)^2 = (-5.165)^2 = 26.68$$ Compute $2\zeta r = 2(0.2)(2.483) = 0.9932$: $$(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.9932^2 = 0.9864$$ $$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{26.68 + 0.9864}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{27.67}} = \frac{1}{5.260} = 0.1901$$ **(iv) Dynamic Amplitude:** $$x_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^5} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$$ $$X = MF \times x_{st} = 0.1901 \times 0.5 = 0.0951 \text{ mm} = 9.51 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$ **(v) Phase Angle:** $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.9932}{-5.165}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.1923)$$ Since $r > 1$, $\phi$ is in the second quadrant: $$\phi = 180° - 10.91° = 169.09° \approx 169.1° \text{ (or 2.951 rad)}$$ **Summary of Results:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $\omega_n$ | 63.25 rad/s | | $r$ | 2.483 | | $MF$ | 0.1901 | | $X$ | 0.0951 mm | | $\phi$ | 169.1° | > **Interpretation:** Since $r > \sqrt{2}$, the system acts as a **vibration isolator**. The dynamic amplitude is **less than** the static deflection. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] #### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the response of a system subjected to rotating unbalance. Derive the expression for the steady-state amplitude. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Concept:** When a rotor carries a small unbalanced mass $m_0$ at a radial eccentricity $e$ from the axis of rotation, the center of mass does not lie on the rotation axis. As the rotor spins at angular speed $\omega$, the unbalanced mass creates a centrifugal force: $$F(t) = m_0 e \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$ This is a **harmonic force** at the running speed $\omega$ and is the most common form of excitation in rotating machinery. **Sketch Description (Verbal — for board exam):** A horizontal shaft supported by two bearings carries a disk. The geometric center of the disk is $O$, while the center of mass is $G$, offset by eccentricity $e$. As the disk rotates, $G$ traces a circle around $O$. The centrifugal force $F_0 = m_0 e \omega^2$ acts radially outward through $G$. **Governing Equation of Motion:** The rotor of mass $m$ supported by stiffness $k$ and damping $c$ experiences: $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = m_0 e \omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$ **Steady-State Response:** Following the same procedure as Part (a) of Question A, the steady-state amplitude is: $$X = \frac{m_0 e \omega^2 / k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ Since $k = m\omega_n^2$: $$\frac{m_0 e \omega^2}{m \omega_n^2} = \frac{m_0 e}{m} \cdot r^2$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{X = \frac{m_0 e}{m} \cdot \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$ **Phase Angle:** Same as before: $$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right)$$ **Critical Observation — Critical Speed:** The amplitude $X$ becomes maximum when $r = 1$ (resonance), corresponding to $\omega = \omega_n$. This speed is called the **critical speed** of the rotor: $$\omega_c = \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ At the critical speed, the rotor undergoes violent whirling and may self-destruct. Turbines and high-speed rotors must be **dynamically balanced** and accelerated through critical speed rapidly. --- #### (b) A rotor of mass 100 kg has an unbalanced mass of 0.5 kg at a radius of 100 mm. The rotor runs at 3000 rpm in bearings that provide a total stiffness of $5 \times 10^6$ N/m. Damping ratio is 0.15. Find: (i) Critical speed, (ii) Steady-state amplitude, (iii) Dynamic force transmitted to the bearings. (7 marks) **Given Data:** - $m = 100$ kg (rotor mass) - $m_0 = 0.5$ kg (unbalanced mass) - $e = 100$ mm = $0.1$ m - $N = 3000$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi(3000)/60 = 314.16$ rad/s - $k = 5 \times 10^6$ N/m - $\zeta = 0.15$ **Solution:** **(i) Critical Speed:** $$\omega_c = \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^6}{100}} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^4} = 223.6 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$N_c = \frac{60 \times \omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 223.6}{2\pi} = 2135 \text{ rpm}$$ **(ii) Steady-State Amplitude:** $$F_0 = m_0 e \omega^2 = 0.5 \times 0.1 \times (314.16)^2 = 0.05 \times 98696 = 4934.8 \text{ N}$$ $$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{314.16}{223.6} = 1.405$$ Compute: - $1 - r^2 = 1 - 1.974 = -0.974$, so $(1 - r^2)^2 = 0.949$ - $2\zeta r = 2(0.15)(1.405) = 0.4215$, so $(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.1777$ $$X = \frac{m_0 e}{m} \cdot \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ $$\frac{m_0 e}{m} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.1}{100} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$ $$X = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1.974}{\sqrt{0.949 + 0.1777}} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1.974}{\sqrt{1.127}}$$ $$X = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1.974}{1.0615} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.859 = 9.30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.930 \text{ mm}$$ **(iii) Dynamic Force Transmitted to Bearings:** $$F_T = X \sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$ First compute $c$: $$c = 2 m \omega_n \zeta = 2 \times 100 \times 223.6 \times 0.15 = 6708 \text{ Ns/m}$$ $$c\omega = 6708 \times 314.16 = 2.107 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$ $$k^2 = (5 \times 10^6)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{13}$$ $$(c\omega)^2 = (2.107 \times 10^6)^2 = 4.440 \times 10^{12}$$ $$\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{13} + 4.44 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{2.944 \times 10^{13}} = 5.426 \times 10^6$$ $$F_T = 9.30 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.426 \times 10^6 = 5046 \text{ N}$$ **Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Critical speed $N_c$ | 2135 rpm | | Steady-state amplitude $X$ | 0.930 mm | | Transmitted force $F_T$ | 5046 N | > **Note:** Since $r = 1.405$ is **between 1 and $\sqrt{2}$**, the magnification is still slightly greater than 1, so the system is in the **amplification region** and dangerous. Operating speed should be increased above $r > \sqrt{2}$ for safety. --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Forgetting the $r^2$ factor in rotating unbalance formula.** Unlike generic forced vibration, the unbalance response has an extra $r^2$ in the numerator. Using $F_0 = m_0 e\omega^2$ alone and ignoring the $r^2$ factor leads to a wrong amplitude. > 2. **Confusing undamped natural frequency $\omega_n$ with damped natural frequency $\omega_d$.** The KTU board often tests whether students know that $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the **reference** for resonance, not $\omega_d$. > 3. **Sign of $\phi$ for $r > 1$:** Phase angle should be in the **second quadrant** (between 90° and 180°), not a negative angle from the calculator. Always report $\phi = 180° - \alpha$. > 4. **Forgetting unit conversions:** $N$ in rpm must be converted to $\omega$ in rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. KTU strictly deducts 1 mark for missing units. > 5. **Transmissibility vs. Magnification Factor confusion:** $TR$ uses the **square root of $1 + (2\zeta r)^2$** in the numerator; $MF$ has **just 1** in the numerator. These are NOT the same formula. > 6. **Static deflection $x_{st} = F_0/k$:** Students often write $F_0 \cdot k$ by mistake. Remember: stiffness is in the denominator. > 7. **Resonance condition:** $r = 1$ only for **undamped** systems. With damping, peak occurs at $r = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$, which is slightly less than 1. --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical points to remember before the KTU exam:** - [x] **Governing equation** of damped forced SDOF vibration: $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ - [x] **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (independent of damping and forcing) - [x] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c / (2m\omega_n)$ — fraction of critical damping - [x] **Frequency ratio:** $r = \omega / \omega_n$ — the **most important parameter** in forced vibration - [x] **Magnification Factor formula:** $MF = 1 / \sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ - [x] **MF at $r = \sqrt{2}$ = 1**, independent of damping — **classic KTU question** - [x] **MF at $r = 1$ = $1/(2\zeta)$** for damped; $\infty$ for undamped - [x] **Phase angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right)$ - [x] **At resonance, $\phi = 90°$** (force leads response by 90°) - [x] **At $r \to 0$, $\phi = 0°$** (in-phase) - [x] **At $r \to \infty$, $\phi = 180°$** (out-of-phase, force in opposite direction) - [x] **Rotating unbalance force:** $F_0 = m_0 e \omega^2$ — proportional to **$\omega^2$** - [x] **Rotating unbalance response:** $X = \dfrac{m_0 e}{m} \cdot \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ - [x] **Critical speed:** $\omega_c = \omega_n$ — speed at which rotor amplitude peaks - [x] **Reciprocating unbalance:** Primary force $F_1 = m_1 r \omega^2 \cos\theta$, Secondary $F_2 \propto \cos(2\theta)$ - [x] **Transmissibility:** $TR = \sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2} / \sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ - [x] **Isolation region:** $TR < 1$ only when $r > \sqrt{2}$ - [x] **Vibration isolation requires:** $f_{operating} > \sqrt{2} \cdot f_{natural}$ - [x] **Maximum MF location (damped):** $r_{peak} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ - [x] **Maximum MF value (damped):** $MF_{max} = 1 / (2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2})$ - [x] **Undamped free vibration decays at:** $e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}$ - [x] **Steady-state response frequency = forcing frequency** (NOT natural frequency) > **Final Word:** In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, forced vibration problems almost always test **two things**: > 1. Recognition of the **MF formula and its variants** (with/without unbalance). > 2. Numerical computation of amplitude, phase, and resonance conditions. > Master the formula sheet, draw clean free-body diagrams, and **always** convert rpm to rad/s.

topics.title: Whirling of Rotating Shafts, Vibration Isolation and Transmissibility, Accelerometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET501 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Whirling of Rotating Shafts, Vibration Isolation and Transmissibility, Accelerometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Whirling of Rotating Shafts

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Whirling of a shaft* is the transverse lateral vibration of a rotating shaft caused by the centrifugal force of an unbalanced mass (rotor) acting on the shaft. The speed at which this lateral vibration becomes self-sustaining and the shaft whirls with large amplitude due to resonance is called the **Critical (or Whirling) Speed** of the shaft.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Imagine a spinning top. If the top's central axis wobbles side-to-side while it spins, that side-to-side motion is "whirling." Now picture a ceiling fan. The blades (which are slightly heavier on one side) act as an unbalanced mass. As the motor speeds up, the shaft carrying the blades starts to bend sideways. At a *specific* speed, this bending becomes dangerously large — the fan body "wobbles violently." This speed is the **critical speed** of the shaft. The shaft's natural bending frequency matches the rotation frequency → *resonance*. Any further operation at this speed is catastrophic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Highlight:** A shaft can have **multiple critical speeds** — first, second, third, etc. — corresponding to its different mode shapes. Designers always ensure the operating speed lies **at least 20–25% away** from any critical speed.

---

### 1.2 Vibration Isolation and Transmissibility

**Formal Definition:**
> *Vibration isolation* is the technique of reducing the transmission of vibrations from a vibrating source (machine) to its surroundings, or vice versa, using flexible supports (isolators). *Transmissibility* is the ratio of the force (or motion) transmitted to the foundation to the force (or motion) generated by the vibrating source.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Picture a washing machine on a rigid concrete floor — it shakes the entire house. Now place the same machine on **rubber pads** — the shaking almost disappears from the floor. The rubber pads "absorb" or "isolate" the vibration. The **transmissibility ratio (TR)** tells you *how much* vibration actually gets through. If TR = 0.1, then only 10% of the vibration reaches the floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vibration isolation is effective only when the forcing frequency $f$ is greater than $\sqrt{2}$ times the natural frequency $f_n$ of the system on its isolator** (i.e., $r = f/f_n > \sqrt{2}$).

---

### 1.3 Accelerometer (Vibrometer)

**Formal Definition:**
> An *Accelerometer* is a seismic instrument that measures the **acceleration** of a vibrating body. It is a vibrometer modified by a damping fluid in such a way that its natural frequency is much lower than the frequency of vibration being measured, so that the relative displacement of the seismic mass is directly proportional to the acceleration of the vibrating base.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Hold a pencil on a vibrating table. If you tape a small mass to the top of the pencil, the pencil's tip will move relative to the base. The mass "lags behind" because of its inertia. By carefully calibrating how much the tip moves and the phase lag, you can find the *acceleration* of the table. The accelerometer in your smartphone (MEMS chip) works on the very same principle — a tiny suspended mass moves relative to a fixed frame, and the movement is measured electrically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** A **Vibrometer** measures *displacement* (low frequency regime). An **Accelerometer** measures *acceleration* (high frequency regime where $r \gg 1$).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Whirling of Rotating Shafts — Theoretical Foundation

**A. Shaft with a Single Rotor (Concentrated Mass, Massless Shaft)**

Consider a shaft of negligible mass carrying a single disc of mass *m* at its centre. The disc is unbalanced by an eccentricity *e*.

* **Centrifugal force of the unbalanced mass:**
$$F_c = m\,e\,\omega^2$$

* **Shaft acts as a spring of stiffness *s***. The shaft deflects by a distance *y* (deflection of shaft from bearing axis).

* **Resonance condition:** The whirl frequency = natural frequency of the shaft-mass system.

* **Critical Angular Velocity:**
$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{s}{m}} \quad \text{rad/s}$$

* **Critical Speed (RPM):**
$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} = 9.55\,\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} \quad \text{RPM}$$

where $\delta = \dfrac{mg}{s}$ is the **static deflection** of the shaft due to the weight of the rotor (in metres), and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

> [!NOTE]
> This equation $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{g/\delta}$ is the **most-frequently asked formula** in KTU Module 4.

**B. Shaft with Multiple Discs — Dunkerley's Method**

When several discs are mounted on a shaft, the lowest (first) critical speed is computed by:
$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \frac{1}{N_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{N_{c2}^2} + \frac{1}{N_{c3}^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{N_{cn}^2}$$

where $N_{ci}$ is the critical speed when **only the *i*-th disc is present** (others removed), given by:
$$N_{ci} = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dunkerley's method is accurate for the **first critical speed** only and assumes discs do not interact dynamically.

**C. Effect of Disc Inertia (Shaft with Disc Mass Included)**

When the shaft is not massless, the critical speed reduces. The corrected equation is:
$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{s}{m + m_s/3}} \quad \text{(for a simply supported uniform shaft)}$$

where $m_s$ is the mass of the shaft.

---

### 2.2 Vibration Isolation and Transmissibility

**A. The Basic Isolated System**

A machine of mass *m* supported by an isolator of stiffness *k* and damping coefficient *c*. The machine is excited by a harmonic force $F_0 \sin\omega t$.

* Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
* Damping ratio: $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$
* Frequency ratio: $r = \omega/\omega_n$

**B. Transmissibility Ratio (TR)**

The force transmitted to the foundation is:
$$F_T = F_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

So the transmissibility is:
$$\boxed{\text{TR} = \dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isolation Condition:** For $\text{TR} < 1$, the operating frequency must exceed $\sqrt{2}\,\omega_n$ (i.e., $r > \sqrt{2}$). The minimum transmissibility point shifts to higher *r* as damping increases.

**C. Motion Isolation (Base Excitation)**

For base excitation $y = Y\sin\omega t$, the transmitted displacement to the mass is governed by:
$$\dfrac{X}{Y} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

---

### 2.3 Accelerometer / Vibrometer — Working Principle

A seismic instrument is essentially a single-DOF spring-mass-damper system where the **base** is the vibrating body and the **seismic mass** records relative motion.

**Two operating regimes (regime determined by the frequency ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$):**

| Regime | Operating Range | Quantity Measured | Instrument Name |
|:------:|:---------------:|:-----------------:|:---------------:|
| Low frequency | $r \ll 1$ | Displacement (X) | **Vibrometer** |
| High frequency | $r \gg 1$ | Acceleration ($\omega^2 Y$) | **Accelerometer** |

For a base-excited instrument with recorded relative displacement *x* (between mass and base):

$$x = \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;Y$$

* When $r \gg 1$: $x \approx Y$, and the **inertial force** $m\omega^2 Y$ on the mass is proportional to acceleration → *Accelerometer mode*.
* When $r \ll 1$: $x \approx r^2 Y = (\omega^2/\omega_n^2)Y$, so the recorded *x* is proportional to the displacement *Y* of the base → *Vibrometer mode*.

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:--:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | Critical speed (single disc) | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}} = 9.55\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}$ | $\delta$ in m, $N_c$ in RPM |
| 2 | Static deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{mg}{s}$ | $s$ = shaft stiffness in N/m |
| 3 | Dunkerley's equation | $\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \sum_{i}\dfrac{1}{N_{ci}^2}$ | First critical speed only |
| 4 | Frequency ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Transmissibility (Force) | $\text{TR} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1+(2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Effective when $r > \sqrt{2}$ |
| 7 | Isolation condition | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | $\text{TR} < 1$ region |
| 8 | Vibrometer magnification | $\dfrac{x}{Y} = r^2$ (when $r \ll 1$) | $x \propto$ base displacement |
| 9 | Accelerometer magnification | $\dfrac{x}{Y} \approx 1$ (when $r \gg 1$) | $x \propto$ base displacement; $m\omega^2 Y$ ∝ acceleration |
| 10 | Natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz |

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Whirling speed analysis** is the *first* step in the design of any rotating machine — turbines, turbo-generators, propeller shafts, centrifuges, and CNC spindles. Catastrophic failures in the 1940s–50s (e.g., power-plant turbines) drove the entire field of rotor dynamics.
* **Vibration isolation** is critical in HVAC systems, automotive engine mounts, building base-isolators in earthquake engineering, and precision-tool mounts in semiconductor fabs.
* **Accelerometers** are embedded in smartphones, airbag systems, aircraft black boxes, structural-health monitoring of bridges, and biomedical gait analysis.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Speed of a Shaft Carrying a Single Rotor

**Given:** A shaft of stiffness *s* (N/m) carrying a disc of mass *m* (kg) at its centre, with the disc centre at a distance *e* from the geometric axis (eccentricity).

**Step 1 — Free-body diagram of the disc:**
Let $y$ = deflection of the shaft from the bearing axis.
The distance of the disc's centre of gravity from the bearing axis is $(y + e)$.

**Step 2 — Centrifugal force (acts outward on the disc):**
$$F_c = m\,(y+e)\,\omega^2$$

This force tends to increase the deflection, while the shaft's elastic force $s\,y$ opposes it.

**Step 3 — Equation of motion (steady-state, no damping, equilibrium of forces):**
$$m\,(y+e)\,\omega^2 - s\,y = 0$$
$$m\,y\,\omega^2 + m\,e\,\omega^2 = s\,y$$
$$y\,(s - m\,\omega^2) = m\,e\,\omega^2$$
$$y = \dfrac{m\,e\,\omega^2}{s - m\,\omega^2}$$

**Step 4 — Physical insight:**
As $\omega \to \sqrt{s/m}$, the denominator → 0, and $y \to \infty$. This is the **critical (whirling) speed**.

**Step 5 — Critical angular velocity:**
$$\boxed{\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{s}{m}} \quad \text{rad/s}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to RPM:**
$$N_c = \dfrac{60\,\omega_c}{2\pi} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{s}{m}} \quad \text{RPM}$$

**Step 7 — Introduce static deflection $\delta = mg/s$, so $s/m = g/\delta$:**
$$N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}} = 9.55\,\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}} \quad \text{RPM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most tested derivation** in KTU Module 4. Memorise it.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Dunkerley's Equation for Multiple Discs

**Given:** A shaft carrying *n* discs at various locations, with stiffness $s_i$ as seen by each disc.

**Step 1:** Let $\delta$ be the static deflection at a reference point due to the weight of *all* discs. By the principle of superposition:
$$\delta = \delta_1 + \delta_2 + \delta_3 + \cdots + \delta_n$$

**Step 2:** Each $\delta_i = m_i g / s_i$. Squaring and inverting:
$$\dfrac{1}{N_{c}^2} = \dfrac{1}{N_{c1}^2} + \dfrac{1}{N_{c2}^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{N_{cn}^2}$$

where $N_{ci} = 9.55\sqrt{g/\delta_i}$ is the critical speed when **only the *i*-th disc is present** (with all other discs removed).

**Step 3 — Why is it conservative?**
Dunkerley's method neglects the *interaction* between discs during whirling. The first critical speed is therefore **lower than the actual** → design is safe.

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{N_{ci}^2}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Transmissibility Ratio (Damped System)

**Given:** A mass *m* on spring *k* and damper *c*, with harmonic excitation $F_0 \sin\omega t$.

**Step 1 — Equation of motion:**
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin\omega t$$

**Step 2 — Assume steady-state solution:** $x = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.
Substituting and equating coefficients (or using complex phasors):
$$X = \dfrac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Force transmitted to the foundation:**
The force on the foundation is the sum of spring force and damper force:
$$F_T = \sqrt{(kX)^2 + (c\omega X)^2} = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Transmissibility Ratio:**
$$\dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \dfrac{X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{F_0} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by *k*, with $\omega_n^2 = k/m$, $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$, and $r = \omega/\omega_n$:**

$$\dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Special cases:**

* Undamped ($\zeta = 0$):
$$\text{TR} = \dfrac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$$

* At $r = \sqrt{2}$, with $\zeta = 0$: $\text{TR} = 1$ (boundary between isolation and amplification).
* At $r = \sqrt{2}$, with $\zeta \ne 0$: $\text{TR} > 1$ (damping *increases* TR in isolation region but *decreases* it near resonance).

**Step 7 — Condition for effective isolation:**
$$\text{TR} < 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad r > \sqrt{2}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Accelerometer (Base Excitation)

**Given:** Base moves as $y = Y\sin\omega t$. Seismic mass *m* connected to base by spring *k* and damper *c*. Let $x$ = relative displacement of mass w.r.t. base (i.e., $x = x_m - y$).

**Step 1 — Equation of motion (in inertial frame):**
$$m\ddot{x}_m + c(\dot{x}_m - \dot{y}) + k(x_m - y) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $x = x_m - y$:**
$$m(\ddot{x} + \ddot{y}) + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y} = m\omega^2 Y\sin\omega t$$

**Step 3 — Forced vibration solution for *x*:**
$$x = \dfrac{(m\omega^2 / k)}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} \cdot \dfrac{Y}{1} = \dfrac{r^2 Y}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{x}{Y} = \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Accelerometer regime ($r \gg 1$):**
Denominator $\to \infty$ in the term $(1 - r^2)^2$, so:
$$\dfrac{x}{Y} \approx \dfrac{r^2}{r^2} = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x \approx Y$$

The relative displacement equals the base displacement. The **inertial force** on the seismic mass is:
$$F_{inertia} = m\ddot{y}_{\max} = m\,\omega^2 Y$$

which is directly proportional to the **acceleration** of the vibrating base.

**Step 5 — Vibrometer regime ($r \ll 1$):**
$$\dfrac{x}{Y} \approx r^2 = \dfrac{\omega^2}{\omega_n^2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad x \propto Y$$

The relative displacement is proportional to base displacement, so the instrument reads the *displacement* of the base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The transition frequency is around $r = 1$ to $r = 2$. The "Vibrometer" works for *low frequencies*; the "Accelerometer" works for *high frequencies*.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Quantity | Vibrometer | Accelerometer |
|:--------:|:----------:|:-------------:|
| Operating regime | $r \ll 1$ (low freq.) | $r \gg 1$ (high freq.) |
| Mass displacement $x_m$ | $x_m \approx x$ (small) | $x_m \approx 0$ (mass is stationary) |
| Relative displacement $x/Y$ | $r^2$ (small) | $1$ (≈ 1) |
| Measured quantity | Displacement $Y$ | Acceleration $\omega^2 Y$ |
| Typical $\omega_n$ (Hz) | Low (< 10 Hz) | High (> 100 Hz) |
| Application | Slow machinery vibration, buildings | High-freq. machinery, shock testing, automotive |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Whirling of a Shaft — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ROTATING SHAFT WITH UNBALANCED MASS m"] --> B["SHAFT DEFLECTION y"]
    B --> C["CENTRIFUGAL FORCE m times e plus y times omega squared"]
    C --> D["INCREASED DEFLECTION FEEDBACK"]
    D --> E["STIFFNESS FORCE s times y OPPOSES"]
    E --> F{"EQUILIBRIUM CONDITION"}
    F -- omega equal to sqrt s by m --> G["CRITICAL / WHIRLING SPEED"]
    F -- omega less than omega_c --> H["STABLE WHIRL WITH FINITE AMPLITUDE"]
    F -- omega greater than omega_c --> I["SHAFT SELF CENTRES BUT RESIDUAL VIBRATION"]
    G --> J["RESONANCE CATASTROPHE"]
    J --> K["AMPLITUDE TENDS TO INFINITY"]
```

### 4.2 Vibration Isolation — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["VIBRATING MACHINE mass m"] --> B["EXCITING FORCE F0 sin omega t"]
    B --> C["SPRING k"]
    B --> D["DAMPER c"]
    C --> E["FOUNDATION"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["TRANSMITTED FORCE FT"]
    F --> G{"COMPUTE TR"}
    G --> H["TR less than 1 ISOLATION ACHIEVED"]
    G --> I["TR greater than 1 VIBRATION AMPLIFIED"]
    H --> J["OPERATING r greater than sqrt 2"]
    I --> K["OPERATING r less than sqrt 2"]
```

### 4.3 Accelerometer — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["VIBRATING BASE y equals Y sin omega t"] --> B["SEISMIC MASS m ON SPRING k DAMPER c"]
    B --> C["RELATIVE DISPLACEMENT x RECORDED BY SENSOR"]
    C --> D{"FREQUENCY RATIO r"}
    D -- r much less than 1 --> E["VIBROMETER MODE - READS DISPLACEMENT Y"]
    D -- r much greater than 1 --> F["ACCELEROMETER MODE - INERTIAL FORCE m omega squared Y"]
    F --> G["OUTPUT PROPORTIONAL TO ACCELERATION OF BASE"]
    E --> H["OUTPUT PROPORTIONAL TO DISPLACEMENT OF BASE"]
```

### 4.4 Transmissibility vs. Frequency Ratio — Qualitative Plot (Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FREQUENCY RATIO r ON X AXIS"] --> B["TR ON Y AXIS"]
    B --> C["REGION r less than sqrt 2 - TR greater than 1 - AMPLIFICATION"]
    B --> D["POINT r equals sqrt 2 - TR equals 1 - BOUNDARY"]
    B --> E["REGION r greater than sqrt 2 - TR less than 1 - ISOLATION"]
    C --> F["NEAR r equals 1 - DAMPING REDUCES PEAK"]
    E --> G["HIGHER r - TR APPROACHES ZERO - PERFECT ISOLATION"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Whirling" of a shaft. Mention the condition for the critical speed of a shaft with a single disc.**

**Model Answer:**
Whirling of a shaft is the transverse vibration of a rotating shaft due to the centrifugal force generated by an unbalanced mass. The condition for the critical (whirling) speed of a shaft carrying a single disc is when the angular velocity of rotation $\omega$ equals the natural frequency of the shaft-mass system:
$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{s}{m}}$$
where *s* is the shaft stiffness and *m* is the mass of the disc.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State Dunkerley's method for finding the critical speed of a shaft with multiple discs.**

**Model Answer:**
Dunkerley's method is used to find the **first critical speed** of a shaft carrying multiple discs:
$$\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \dfrac{1}{N_{c1}^2} + \dfrac{1}{N_{c2}^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{N_{cn}^2}$$
where $N_{ci}$ is the critical speed if only the *i*-th disc is present (with all others removed), given by $N_{ci} = 9.55\sqrt{g/\delta_i}$ where $\delta_i$ is the static deflection at the *i*-th disc location due to its own weight.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

> *A shaft of stiffness 25 kN/m carries a single disc of mass 5 kg at its centre. The disc is unbalanced with an eccentricity of 0.5 mm. Determine:*
> *(a) [7 marks] The critical speed in RPM and the whirling amplitude at a speed of 2000 RPM.*
> *(b) [7 marks] If a second identical disc (5 kg, e = 0.5 mm) is added at a location where the shaft stiffness contribution gives a static deflection of 0.6 mm for the second disc alone, find the new first critical speed using Dunkerley's method.*

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Critical speed and whirling amplitude at 2000 RPM**

* Given: $s = 25{,}000$ N/m, $m = 5$ kg, $e = 0.5$ mm = $0.0005$ m.
* Static deflection: $\delta = mg/s = (5 \times 9.81)/25{,}000 = 1.962 \times 10^{-3}$ m.
* Critical speed:
$$N_c = 9.55\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}} = 9.55\sqrt{\dfrac{9.81}{0.001962}} = 9.55 \times \sqrt{4999.5}$$
$$N_c = 9.55 \times 70.71 \approx 675.3 \text{ RPM}$$

* Whirling amplitude *y* at $N = 2000$ RPM:
$\omega = 2\pi N/60 = 2\pi \times 2000/60 = 209.44$ rad/s.
$\omega_c = \sqrt{s/m} = \sqrt{25000/5} = \sqrt{5000} = 70.71$ rad/s.
$$y = \dfrac{e\,(\omega/\omega_c)^2}{1 - (\omega/\omega_c)^2} = \dfrac{0.0005 \times (209.44/70.71)^2}{1 - (209.44/70.71)^2}$$
$$\left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_c}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{209.44}{70.71}\right)^2 = 8.766$$
$$y = \dfrac{0.0005 \times 8.766}{1 - 8.766} = \dfrac{0.004383}{-7.766} = -5.64 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

Magnitude of whirling amplitude ≈ **0.564 mm** (negative sign indicates the centre of gravity lies on the opposite side of the geometric axis — self-centring).

**(b) Dunkerley's method**

* $N_{c1} = 9.55\sqrt{9.81/0.001962} = 675.3$ RPM (from part (a)).
* $N_{c2} = 9.55\sqrt{9.81/0.0006} = 9.55 \times \sqrt{16350} = 9.55 \times 127.87 = 1221.2$ RPM.
* Dunkerley:
$$\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = \dfrac{1}{675.3^2} + \dfrac{1}{1221.2^2}$$
$$\dfrac{1}{N_c^2} = 2.193 \times 10^{-6} + 6.706 \times 10^{-7} = 2.864 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$N_c^2 = 3.49 \times 10^{5} \quad\Rightarrow\quad N_c = 590.8 \text{ RPM}$$

The new first critical speed ≈ **591 RPM** (lower than 675.3 RPM, as expected with the added disc).

---

**Question B (14 Marks):**

> *A machine of mass 50 kg is supported by an isolator with stiffness 50 kN/m and damping coefficient 400 N·s/m. The machine is excited by a harmonic force of 1000 N at a frequency of 25 Hz. Determine:*
> *(a) [7 marks] The transmissibility ratio, the transmitted force, and the natural frequency. Is the system in the isolation region?*
> *(b) [7 marks] If the same setup is used as an accelerometer to measure the vibration of a base vibrating at 250 Hz with amplitude 1 mm, find the relative displacement of the seismic mass and the acceleration recorded by the instrument.*

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Transmissibility and transmitted force**

* Natural frequency: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{50{,}000/50} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1000}$
$$f_n = 31.83/2\pi = 5.03 \text{ Hz}$$
* $\omega_n = 2\pi \times 5.03 = 31.62$ rad/s.
* $\omega = 2\pi \times 25 = 157.08$ rad/s.
* $r = \omega/\omega_n = 157.08/31.62 = 4.97$.
* Damping ratio: $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km}) = 400/(2\sqrt{50{,}000 \times 50}) = 400/(2 \times 1581.14) = 0.1265$.
* $2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.1265 \times 4.97 = 1.257$.
* $(1 - r^2)^2 = (1 - 24.7)^2 = (-23.7)^2 = 561.7$.
* Transmissibility:
$$\text{TR} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + 1.257^2}{561.7 + 1.257^2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + 1.580}{561.7 + 1.580}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.580}{563.28}}$$
$$\text{TR} = \sqrt{0.00458} = 0.0677$$

* Transmitted force: $F_T = \text{TR} \times F_0 = 0.0677 \times 1000 = 67.7$ N.
* Since $r = 4.97 > \sqrt{2} = 1.414$, the system is in the **isolation region**. ✓

**(b) Accelerometer calculation**

* Base frequency $f = 250$ Hz, $\omega = 2\pi \times 250 = 1570.8$ rad/s.
* $r = 1570.8/31.62 = 49.68$ → $r \gg 1$ → accelerometer regime.
* $\zeta$ same = 0.1265.
* $2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.1265 \times 49.68 = 12.57$.
* $(1 - r^2)^2 \approx (r^2)^2 = (49.68)^4 = 6.10 \times 10^{6}$.
* Denominator: $\sqrt{6.10 \times 10^{6} + 158.0} \approx 2469.6$.
* Relative displacement:
$$\dfrac{x}{Y} = \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \dfrac{(49.68)^2}{2469.6} = \dfrac{2468.1}{2469.6} \approx 0.9994$$
$$x \approx 0.9994 \times 1\text{ mm} \approx 0.999 \text{ mm}$$

* Acceleration of base: $a = \omega^2 Y = (1570.8)^2 \times 0.001 = 2.467 \times 10^6 \times 0.001 = 2467$ m/s².
* Recorded acceleration: $a_{recorded} = \omega^2 Y \approx$ **2467 m/s²** ≈ 251.6 g.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Dunkerley's method** is for the **first** critical speed only — students often apply it for higher modes. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 2. **Transmissibility** must be calculated using the *damped* formula, not the undamped one. If damping is given, ignoring it loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Isolation region check:** You must explicitly state whether $r > \sqrt{2}$; failure to do so is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Accelerometer vs. Vibrometer:** The sign of the question (high $r$ vs. low $r$) is the deciding factor — students often confuse the two and write the wrong magnification formula. (Lose 2–3 marks.)
> 5. **Unit errors:** $N_c$ must be in **RPM**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**. Mixing them up is a frequent mistake.
> 6. **Dunkerley is conservative:** Always report $N_c$ from Dunkerley is *less than* the true first critical speed. Forget this, lose 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* ✅ Whirling = lateral vibration of a rotating shaft due to unbalance.
* ✅ Critical speed: $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{g/\delta}$ (RPM) — single-disc formula.
* ✅ Dunkerley: $1/N_c^2 = \sum 1/N_{ci}^2$ — for first critical speed with multiple discs.
* ✅ Operating speed must be at least **20–25% away** from any critical speed.
* ✅ Transmissibility: $\text{TR} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1+(2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$.
* ✅ Isolation region: $r > \sqrt{2}$ (i.e., $\omega > 1.414\,\omega_n$).
* ✅ Higher damping → better at resonance, worse in isolation region.
* ✅ **Vibrometer** = low frequency ($r \ll 1$) → reads displacement.
* ✅ **Accelerometer** = high frequency ($r \gg 1$) → inertial force ∝ acceleration.
* ✅ Accel. formula: $a = \omega^2 Y$, displacement formula: $Y = A$ (peak amplitude).
* ✅ Acceleration of base = $\omega^2 \times$ base displacement amplitude.
* ✅ Always check units: $\delta$ in m, $g$ in m/s², $N_c$ in RPM.
* ✅ In the accelerometer, the recorded relative displacement $x \approx Y$ (base amplitude) when $r \gg 1$.

---
